 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                                  11 August 2025                                              Page | 1
P1: Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP)




1.                Scope
The purpose of this Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP) is to set out the Project Proponent’s approach to manage and
mitigate the impacts of air pollutant emissions potentially generated by the Contractor/(s), as identified in the Project’s
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA).
This AQMP has been prepared based on relevant regulations, guidelines and standards at the time of preparation of the
ESIA Study, dated March 2025. Any alteration of the Project activities, revision or new regulations, guidelines or
applicable standards may require revision of this AQMP and subsequent review & approval.
This AQMP (1st iteration) shall be updated by the appointed Contractor into an AQMP (2nd iteration), prior to the
commencement of works.
This AQMP is applicable to the following impact areas:
     •     The construction site for the dam including material stockpile area, batching plant, HPP and off-site facilities
           including the quarries and crushing plant as well as borrow pits.
The Workshop areas and Workers’ camp/s that will generate emissions during construction and also become sensitive
receptors for air pollution due to the presence of workers.
     •     Approach Road (From Highway to Kambarata 1 project site) including tunnels used by vehicles for
           transporting raw materials and equipment to the project site.
     •     Resettlement Area
The plan is also applicable within Areas of Impact (AOI) where the emissions from the construction activities are likely
to get dispersed, limited to 2km downwind from the Construction site and within 2km from road centre line.



2.                Objectives
The objectives of the AQMP are to:
     •     Ensure that the mitigation measures are appropriately selected, designed, and implemented so that the air
           quality standards are met throughout the project’s lifecycle.
     •     Adhere to the applicable local, national, and international standards, including those established by the Kyrgyz
           Republic and multilateral funding agencies.
     •     Provide periodic monitoring requirements to assess air quality parameters to detect deviations and implement
           corrective measures as needed.
     •     Ensure proper occupational health for the working personnel as well as other nearby sensitive receptors by
           ensuring appropriate implementation of mitigation measures.
     •     Ensure transparent communication with affected communities with respect to air quality management.



3.                Roles and Responsibilities
Table 1 provides an outline of key roles and responsibilities in the context of AQMP. This is supported by an
Organisational Chart representing the hierarchy of key roles and responsibilities in Figure 1.

 Entity               Role                           Key Responsibilities
                                                     •   Responsible for updating AQMP
                                                     •   Submission of any required compliance reporting to MoE
                                                     •   Provision of reporting to the PM regarding the level of compliance of the
                                                         contractor, and progress regarding AQMP implementation
                                                     •   Liaison with the Contractor’s EHS manager (CEHSM)
                                                     •   Ensure that all personnel including subcontractors and vendors are adequately

5.                Context
The proposal for the Kambarata – 1 HPP is an 1,880 MW facility designed to produce approximately 5,640 GWh of
electricity each year. The proposed Project consists of the following main elements:
     •     Roller-compacted concrete (RCC) gravity dam is 10 m wide at the crest and has a maximum height above the
           riverbed of 260.8 m.
     •     A reservoir area of 61.7 km2 at a full supply level (FSL) of 1,198 msl. The inundated area extends approximately
           68 km upstream from the Dam wall.
     •     An overground powerhouse including Francis-type turbines with a total electricity generating capacity of 1,880
           MW.
     •     Quarry sites to source raw materials for the Dam.
     •     Temporary concrete batching plant, materials storage, waste disposal, construction sites and workforce
           accommodation facilities.
     •     Permanent and temporary accommodation facilities for Dam and hydropower operations and maintenance
           personnel.
     •     Associated facilities (i.e. a 16 km access road to the K-1 site from the Highway; a bridge over the Naryn River;
           110 kv overhead transmission line and a 110/6 kv substation).
The project is envisaged to incur adverse impacts on air environment during various phases of its lifecycle, due to the
following activities presented below.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1              Client Reference No. 5018007

Table 3: Potential Air Quality Impact Activities by Project Phase

   Project Phases                                       Project activities likely to cause impacts on air quality
   Pre- Construction Phase                              •    Site Preparation for construction of HPP & Associated Facilities
   Construction Phase                                   •    Developing and Strengthening of Access Road and tunnelling
                                                        •    Blasting and quarrying for construction aggregates and crushing operation
                                                        •    Construction and operation of Workers ‘Camp
                                                        •    Setting up of construction yard and material laydown area for the Project
                                                        •    Construction of Vehicular Traffic Tunnels
                                                        •    Transportation of Construction materials, equipment and Manpower
                                                        •    Operation of Batching Plant and Conveyor Belt
                                                        •    Construction activities of Kambarata-1 HPP (RCC dam, Penstock, Spillways,
                                                             etc)

         •   Apart from the impacts listed above, construction activities in Resettlement areas are also expected to cause
             emission to air in form of particulates and gases like SO2 & NOx. All these activities would cause impacts on air
             quality, which is discussed in the following sections.
Apart from the impacts caused during Pre-construction and construction phases, potential impacts are also envisaged
during the operation and maintenance (O&M) phases due to the following activities:
         •   Gaseous Emission to air due to operation of emergency DG sets.
         •   Air emissions due to Maintenance & refurbishment activities of the HPP and associated facilities, like repair of
             access road, bridges etc.
         •   However, air emissions due to the above-mentioned activities is envisaged to be intermittent in nature and for
             short durations only.
This AQMP is linked to several other management plans & sub-plans as presented below:
    i.       Occupational Health & Safety Plan: Details the measures required to maintain a safe, healthy and secure work
             environment for all employees working under the project and provide safety guidelines & procedures for
             employees and to clearly define roles and responsibilities for its implementation.
   ii.       Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan: Deals with the management of community health
             and disease vectors, including management measures which ensure the health and safety of the community
             (e.g. health and disease awareness).
  iii.       Grievance Redress Mechanism: Provides the plan for responding to grievances relating to air quality and dust
             emission from the project activities.
  iv.        Project Commissioning and Construction Closeout Management Plan including Site restoration Plan: Presents
             the plan for managing adverse impacts on the environment, workforce and community during construction
             close-out and project decommissioning, along with rehabilitation of the cleared site.
   v.        Traffic and Road Safety Management Plan: Delineates the mitigation measures for traffic & road safety
             management, including emissions from the traffic.
  vi.        Material and Waste Management Plan, Material Supply Management Plan: Describes the mitigation measures
             to reduce air pollution from material handling and storage.
 vii.        Muck and Spoil Management Plan: Provides mitigation measures to control fugitive dust from muck and spoil
             storage area
 viii.       Blasting and Explosives Management Plan: Outlines procedures and safety protocols for the controlled use of
             explosives during construction, quarrying operations, including the storage, transportation, handling, and
             detonation of explosives, while minimizing risks to personnel and the environment.

     x.    Biodiversity Management Plan: Describes plans to conserve, restore, and enhance biodiversity, including
           impacts due to air pollutants on fauna and flora and the mitigation measures.



6.                Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
As stated in Section 5, project activities in Pre-construction and construction phases are likely to generate air pollutants,
including both particulate matter & gaseous emissions (NOx, SOx, CO etc), which would lead to an increase in
concentration of these pollutants in the ambient air & subsequent potential for detrimental impact to the environment
& human health. The succeeding sections present the baseline air quality and the key impacts.


6.1               Baseline Air Quality
Initial ambient air quality monitoring was carried out in September 2013 during the preparation of the 2014 ESIA study.
This monitoring involved a comprehensive analysis of air samples to measure concentrations of several critical
pollutants, including sulphur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and particulate matter (PM).
The data indicated that concentrations of sulphur dioxide, nitrogen dioxide, carbon monoxide and particulate matter
across different sampling points were below the National stipulated standards.
For the present study, air quality monitoring was undertaken during October 2024 (Autumn season) and April 2025
(Spring Season) to establish the existing quality of ambient air, so that the impacts from the proposed project activities
on ai quality during the construction & operational phases of the Project can be assessed. The sampling was carried out
by the “Sanitary and Hygienic laboratory of the Interdistrict Kadamjai Center for Disease Prevention and State Sanitary
and Epidemiological Surveillance” under the supervision of National Experts. Factors influencing the collection of
ambient air quality samples were the presence of settlements, traffic situations, and the presence of pollution sources
like mining, quarrying, industries, etc. The Table 4 presents the details of air quality monitoring locations:

The analysis of suspended particle (dust) concentration showed values varying from 0.12±0.03 mg/m³ to 0.24±0.06
mg/m³, which is significantly lower than the established MPC (0.5 mg/m³). The concentration of dust during Spring
monitoring was found to vary between 0.08±0.023 mg/m³ and 0.14±0.04 mg/m³. Minimum dust concentrations were
recorded at sampling point A-KA-N44 between K1 & K2 sites (0.12±0.03 mg/m³), while the maximum was recorded at
sampling point A-KA-N7 adjacent to K2 site (0.24±0.06 mg/m³) during Autumn. During Spring, lower concentrations were
recorded at most the monitoring locations, except at A-KA-N6 (near Highway at Uch-Terek), and A-KA-N12 (On the Access
Road to K1 HPP site near the Highway interception) where concentration greater than 0.14±0.04 mg/m³ were found.
Analysis of the spatial distribution of suspended particle concentrations reveals that higher concentrations were reported
at locations associated with transport infrastructure (Highway, Access Road), which may indicate the contribution of
motor vehicles and road dust to the ambient concentration of dust. Higher concentrations are also noted in areas with
anthropogenic activity, such as construction work or populated areas.In areas away from roads and populated areas, the




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1             Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                               SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                            11 August 2025                                     Page | 11
P1: Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP)




concentration of suspended particles is lower, which further ascertains that transportation and construction activities
are major anthropogenic sources of dust in the ambient air.
Concentrations of sulfur dioxide (between <0.08 g/m³ and <0.2 mg/m³) and Nitrogen Dioxide (between <0.02 and
0.023±0.006 mg/m³ ) are below the detection limits. Carbon monoxide concentration were found to be below the MPC
during Autumn; however during Spring analysis of carbon monoxide could not be carried out due to absence of functional
equipment. The low values indicate the absence of significant industrial emissions sources and low traffic intensity within
the study area.


6.2               Key Risks & Impacts
The key risks & impacts on air quality identified during Pre-construction & construction phases of the project are
presented below:
     •     Pre-construction activities including site clearance, grubbing, vegetation removal, grading will cause emission of
           windblown dust, as well as gaseous emissions from equipment used for these activities.
     •     Construction activities involve large-scale earthmoving, blasting, movement of vehicles, etc which can generate
           substantial particulate matter, impacting localised ambient air quality due to rise in concentration of PM in air.
     •     Use of equipment operated by hydrocarbon-based fuels such as diesel and gasoline, which will generate
           emissions of sulphur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), carbon monoxide (CO) and carbon dioxide (CO 2),
           leading to an increase in concentrations of these pollutants in the ambient air.
     •     Increased concentrations of PM and pollutant gases which has the potential for impact to the health of working
           personnel, nearby communities and surrounding flora & fauna. Some of the adverse impacts include respiratory
           & cardiovascular issues and soil contamination due to deposition of generated air pollutants. Deposition of
           pollutants (primarily particulates) on water bodies, like river and springs may lead to its contamination and
           subsequent adverse impacts on its dependent ecosystems.
It may be noted that minimal risks on air quality are envisaged during the operational phase of the project, due to
emission of occasional particulates & gases due to vehicle movement & maintenance activities.



7.                Mitigation and Monitoring
The mitigation measures suggested to alleviate the identified impacts on air quality is summarised below and discussed
in Table 6.


7.1               Mitigation Measures
The key mitigation measures are presented below:
     •     Water spray on access roads, construction zones and stockpiles at a frequency of at least twice to three times
           daily, especially on dry & windy days.
     •     Covering of stockpiled materials or storage in an enclosure, and covered transportation of materials using
           impervious materials such as tarpaulin.
     •     Speed limits will be adjusted and enforced according to prevailing road conditions and the surrounding
           environment
     •     Batching plant, crushers, and equipment causing emissions to air would be sited upwind and as far as possible
           (minimum 500 m) from workers’ accommodation and local community.
     •     Use of Dust Control Systems for point and area sources, including the batching plant and crushers, to control
           dust at the source and prevent its propagation.
     •     Provide progressive stabilization and restoration of disturbed areas (e.g., stabilize a construction area before
           disturbing a new area), possibly with native fast-growing vegetation or hydroseeding/ hydromulching, to
           establish a vegetative cover and reduce the potential for dust emissions.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                     Page | 12
P1: Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP)




     •     Periodic maintenance for all construction equipment to ensure its smooth operation.
     •     Where appropriate, use of wind barriers/ fences to effectively reduce dispersion of generated particulate matter
           from working areas to the nearby receptors.

Table 6: Summary of Mitigation and Monitoring Actions

P1: Air Quality Management Plan (AQMP)




8.                Stakeholders Engagement
On a regular basis the EPC Contractor during construction and EPP during O&M should engage with all stakeholders,
including nearby community stakeholders in upstream and downstream areas, regarding any concerns faced related to
air quality from project activities. All feedback from various stakeholders should be reviewed and addressed suitably,
with the AQMP updated if required to monitor the relevant aspects for proper compliance.



9.                Community Complaints and Grievance
                  Redressal Mechanism
Any complaints received will be recorded, investigated and corrective actions implemented, and feedback given to the
complainant as per the Grievance Redressal Mechanism (part of Stakeholder Engagement Plan) designed for the project.



10.               Training and Capacity Development Programs
Training should be provided on the potential risks & impacts caused by air pollutants and subsequent mitigation measures
required by the AQMP. Training will be imparted to all working personnel, and it will be ensured that Contractors also
provide similar training to their own personnel during inductions. Periodically and as required, refresher trainings will
also be provided. The training will cover relevant aspects of the AQMP, compliance with legislative requirements and
international standards, identification of potential issues and general awareness for emission reduction and reporting
requirements.



11.               Implementation Budget
For implementation of AQMP, the estimated budget would include material cost & Monitoring Cost. A tabular
presentation of an estimated budget is provided in the table below:

 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                                 11 August 2025                                             Page | 22
P2: Noise and Vibration Management Plan (NVMP)




1. Scope
This Noise and vibration management plan (NVMP) is to set out the Project Proponent’s approach to manage and
mitigate the impacts of noise generated during construction, construction, and O&M phase, as identified in the Project’s
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA).
This NVMP has been prepared based on relevant regulations, guidelines and standards at the time of preparation of the
ESIA Study, dated March 2025. Any alteration of the Project activities, revision or new regulations, guidelines or
applicable standards may require revision of this NVMP and subsequent review & approval.
This NVMP (1st iteration) shall be updated by the appointed Principal Contractor (PC) into an NVMP (2nd iteration),
prior to commencement of works.
The plan is applicable to the following project-impacted areas:
     •     Right Bank Quarry Sites. No existing receptor identified from the google imagery, although receptors envisaged
           during quarrying operation.
     •     Left Bank Quarry Sites. No existing receptor identified from the google earth imagery, although receptors
           envisaged during quarrying operation.
     •     Dam Construction Site including Water Pathway Tunnels, Workers Camp and Construction Yard.
     •     Nearby Communities and Roads.
           −     Uch-Terek; Settlements adjoining to the Highways
           −     Kara-Jygach; Settlements adjoining to the Highways
           −     Kambarata-2 HPP (Working personnel)
           −     Small scale industrial establishments near the Highway and the HPP Approach Road
     •     Resettlement areas.
     •     Powerhouse



2. Objectives
The objectives of the NVMP are to:
     •     develop a noise and vibration management plan detailing any recommendations for noise and vibration
           mitigation or management in line with the International Finance Corporation’s (IFC) Environmental Health and
           Safety Guidelines, 2007(EHS)2.
     •     reduce the disturbance caused by noise and vibration on local communities, residents, and workers,
           particularly during pre-construction, construction, and O&M phases.
     •     ensure the project complies with local, national, and international regulations and standards regarding noise
           and vibration levels.
     •     manage noise and vibration impacts that may arise during the construction phase, including from blasting,
           heavy machinery, and transportation activities.




2https://www.ifc.org/content/dam/ifc/doc/2000/2007-general-ehs-guidelines-noise-en.pdf

4. Governing Standards and Additional Guidance
Key regulations, standards and guidelines which have informed this plan, and which offer additional guidance are
outlined in Table 10.
Table 10: Applicable Standards and Regulations

 Guidelines/Regulations                                     Applicability
 Kyrgyz Republic Regulations, Standards, Guidelines & Policies
 Kyrgyz Republic National Noise Standards                   Articulates ambient noise quality standards
 International Standards and Guidelines
 WBG guidelines                                             Articulates ambient noise quality standards
 WB ESS1: Assessment and Management of                      Sets an overarching framework for assessing, managing, and monitoring
 Environmental and Social Risks and Impact                  environmental and social impacts associated with each stage of a project.
 WB ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions                      Ensures fair treatment, non-discrimination, and safe and healthy working
                                                            conditions for project workers.
 ESS4: Community Health and Safety                          Addresses the risks and impacts of the project on the health, safety, and
                                                            security of affected communities.
 ADB SPS 1: Environment                                     Outlines requirements pertaining to sustainable management of natural
                                                            resources, pollution prevention through impact assessment and compliance
                                                            with the requirements is attained through implementation of management
                                                            plans.
 AIIB ESS 1: Environmental and Social                       Guidance to support the integration of environmental and social risk reduction
 Assessment and Management                                  measures into the Project lifecycle.
 EIB Standard 1: Environmental and Social                   Guides effective and sustained environmental and social performance through
 Impacts and Risks                                          the establishment of an E & S management system in line with the identified
                                                            impacts.
 EBRD PR 1: Assessment and Management of                    Establishes the framework for identifying, assessing, and managing
 Environmental and Social Impacts and Issues                environmental and social impacts, including impacts on air quality due to
                                                            project activities, throughout a project’s lifecycle.
 EBRD PR 3 – Resource Efficiency and                        Assists in achieving resource efficiency and robust pollution control, like
 Pollution Prevention and Control                           managing emissions to air during various phases of the project.



5. Context
The proposal for the Kambarata – 1 HPP is a1,880 MW facility designed to produce approximately 5,640 GWh of
electricity each year. The proposed Project consists of the following main elements:
     •     Roller-compacted concrete (RCC) gravity dam is 10 m wide at the crest and has a maximum height above the
           riverbed of 260.8 m.
     •     A reservoir area of 61.7 km2 at a full supply level (FSL) of 1,198 msl. The inundated area extends approximately
           68 km upstream from the Dam wall.
     •     An overground powerhouse including Francis-type turbines with a total electricity generating capacity of 1,880
           MW.
     •     Quarry sites to source raw materials for the Dam.
     •     Temporary concrete batching plant, materials storage, waste disposal, construction sites and workforce
           accommodation facilities.
     •     Permanent and temporary accommodation facilities for Dam and hydropower operations and maintenance
           personnel.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1              Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                             11 August 2025                              Page | 27
P2: Noise and Vibration Management Plan (NVMP)



     •     Associated facilities (i.e. a 16 km access road the K-1 site to the existing road network; a bridge over the Naryn
           River; temporary workers’ camp; a 23.6 km long 110kv overhead transmission line; and a 110/6 kv substation).
     •     Noise is expected to be primarily generated during the site preparation and construction phases of the Project.
           The principal source of noise during the construction of the Kambarata-1 (K-1) HPP would be through the
           operation of construction equipment and machinery, including the transportation of this equipment and
           materials to the Project Site. The operation of earth-moving and rock breaking machinery, e.g. excavators,
           graders and vibratory rollers; construction equipment, e.g. cranes, operated pile drivers, generators, and
           cement slurry pumps, in particular are considered to pose the potential to generate high noise levels in the
           range of 90- 120 dB (A).
     •     The predominant source of vibration is expected to be from drilling and blasting operations, earthworks and
           excavation works, construction of foundations and structures and from movement of heavy construction
           equipment. This can cause disturbance on the surrounding sensitive receptors, adjoining the project area.
Vibration is expected from following activities:
     •     Drilling and blasting operations: conducting controlled explosions during the preparation of pits and clearing
           of areas for dams and other hydraulic structures.
     •     Earthworks and excavation work: the use of heavy construction equipment (excavators, bulldozers, loaders)
           to excavate, load and transport large volumes of soil.
     •     Construction of foundations and structures: driving piles, constructing pile foundations, compacting soils using
           specialized vibration compaction mechanisms.
     •     Movement of heavy construction equipment: movement of heavy loads, construction machinery and
           equipment across the construction site and adjacent public roads.
The NVMP is linked to several other management plans & sub-plans as presented below:
     •     Construction Environmental & Social Management Plan: An overarching plan comprising the management
           measures suggested during the Construction phase for mitigating adverse environmental & social impacts of
           the project, organizational charts, detailed description of the audit process, etc. Construction Environmental &
           Social Management Plan will be prepared by the EPC contractor after their engagement.
     •     Occupational Health & Safety Plan: Details the measures required to maintain a safe, healthy and secure work
           environment for all employees working under the project and provide safety guidelines & procedures for
           employees and to clearly define roles and responsibilities for its implementation.
     •     Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan: Deals with the management of community health
           and disease vectors, including management measures which ensure the health and safety of the community
           (e.g. health and disease awareness).
     •     Stakeholders Engagement and Grievance Redress Mechanism: Provides the plan for engaging with all
           stakeholders and the workforce of the Project, and responding to grievances relating to noise and vibration, or
           other project activities.
     •     Project De-commissioning and Construction Closeout Management Plan including Site Restoration Plan:
           Presents the plan for the restoration of the land on a continual basis as soon as construction in a particular
           zone is completed and are vital to ensure the Project meets the desired standards. Further, it also includes plan
           for managing adverse impacts on air environment due to noise and vibration generated during construction
           close-out and project decommissioning, along with rehabilitation of the cleared site.
     •     Traffic and Road Safety Management Plan: Delineates the mitigation measures for traffic & road safety
           management, including noise and vibration generated from the traffic.
     •     Blasting and Explosives Management Plan: Outlines procedures and safety protocols for the controlled use of
           explosives during construction, quarrying operations, including the storage, transportation, handling, and
           detonation of explosives, while minimizing risks to personnel and environment from noise and vibration and
           other blasting activities.
     •     Biodiversity Management Plan: Describes plans to conserve, restore, and enhance biodiversity, including
           mitigation of impacts due to noise and vibration on aquatic and terrestrial fauna.

 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                    Page | 28
P2: Noise and Vibration Management Plan (NVMP)




6. Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
Noise and vibration from the construction of hydropower dam can have a range of impacts and associated risks on the
environment, communities, workers, and infrastructure. The Project is located in a river valley of a mountainous terrain.
The ambient or background noise is consistent with a largely natural and non-anthropogenic area. The mountainous
terrain in the area would limit noise propagation to any human receptors near the dam and HPP site, however, during
the construction period o, the workers' camp set up immediately downstream on the right bank may experience higher
noise levels. Since there are no sensitive receptors nearby, other than the construction workers, the noise impacts are
expected to be minor.
Below are the key impacts and risks:
     •     P2-1: noise and vibration generated by construction activities which may impact to all nearby receptors
           including community, workforce and aquatic and terrestrial fauna:
           −     Drilling and charging
           −     Blasting
           −     Operation of crusher and batching plants
     •     P2-2: noise and vibration generated from vehicular movement including movement of heavy vehicles on the
           highway, approach road and project site, utility vehicles, reversing beepers and idling vehicles
     •     P2-2: noise and vibration impact to the sensitive receptors including fish and other aquatic species in the Naryn
           river and other adjoining streams

8. Stakeholders Engagement
On a regular basis the EPC during the entire construction phase and EPP during O&M phase especially for the hydropower
plant need to engage with all stakeholders which include nearby community in the dam upstream and downstream areas
regarding any concerns faced related to noise and vibration aspects. All such feedback from various stakeholders needs
to be addressed suitably and update the NVMP to monitor the relevant aspects for proper compliance.



9. Community Complaints and Grievance Redressal
   Mechanism
The Grievance Redressal Mechanism (GRM) will be set-up within the project programmes which will offer a platform for
stakeholders including local communities to voice their concerns and issues related to the project. It will be accessible to
all stakeholders, with regular updates to ensure its relevance throughout both the construction and operation phases.
In case of any issues or concerns related to noise and vibration received from community and the stakeholders, the same
can be communicated by the PMO SLO to the GRM committee for prompt addressal.



10. Training and Capacity Development Programs
The following training and capacity development for educating and raising awareness among all construction personnel
regarding noise and vibration aspect will be carried out on routine basis:
     •     Training personnel on Blasting and Explosives Management Plan.
     •     Training personnel to install vibration damping pads or devices, if compatible and possible.
     •     Encouraging worker to worn PPE (earplugs, earmuffs, OR canal caps) at all times.
     •     Training vehicle drivers on Traffic and Road Safety Management Plan.
     •     Encouraging vehicle driver to switch off vehicles when not in use, including when waiting to enter site.
     •     Encouraging machine operator to shut down between work periods or throttled down to an idling state, when
           used intermittently

P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)




1.                Scope
Abstraction and utilisation of surface water from the Naryn River and its tributaries during all phases of the Kambarata-
1 HPP project—pre-construction, construction, and operation—will be carefully optimised to avoid overuse of water
resources, prevent contamination of surface water bodies, and ensure that any effluent discharged back into the Naryn
River complies with the most stringent applicable water quality standards, whether set by the WHO or the Government
of the Kyrgyz Republic.
This will be achieved through the implementation of the Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP),
described in this section. The WQRMP sets out a framework of interlinked activities aligned with the construction and
operational schedules of the project. It defines specific roles and responsibilities for implementation and follows the
PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle to support continuous improvement.
During the pre-construction and construction phases, the EPC Contractor will finalise and update the WQRMP framework
for specific construction zones as part of the Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP), which
would be prepared, adopted and updated periodically by the Contractor as per the advice of PMO. Upon completion of
construction and the withdrawal of the EPC Contractor, the Project Proponent (through the PMO) will assume
responsibility for updating the WQRMP in the form of an Operational Environmental and Social Management Plan
(OESMP), thereby ensuring that all mitigation and monitoring measures continue to be implemented effectively during
operation.
Both C-ESMP and OESMP documents associated with the WQRMP will be subject to periodic review and updates based
on audit findings during project implementation.
The WQRMP will apply to the following impact areas:
     •     Impact Area 1: The construction site for the dam and associated plant including: quarries and borrow pits used
           as a source of raw materials for Dam construction, access roads and related infrastructure, as well as areas
           where wastes from the excavation of tunnels and other structures will be disposed of.
     •     Impact Area 2: The area inundated behind the dam (approximately 61.7 sq. km at a full supply level of 1,198
           msl) forming the reservoir.
     •     Impact Area 3: The main channel (including riverbanks) of the Naryn River immediately downstream of the dam
           wall to the upstream face of the K-2 HPP HPP dam.
     •     Impact Area 4: Construction camp for workers.
     •     Impact Area 5: Resettlement areas.



2.                Objectives
The WQRMP is designed to manage and safeguard water resources throughout the lifecycle of the Kambarata-1 HPP
project. Its specific objectives include:
     •     Ensure optimal use of abstracted water, including maximising reuse and recycling of water withdrawn from the
           Naryn River and its tributaries, thereby reducing dependency on fresh water abstraction and minimising stress
           on the river system.
     •     Treat abstracted water to appropriate quality standards before use in construction activities, and apply further
           treatment where necessary to render it suitable for domestic use (e.g., for worker camps), in compliance with
           national and international guidelines.
     •     Ensure all wastewater generated from project activities is appropriately treated before discharge, such that it
           meets applicable effluent quality standards set by the Government of the Kyrgyz Republic or WHO, whichever
           is more stringent, to protect downstream ecosystems and users. The determination of the relevant standard or
           guideline values to be referred would be made based on the respective parameter of interest and plan measures
           accordingly.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                   Page | 36
P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)



     •     Prevent contamination of natural surface water bodies, particularly in and around the key impact areas (1–4),
           by ensuring strict adherence to pollution prevention protocols, management of hazardous materials, and spill
           control procedures.
     •     Promote transparent communication and stakeholder engagement by informing affected communities and
           relevant authorities about water quality monitoring results, potential risks, and mitigation actions being
           undertaken.
     •     Safeguard the availability and quality of water for downstream users, ensuring that project-related abstractions,
           impoundments, and discharges do not compromise the quantity or quality of water currently accessed by
           downstream receptors, including those in transboundary areas, across all project phases.

regulatory requirements of the Kyrgyz Republic, as well as international good practice standards. These are summarised
below.

5.                Context and Baseline Water Quality
5.1               Context
The Kambarata HPP 1 is a hydropower project on the Naryn River, designed to generate renewable energy. The project
involves the construction of a high dam and reservoir, which can significantly alter surface water dynamics. Activities such
as excavation, blasting, and material storage can increase turbidity and introduce contaminants to the river system.
Additionally, worker accommodations and construction camps will produce domestic wastewater that could impact local
water quality if not treated and properly managed, adopting the philosophy of reuse and recycle, wherever possible. The
baseline water quality in Naryn River and adjoining streams would determine the level of treatment required for the use
of this water for construction purposes and the conservation of water resources through the reuse and recycling of the
wastewater generated from the construction yard and the level of treatment required for same. At the same time, it
would also be required to reuse abstracted water from the Naryn River to the maximum possible extent to prevent
overuse of water resources.
A summary of impacts and mitigation measures have been discussed in Section 6 and 7.
This plan is linked to several other management plans, including:
      •    Biodiversity Management Plan: To address impacts on aquatic ecosystems.
      •    Spill Prevention and Control Plan: To minimize contamination from hazardous materials.
      •    Waste Management Plan: To ensure proper disposal of construction and domestic waste.
      •    Dam Break Analysis and Emergency Preparedness Response Plan: To address accidental spills or contamination
           events.
      •    Air Environment Management Plan: To minimize air pollution due to construction and operation of the project
      •    Muck and Spoil Management Plan: To minimize impacts on local environment due to muck and spoil generation
           during excavation and tunnelling activities.
      •    Site Restoration Management Plan: To effectively clear the site comprising of dam, reservoir, HPP and other
           associated facilities after construction activities have been completed.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                  11 August 2025                              Page | 40
P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)




5.2               Baseline: Surface Water Quality Monitoring (Key
                  Findings)
5.2.1             Feasibility Study 2014
Water monitoring during the Feasibility Study (FS 2014) was conducted between September 21 and October 8, 2013. The
monitoring team collected 15 surface water samples from various rivers and tributaries, including the Naryn, Kokomeren,
Toluk, and Kambarata rivers. Analytical testing was carried out by accredited laboratories such as Stewart Assay
Environmental Laboratories and the Department of Environmental Monitoring under the Government of the Kyrgyz
Republic.

Key Findings:
     •     pH levels ranged from 7.9 to 8.2 (within permissible limits).
     •     Minimal contamination by oil products and heavy metals.
     •     Some exceedances of iron content due to natural geological formations.
     •     Mercury and arsenic levels within acceptable limits for cultural and household use.
     •     Alpha-activity was found to exceed the MPC in 11 surface water samples in the u/s and d/s of dam site.

5.2.2             First Season Environmental Monitoring 2024
To update the baseline scenario, environmental monitoring was conducted from October 14 to 23, 2024. Water samples
from 15 locations were analysed for pH, turbidity, dissolved oxygen, BOD, and heavy metals.

Key Findings:
     •     pH ranged from 7.9 to 8.1, indicating slightly alkaline conditions.
     •     Turbidity varied significantly between 0.58 to 362.5 mg/L
     •     Dissolved Oxygen (DO) ranged from 7.9 to 12.4 mg/L indicating healthy oxygen levels.
     •     Heavy metals such as arsenic and mercury exceeded permissible limits at some locations, requiring further
           verification in spring 2025.
     •     Mercury concentrations in some samples exceeded the Maximum permissible concentration (MPC) (Kyrgyz
           Standard, refer Table 16 and Table 17Error! Reference source not found. above) by up to 112 times, highlighting
           the need for comprehensive monitoring and analysis.

5.2.3             Second Season Environmental Monitoring 2025
To update the baseline scenario, environmental monitoring was conducted in April 2025. Water samples from 19
locations were analysed for pH, turbidity, dissolved oxygen and heavy metals.
     •     pH ranged from 6.8-8.2, indicating slightly acidic to slightly alkaline conditions
     •     Turbidity varied widely from 0.55 to 842 mg/dm3 which suggested that the surface water was highly turbid.
     •     Dissolved Oxygen ranged from 6.23 to 13.2 mg/dm3 indicating healthy oxygen levels.
     •     Heavy metals concentration was found to be within permissible limits at all locations. The exception being the
           concentration of Lead in only one sample exceeding the permissible limit.
     •     The Autumn 2024 monitoring results found significant heavy metal concentrations in multiple samples of surface
           water and ground water which could not be justified due to the absence of anthropogenic activities in the
           adjoining. In addition, the Spring 2025 monitoring results found that the concentration of heavy metals in
           surface and groundwater were within permissible limits which appeared to be more plausible. Also, in multiple
           surface water samples, the FS 2014 report detected the presence of radioactivity especially alpha activity which


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                   Page | 41
P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)



           has also been referenced in several secondary literature sources as discussed in Chapter 8: Biophysical Baseline
           Condition. Also, minor karst-like features identified in the Kambarata-1 Project area, as per AFRY FS May 2025,
           which may locally influence groundwater and surface water movement, as well as slope permeability. These
           features could lead to elevated turbidity, alkalinity, and hardness in nearby water sources. Hence, it is
           recommended that the EPC contractor should carry out detailed water quality tests to affirm the absence of
           heavy metal concentrations and radioactive emissions (alpha and beta emissions) in surface and ground water
           before using the same for drinking and domestic purposes in construction camps and for construction work.



6.                Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The impacts and risks in relation with surface water quality and resource associated with the Kambarata-1 HPP project
has been categorized in two levels, first with respect to the various phases of the project i.e. pre-construction,
construction and operation & maintenance (O&M) and secondly with respect to the impact area identified. Impact on
surface water quality of the Naryn River from construction and operations of the Project will affect not only the
community but other aquatic and riparian species which sustain on this water resource.
NOTE: The EPC contractor before engaging pre-construction activities like land clearing and land preparation would
carry out a surface water quality monitoring through accredited National laboratory to ensure that the baseline surface
water is free of any heavy metals or trace elements and radioactivity and safe for drinking.


6.1               Pre-construction Phase
The main activities involved during pre-construction phase of the project will be the widening and construction of access
roads along with clearing and preparation of land to set up the various construction facilities (labour’s camp, workshops,
batching plant, crushing plant, construction yard, storage areas like stockyards, silos) relevant to the project.

6.1.1             Land Clearing and Preparation
     •     P3-1: Clearing the natural vegetation of land for preparation of construction facilities exposes the natural soil to
           erosion from rainfall events. Eroded soil may then be carried into streams and main channel of the Naryn river
           increasing its turbidity and TSS levels.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in TSS, Turbidity


6.2               Construction Phase (Including Reservoir Filling)
During the construction phase of the project, an interlinked impact may arise from over usage of water (e.g. surface water
resources) and deterioration of water quality through contaminated runoff or untreated wastewater or effluent
discharge into the river. Impacts may arise from the construction zones where water usage will take place and has been
discussed in the proceeding sections.

6.2.1             Access Road
     •     P3-2: Surface runoff during rainfall spells may wash away road constructions materials like sub-base soil and fine
           aggregates into nearby run-off streams which ultimately contribute to the main channel of the Naryn River
           increasing the turbidity of the river water.
     •     P3-3: Fuel and grease spill from construction vehicles and equipment during construction of road may be washed
           into streams and main channel of Naryn River which may alter the turbidity, DO, COD, pH levels and toxicity of
           the surface water of the river and streams.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in turbidity, TSS, COD, toxicity, decrease in DO and alteration of
           pH levels.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                       Page | 42
P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)




6.2.2             Labour Camp
     •     P3-4: Risk to labour health if surface water abstracted from the Naryn River is not properly treated before
           supplying to labour camp for domestic use.
     •     P3-5: Wastewater generated in labour’s camp from domestic activities such as cooking, washing, flushing etc. if
           discharged into streams and channels of Naryn River without proper treatment may introduce organic pollutants
           and pathogens into the surface water thereby adversely altering its characteristics.
     •     P3-6: Introduction of nutrient loads into the surface water may result in eutrophication and consequently algal
           bloom.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in Turbidity, nutrient levels, pathogens, organic compounds
           decrease in DO and alteration of pH levels.

6.2.3             Construction Yard
     •     P3-7: Fine aggregates from the crushing plant, cement and fly ash3 or Supplementary Cement Materials (SCM)
           from the batching plant if not properly contained may enter the surface water streams degrading its quality with
           respect to turbidity, DO and pH levels. There may also be heavy metal contamination of surface water from run-
           off generated from Fly ash/ SCM storage and usage area to be used as construction material in concrete mix.
     •     P3-8: Fuel and grease spills from vehicles and equipment operating in the construction yard may contaminate
           the surface water quality and adversely alter its COD, toxicity, DO and pH levels.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in turbidity, COD, toxicity, decrease in DO and alteration of pH
           levels, heavy metal contamination from fly ash/ SCM.

6.2.4             Storage Facilities
     •     P3-9: In absence of proper dust control mechanisms, construction materials such as fly ash/ SCM and fine
           aggregates, sand especially from stockpiles may enter and contaminate surface water streams and river by
           altering their natural characteristics.
     •     P3-10: Leakage of oil, grease, fuel (and other chemicals if relevant) from their respective storages may
           contaminate surface water sources by increasing turbidity, COD, toxicity and altering pH levels.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in turbidity, COD, toxicity, decrease in DO and alteration of pH
           levels, heavy metal contamination from fly ash/ SCM.

6.2.5             Workshops
     •     P3-11: Improper containment of chemicals, grease and oil may result in spills and contamination of surface water
           upon meeting surface water sources. Effluent released from workshops will contain oil, grease, fuel and
           chemicals which if not properly treated before release into the surface water sources may result in adversely
           altering the qualities of the surface water.
            Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in turbidity, COD, toxicity, decrease in DO and alteration of pH
           levels

6.2.6             Construction Zones
     •     P3-12: Generation of muck and slurry during excavation and tunnelling activities may make the main channel of
           the Naryn River turbid.
     •     P3-13: Construction materials such as fine aggregates, cement, sand, fly ash/ SCM if not properly handled may
           increase the turbidity and TSS of the main channel of the Naryn River. In addition, there may be heavy metal
           contamination of the main channel of the Naryn River from the Fly ash/ SCM in the concrete mix.



3 Please Refer to Sub Plan 04: Materials and Waste Management Plan of ESMP, Chapter 14 for tests for heavy metals




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1           Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                             SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                          11 August 2025                 Page | 43
P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)



     •     P3-14: Fuel and grease spill from construction vehicles and equipment during tunnelling, construction of main
           dam, powerhouse may be washed into the main channel of Naryn River which may alter the turbidity, DO, COD,
           pH levels and toxicity of the surface water of the river and streams.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in turbidity, COD, toxicity, decrease in DO and alteration of pH
           levels, heavy metal contamination from fly ash/ SCM.

6.2.7             Main Channel of Naryn River
     •     P3-15: During the construction phase, it would be required to divert the river channel properly through diversion
           tunnels so that the downstream receptors are not deprived of any water requirement.
     •     P3-16: Diversion tunnels may add to sediment load if the tunnel walls are not properly lined
     •     Impact type on surface water resources and quality:
           −     No net decrease in water availability to downstream users is expected, provided flow continuity is
                 maintained through the diversion system.
           −     Potential increase in Total Suspended Solids (TSS) and turbidity in the Naryn River due to sediment
                 mobilisation from tunnel walls during flow diversion.

6.2.8             Quarry Site
     •     P3-17: As quarrying activities will involve excavation and clearing of natural vegetation, this may lead to
           increased erosion of top surface of soil resulting in sediment runoff into nearby surface water bodies increasing
           their turbidity and TSS levels. The quarry sites include the aggregate quarrying along with the sites identified for
           natural pozzolans if it is finalized to be used as an alternative to fly ash.
     •     P3-18: Potential introduction of harmful chemicals into surface water bodies such as nitrates from explosives
           and hydrocarbons in the form of fuel, grease from machinery.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in turbidity, COD, toxicity, decrease in DO levels.

6.2.9             Resettlement Area
The impacts concerning resettlement area will become effective from the last phase of the construction and will continue
to remain relevant throughout the operation and maintenance period independent of the dam operations. The impacts,
mitigations and monitoring plans will be applicable for only those people or community infrastructure who/which would
be displaced from the reservoir zone or water protection zone (sanitary zone) or for setting up of any associated facility
of the Kambarata 1 HPP project.
     •     P3-19: Risk to resettled inhabitants if surface water abstracted from the Naryn River or any additional surface
           water body is not properly treated before supplying water to resettlement area for domestic use.
     •     P3-20: Wastewater generated from the domestic household activities such as cooking, washing, flushing etc.
           within the resettlement area and discharged into rivers or any surface water bodies without proper treatment
           may introduce organic pollutants and pathogens into the surface water thereby adversely altering its
           characteristics.
           Impact type on surface water quality: Increase in Turbidity, nutrient levels, pathogens, organic compounds
           decrease in DO and alteration of pH levels.
     •     P3-21: In the event of the site not cleared properly of all construction leftovers and spares, unused materials and
           dumped wastes, there may be potential contamination of river water resources especially during reservoir
           impoundment. The same applies the camp area near confluence Kokomeren River and Naryn River and all other
           construction camps to be set up within the reservoir area which if not cleared and restored to its natural state
           cleaned up may result in potential contamination of river water and surface water resources.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                      Page | 44
P3: Water Quality and Resource Management Plan (WQRMP)




6.2.10            Presence of Radioactivity in Surface Water
     •     P3-22: As indicated in the FS 2014 report as well as other recent literature findings, there is a possibility of
           presence of radionuclides in the Naryn river within the Project study area which if found consistently at higher
           than MPC may carry a potentially adverse impact on the use of this water in construction as well as potable use.
           If subsequent monitoring results prove the presence of high radioactivity in the Naryn River, then there is a
           possibility of the same in the ground water aquifers and hence must be monitored before utilizing it as potable
           water.


6.3               Operation and Maintenance Phase
NOTE: The PMO before initiating operation of the project would carry out a surface water quality monitoring through
accredited National laboratory to establish a baseline surface water quality.
     •     P3-23: Wastewater generated from activities such as floor washing, machine cleaning etc. and other domestic
           activities in HPP building disposed into the Naryn River. Considering, that the total workforce during operation
           of the dam will be approximately 200 (as per AFRY FS June 2025) and that 80% of these workers will be residing
           with their families, therefore for a total of 520 residents during the operation phase, it is estimated that the total
           domestic water requirement will be 104 KLD (peak demand) and corresponding domestic waste water generated
           will be 83.2 KLD (considering 80% of water requirement as domestic wastewater generated). Hence, STP of
           capacity ~100 KLD is proposed for treatment of domestic wastewater during the O & M phase of the project. If
           there are any alteration in number of workforce personnel and residing family members of the workforce, then
           the corresponding domestic water requirement and subsequent domestic wastewater generation would be
           updated.
           Impact type on surface water resources and surface water quality: increase in turbidity, BOD, COD, toxicity,
           decrease in DO levels.

A map of the tentative locations for taking samples corresponding to the various monitoring related to water quality and resource management during the construction and operation
phase of the project as discussed in Table 19 above of the project is shown in Figure 6. The drinking water sampling locations have been indicated for the construction period
assuming the tentative workforce camp location provided. The wastewater sampling locations are adjacent to the workforce camps and workshops assuming that STP and ETP will be
located at that location only. The sample location for surface water quality monitoring will include parameters such as turbidity, TSS, DO, COD, pH (for both the construction and
operation period) as well as alpha and beta radioactive emissions (for the construction period only).

7.                Stakeholders Engagement
On a regular basis the EPC Contractors, during construction and EPP during O&M need to engage with all stakeholders
which include nearby community stakeholders in the dam upstream and downstream areas regarding any concerns
related to improper management of water resource and quality aspects. All such feedback from various stakeholders
needs to be addressed suitably and recorded by the Owner. The WQRMP shall be periodically updated in light of the
results of such engagement to efficiently monitor the relevant aspects for proper compliance.



8.                Community Complaints and Grievance
                  Redressal Mechanism
The Grievance Redressal Mechanism (GRM) will be set up within the project programmes, which will offer a platform for
stakeholders, including local communities, to voice their concerns and issues related to the project. It will be accessible
to all stakeholders, with regular updates to ensure its relevance throughout both the construction and operation phases.
In case of any issues or concerns related to quantity of water received from the Naryn River or the quality of water
received, the same can be communicated by the community and the stakeholders to the GRM which will be taken up and
addressed promptly.



9.                Training and Capacity Development Programs
The following training and capacity development for educating and raising awareness among all construction personnel
regarding surface water abstraction and quality will be carried out on a routine basis:
     •     Encouraging the reusing of abstracted water from Naryn River
     •     Training personnel on SPCC plan
     •     Training on construction materials handling

 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                                    SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                                 11 August 2025                                             Page | 59
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




The comprehensive material management plan for the Kambarata-1 Hydropower Plant (HPP) Project involves meticulous
planning and execution across several key areas, including strategic sourcing and procurement, detailed inventory
management, and efficient storage and handling of materials. It ensures that the right resources are available precisely
when needed, in the correct quantities, and at optimal costs. An effective material management plan not only prevents
material losses but also protects the air environment, land and surface water resources from potential contamination
that may arise from improper material management.
The proposed Kambarata-1 HPP Project is also expected to generate a considerable amount of waste during its pre-
construction, construction, and operational phases. According to the Impact Assessment Chapter of this ESIA report,
improper management of this waste could lead to significant adverse effects on air quality, soil quality, and both ground
and surface water quality. Additionally, it poses substantial risks to occupational and community health and safety within
the project area and its surroundings.
To address these issues, the Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP) has been developed, offering detailed
guidelines for implementing measures to reduce the negative impacts associated with material use, handling, and waste
generation. It outlines detailed strategies for effective material and waste management, clearly defines roles and
responsibilities, and specifies budget allocations for both capital expenditures (CAPEX) and operational expenditures
(OPEX). Furthermore, the plan includes a periodic monitoring mechanism designed to ensure the ongoing effectiveness
of the waste management strategies. During the pre-construction and construction phases, the EPC Contractor will
finalise and update the M&WMP as part of the Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP), which
would be prepared, adopted and updated periodically by the Contractor as per the advice of PMO. Upon completion of
construction and the withdrawal of the EPC Contractor, the Project Proponent (through the PMO) will assume
responsibility for updating the M&WMP in the form of an Operational Environmental and Social Management Plan
(OESMP), thereby ensuring that all mitigation and monitoring measures continue to be implemented effectively during
operation.
Both C-ESMP and OESMP documents associated with the M&WMP will be subject to periodic review and updates based
on audit findings during project implementation.



1.                Scope
The scope of the Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP) is applicable to the following impact areas,
     •     The construction site for the dam structures and its associated facilities, including quarries and borrow pits used
           as sources of raw materials for dam construction, a storage yard for material laydown, workshops, and potential
           truck parking areas.
     •     The inundated area behind the dam (approximately 61.7 Sq. Km at a full supply level of 1,198 msl), forming the
           reservoir, where potential contamination from the waste disposal site may occur.
     •     Construction camp for workers.
     •     Project Access road and all haul roads from quarries & borrow pits to the construction site



2.                Objectives
The objective of the M&WMP for the Kambarata-1 Hydropower Plant is to,
     •     Prevent Material Loss and Pollution: Prevention and minimization of loss of raw materials, along with
           prevention of pollution of air environment, land and surface water resources from potential contamination,
           likely to arise from improper management of materials
     •     Minimize Waste Generation: Implementation of strategies to reduce waste at the source, such as optimizing
           construction processes and using materials efficiently to minimize excess and scrap.
     •     Ensure Safe Handling and Storage: Establishment of procedures for the safe handling and storage of materials
           & waste, including hazardous material, to prevent environmental contamination and ensure worker safety.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                      Page | 60
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




     •     Promote Effective Treatment and Disposal: Developing methods for treating and disposing of different types of
           waste, including recycling and reusing materials where possible, to reduce the environmental footprint of the
           hydropower project.
     •     Protect the Environment and Health: Mitigation of the environmental and health impacts of waste by
           implementing best practices for waste management, such as proper containment, transportation, and disposal
           of waste materials.
     •     Comply with Regulations: Ensuring adherence to relevant legal and regulatory requirements, including local,
           national, and international standards, to avoid legal penalties and promote sustainable practices.
     •     Monitor and Report: Monitoring and documentation of material and waste management activities to track
           progress, identification of areas for improvement, and ensuring transparency & accountability.
     •     Address Project-Specific Challenges: Proper alignment of material and waste management strategies to address
           the unique challenges of hydropower projects, such as transport, storage & handling huge quantities of RCC,
           dealing with construction debris, and handling solid and hazardous waste generated during all the phases of the
           proposed Kambarata-1 HPP.
By focusing on these objectives, responsible and sustainable material and waste management will be ensured, minimizing
its impact on the environment and local communities.
