5.                Context
The construction and operation of the Kambarata-1 Hydropower project will utilise various types of materials and
generate various types of waste across its phases. In the pre-construction phase, organic waste from vegetation removal
and construction waste from fencing installation are key concerns. During construction, significant waste includes scrap
stones and debris from quarrying, solid & hazardous waste from workers’ camps, and packaging and hazardous waste
from material storage facilities. Additionally, spillage of construction materials during transportation poses
environmental risks. In the operation and maintenance phase, hazardous waste from hydropower plant maintenance
and both solid and hazardous waste from site offices will be generated, requiring effective management to minimize
environmental impact. The link to other management plans with the M&WMP is given in Table 22 below.

Table 22: Links to other Management Plans
  Management Plans linked with the Waste
                                                                                   Description of the Linked Management Plan
      Management Plan (M&WMP)
 Air Quality Management Plan                              Refer to this plan for the monitoring of air pollution (e.g. particulate matter
                                                          emission during transportation of waste, odour of obnoxious gases at waste
                                                          disposal sites).
 Surface and Ground Water (including                      Refer to this plan for the monitoring of surface and ground water quality in the
 Springs) Quality Management Plan                         potential contaminated areas during transportation, storage and disposal of
                                                          waste.
 Catchment Area Treatment Plan including                  Refer to this plan for the monitoring of water quality in the Naryn River
 Soil Erosion and Sediment Control                        catchment, to detect and manage diffuse pollution.
 Management                                               Refer to this plan for the monitoring of suspended sediment in watercourses,
                                                          from diffuse pollution sources.
 Muck and Spoil Management Plan                           Refer to this plan for implementing the management measures identified to
                                                          alleviate the impacts of muck and spoil generated during the construction of
                                                          proposed Kambarata 1 HPP project structures like headrace tunnel, tailrace
                                                          tunnel, powerhouse, RCC dam and its associated facilities like access roads and
                                                          tunnels.
 Worker Camp Management Plan                              Refer to this plan for management of waste generated due to domestic activities
                                                          in workers’ camp.
 Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) and                    Refer to this plan and mechanisms for management of grievances raised during
 Grievance Redressal Mechanisms (project                  the pre-construction, construction and operation period of the Kambarata-1 HPP.
 and labor)



6.                Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The summary of key impacts and risks of material transport, storage and handling in different phases of the Kambarata-
1 Hydropower project are depicted in the following sub-sections.


6.1               Material Transport, Storage and Handling
6.1.1             Construction Materials
The construction activities of Roller-Compacted Concrete (RCC) Dam, Penstock, Spillways, etc., associated with
Kambarata-1 HPP typically involves mass amount of concreting. The raw materials required for the batching of concrete
include fine and coarse aggregates, cement, fly-ash and water. The management of these raw materials with proper
environmental measures for handling, transportation and storage of respective materials are detailed in subsequent
sections.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                  Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                    SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                 11 August 2025                            Page | 67
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




6.1.1.1           Fine and Coarse Aggregates
     •     Sourcing of Coarse Aggregate: The coarse aggregates will be sourced from nearby quarry sites. The gravel and
           chunk of stones will be extracted from quarry sites, go through screening and be crushed through the crusher
           system into different sizes and segregated through separators. The tentative locations of quarry areas along with
           material laydown areas of construction materials and crusher system are shown in a map in Annex 1, prepared
           in line with the Potential Site Installation Area figure provided by AFRY4. The segregated coarse aggregates
           having a larger size would be used for the construction of the dam, and the aggregates having a comparatively
           smaller size will be used for the construction of other project components of the hydropower plant.
     •     Sourcing of Fine Aggregate: The fine aggregates would be sourced from the finer particles of crushed material
           from the quarry site, segregated through separators. Additionally, fine aggregates may be also acquired from
           riverbanks through selected suppliers. In this regard, conducting market analysis, researching and evaluating the
           potential capability of suppliers for providing the raw material throughout the construction phase of Kambarata-
           1 HPP, would be assessed, ensuring the supplier would maintain quality and adopt the specified precautionary
           measures during transportation, handling and storage of fine aggregate.
     •     Inventory Management of aggregate: This includes maintaining accurate records of both fine and coarse
           aggregate quantities, storage locations, and usage rates. These records will ensure efficient use of raw materials
           as per the scheduled construction activities and reduce material stocking as well as wastage. The supplier
           performance would be continuously monitored through material testing of specified parameters of fine and
           coarse aggregates (Fine Aggregate: weight, bulk density, sieve analysis, moisture absorption capacity, etc.
           Coarse Aggregate: weight, bulk density, shape and size etc.)
     •     Transportation, Handling and Storage: Key practices to be adopted for proper transportation, handling and
           storage of fine and coarse aggregates at the construction site are outlined as follows:
           −     Fine aggregates would also be extracted and transported to the site using appropriate vehicles such as
                 dump trucks, ensuring precautionary measures during handling and transportation from the quarries and
                 local market.
           −     Open stockpiles for storage of coarse aggregate.
           −     For transport of segregated coarse aggregates of different sizes from quarry sites, conveyor belt would be
                 used from quarry site to silos nearby batching plant. The expected length of the conveyor from the
                 crushing point to the batching plants would be (~ 20-50 m). The conveyor belt would be covered to
                 prevent spillage and loss of material. This also reduces the potential emission of particulate matter during
                 transporting aggregates through the conveyor belt.
           −     Both fine and coarse aggregates will be stored in designated areas with proper containment to prevent
                 contamination and degradation of underlying soil and groundwater.
           −     Garland drains with catchpits would be constructed around each of the designated storage sites of fine
                 and coarse aggregate to arrest silt and sediment flows into Naryn River and reuse & recycling the
                 sediment as raw material in construction.
                 -     Any aggregates transported by dumpers from the local market, would be covered with tarpaulin sheet
                       during transportation to prevent potential spillage on the bank of the Naryn River.

6.1.1.2           Cement and Fly-ash or other Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCM)
     •     Sourcing and Procurement: This includes identifying reliable suppliers, evaluating their quality standards, and
           complying with relevant environmental and safety regulations to ensure a steady supply of high-quality cement
           for construction work of different project components. Incorporating fly ash or other supplementary
           cementitious material (SCM) with pozzolanic properties is a sustainable practice that enhances the properties
           of concrete. Before procuring fly ash or other SCM, the material shall be tested at periodic intervals for
           contamination by heavy metals and trace elements e.g. Hg, Pb, Cd, Cr etc and radionuclides. A valid test
           certificate confirming the results must be obtained from the supplier. The EPC Contractor will then review the




4 Figure 8-1: Potential Site Installation Area in Draft Interim Report (Concept Design) – February 2025 by AFRY




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                  SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                               11 August 2025                 Page | 68
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




           Contamination Analysis Reports and intermittently conduct their own tests5 of any presence of these elements
           to cross check before accepting the fly ash or other SCM for use in dam construction.
     •     Inventory Management: This includes maintaining accurate records of bags of cement (Weight: 50 Kg each),
           brought to the site by 25-ton trucks. The supplier performance would be continuously monitored through
           material testing of specified parameters of Cement (fineness, consistency test, strength test, heat of hydration,
           soundness test etc). These records will also ensure efficient use of quality raw materials as per the scheduled
           construction activities and reduce material wastage. A test certificate from the supplier would be obtained for
           cement and fly ash/ SCM from dedicated sources.
     •     Transportation, Handling and Storage: Key practices to be adopted for proper transportation, handling and
           storage of cement at the construction site are outlined as follows:
           −     Transportation of cement would be done in cement bags of weight (~50-60 Kg each).
           −     Transportation of fly ash/ SCM would be done by covered containers/sealed bags.
           −     Cement would be stored in a dry, enclosed space to protect it from moisture.
           −     A raised platform would be used to keep cement bags off the ground.
           −     Bags would be stacked properly, and the 'first-in, first-out' (FIFO) method would be followed to use older
                 stock first.
           −     Dust extraction systems would be adopted to minimize emission of fugitive dust during handling of fly-
                 ash/ SCM, if necessary.
           −     Fly-ash/ SCM would be stored at site in covered storage or silos of capacity (Standard capacity: ~1500-
                 10000 tons)
           −     Garland drains with catchpits would be constructed around each of the designated storage sites of cement
                 and fly ash/ SCM to catch the run-off from the covered storage roof-tops and arrest silt & sediment flows
                 into the Naryn River.
           −     Regular inspections of storage areas would be conducted to check for signs of moisture ingress or
                 contamination.

6.1.1.3           Water for construction purposes
     •     Sources of Water for construction: Most of the water to be used for construction purpose would be sourced
           from the Naryn river, ensuring proper quality for industrial use.
     •     Inventory Management: Construction water usage would involve accurate demand forecasting, real-time
           monitoring and tracking with water-meters and updating in the system.
     •     Water Supply and Storage at the construction site: The supply of water for construction at the Kambarata-1
           HPP will be managed through dedicated water pipelines, including pumps of adequate capacity and a complete
           river intake system and stored in a reservoir of adequate capacity with the construction yard. If necessary, the
           water reservoir will need to be provided with an impervious liner to prevent any losses to the underlying
           fractured rocks.

6.1.1.4           Chemicals for RCC dam and HPP construction
     •     Sourcing and Procurement: In general, the admixture that can reduce the heat of hydration of cement and slow
           down the hardening process, are emphasized in the construction of dam and reservoir. Typical examples of
           chemicals would be Epoxy Resin, water proofing agents (to prevents seepage), sealants (for filling joints), Red-
           oxide/zinc compounds (to prevent corrosion and rusting), etc. The potential suppliers of respective chemicals
           will be identified and strategized for procurement, ensuring the proper handling and storage to avoid potential
           contamination of the surrounding environment.




5 In the absence of any standard specification for fly ash in Kyrgyzstan, international specifications like ASTM C618 fly ash specification and AASHTO M

295 may be referred.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1               Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                 SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                              11 August 2025                                  Page | 69
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




     •     Inventory Management: Register to be maintained to keep the records of chemical inventory to be used in
           construction and to ensure that chemical wastage can be prevented.
     •     Transportation, Handling and Storage: Key practices to be adopted for proper transportation, handling and
           storage of chemicals to be used as admixture in concrete are,
           −     Admixture/Chemicals would be transported in sealed bags/jars by trucks.
           −     These would be stored in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight and extreme temperatures to
                 prevent degradation.
           −     All containers would be clearly labelled with the name of the admixture, its concentration, and any hazard
                 warnings (HAZMAT) to ensure safe handling.
           −     Spill containment measures, such as dykes and spill kits, would be arranged to arrest any accidental
                 releases.
           −     Regular training sessions would be conducted for staff and the workforce on the proper handling, storage,
                 and emergency procedures related to chemical admixtures using appropriate PPEs.

6.1.1.5           Ready-mix Concrete from Batching Plants
The concrete mix from batching plants would be transferred to construction zones of the dam as well as other project
components, through a conveyor belt. The expected length of the conveyor from batching plants to dam site would be
(~600-1200 m). The conveyor belt and concrete conveyor transfer points would be of covered design to prevent any
spillage and losses of materials.

6.1.1.6           Steel Reinforcement
     •     Procurement: The construction of the hydropower plant and other project components would require a
           significant amount of steel reinforcement which would be procured from reliable suppliers ensuring the quality,
           sizes (diameter and length), and tensile strength property of the steel.
     •     Precautionary measures: Key practices to be adopted for proper handling, transportation and storage of
           reinforcement steel are outlined below,
           -     During transportation, the reinforcement steel rods/bars will be covered by packaging material.
           -     Steel reinforcement rods/bars are to be stored in a dry, covered area to prevent contact of moisture
                 resulting in corrosion.

6.1.1.7           Excavated Soil used as backfilling material
An excavation up to 20 m depth for the construction of the dam and up to 40 m depth for the powerhouse is required,
as per the preliminary design of FS Consultant. Approximately, about 14,38,116 m 3 of rock cuts and 72,74,699 m3 of soil
would be generated from excavation work of the dam and the HPP. These materials would need to be properly stored at
designated locations for reuse in backfilling after the completion of construction. Following are the criteria for selection
of designated storage of these materials:
     •     Excavated soil and rocks would be stored at safe distance from the edge of the excavation to prevent
           destabilization.
     •     Storage areas would be on stable ground, away from slopes or areas prone to vibrations from heavy equipment
     •     Storage areas would be located away from Naryn River to avoid potential contamination of surface water body
           from the contaminants in stored material.
     •     Adequate drainage system will be provided along the external periphery of the designated storage arears to
           catch surface water runoff, with provisions for catch pits and centralised sump pits to trap suspended materials
           in the runoff water.
Only any additional quantity of rock that cannot be used for backfilling would need to be transported out of the
construction site. On the other hand, soils need to be fetched from nearby identified borrow pits for completing the
backfilling and site leveling work.

 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                       Page | 70
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




Transportation of required Soil and for disposal of surplus rocks for the above construction would be carried out by trucks
from the place of origin to the designated sites of re-use as backfilling material at other construction sites or other low-
lying area for development, to be determined by EPC Contractor. This would be carried out by properly covering the
trucks with impermeable sheets like tarpaulin. The haul roads where this truck transportation would be carried out would
be sprinkled periodically to contain any fugitive dust emission.

6.1.2             Mechanical and Electrical Equipment
Material management of the electro-mechanical equipment required in hydropower projects involves planning,
procuring, storing, and controlling essential components like turbines and generators.

6.1.2.1           Mechanical Equipment
     •     Identification of list of equipment required: Mechanical equipment essential for the construction of the
           Kambarata-1 HPP would tentatively include turbines (Francis Turbine) for harnessing the kinetic energy of water
           flow of the Naryn river, valves for controlling water flow and ensuring safe operation, pumps for cooling systems



6 The Construction water from Naryn River shall be periodically tested for radioactive emission (alpha, beta) before accepting same for safe use in

construction. Please also refer to the ESMP Sub-Plan: 03 Water Quality and Resource Management Plan in this regard.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                      Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                        SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                     11 August 2025                             Page | 71
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




           and dewatering, and gates for regulating water flow into turbines and providing safety during maintenance.
           These components are crucial for efficient and safe generation of hydropower and overall plant operation.
     •     Transportation, Storage and Handling of equipment: Key practices to be adopted for transportation and fitting
           of mechanical equipment are,
           −     Planning of transportation routes carefully to avoid obstacles and ensure smooth transit of equipment.
           −     Designated warehouse for storage of mechanical equipment of powerhouse.
           −     Ensure all personnel are trained in the correct handling procedures and use of equipment
           −     Usage of ergonomic tools and techniques to minimize strain and injury during handling
           −     Usage of secure packaging materials to protect equipment from damage during transit
           −     Usage of appropriate loading techniques and equipment, such as cranes or forklifts, to safely load and
                 unload machinery

6.1.2.2           Electrical Equipment
     •     Identification of list of equipment required: Electrical equipment essential for the construction of Kambarata-
           1 HPP would include generators, which convert mechanical energy from turbines into electricity; transformers,
           which step up or step-down voltage for efficient transmission and distribution; and switchgear, which protects
           electrical circuits and equipment from overloads and short circuits. These are crucial for ensuring the efficient
           and safe operation of the plant.
     •     Precautionary Measures for Transportation, Handling and Storage of Electrical Equipment: Key practices to be
           followed for safe transportation, handling and storage of electrical equipment are,
           −     Planning of transportation routes carefully to avoid obstacles and ensure smooth transit of equipment.
           −     Designated warehouse for storage of electrical equipment of powerhouse.
           −     Ensure that the worker would be equipped with insulated operation tools and PPE, such as insulated
                 gloves, safety goggles, and rubber-soled shoes, which would be worn to protect against electrical hazards.
           −     Workers and staff would be adequately trained in electrical safety procedures and the correct use of
                 equipment.
           −     Clear warning signs would be displayed around high-voltage areas and equipment to alert workers, staff
                 and the nearby community of potential hazards.

6.1.3             Hazardous Materials
Material management of hazardous materials in hydropower projects involves identifying, handling, storing, and
disposing of hazardous substances like solvents, waste oil etc.

6.1.3.1           Inventory Management of Hazardous Materials
The volume of all hazardous materials will be recorded upon inward receipt, with each material tracked in terms of
quantity stored, quantity used or generated, and quantity disposed of. The sample inventory of hazardous materials is
represented in Table 24 below.

6.1.3.2           Transportation, Handling and Storage of Hazardous Material
Key measures to be adopted in handling, transportation and storage of Hazardous Waste are given as follows,
     •     Vehicle and machinery workshops and maintenance areas will have impermeable flooring and a sump that
           drains to an oil/water separator to collect any leaks and washdown water.
     •     All refuelling of plant and machinery will occur in designated locations. Refuelling at site will be undertaken using
           a suitably equipped service vehicle with appropriate spill prevention and containment.
     •     All construction plant and machinery (e.g. trucks, excavators, bulldozers, generators) will be maintained in good
           working order to prevent the leakage of contaminants.
     •     Vehicles used will be authorised to carry hazardous materials and the volumes of hazardous materials
           transported will be limited at any one time to reduce potential impacts from accidental rupture or leaks.
     •     The storage area of hazardous materials would be at least 100 m from any waterbody to prevent potential
           contamination of the Naryn River.
     •     There would be a provision for secondary containment at each potential source of hazardous waste generation.
     •     Flammable products will always be stored separately in dedicated areas with adequate ventilation.

6.1.4             Other Materials for Domestic and Administrative Requirements
6.1.4.1           Food material for working personnel
Handling, transportation, and storage of food materials for staff and workers involve maintaining high hygiene standards,
ensuring proper sanitization, segregating raw and cooked foods, controlling temperatures during transport, using
appropriate packaging, handling food carefully, storing perishable foods at correct temperatures, implementing stock
rotation, and conducting regular pest control inspections. These practices ensure food safety, quality, and prevent
contamination.

6.1.4.2           Paper usage in administrative work
Paper would be handled with clean hands to avoid smudges and damage. During transportation, it would be kept in
protective packaging to prevent tearing and exposure to moisture. For storage, paper wood be kept in a cool, dry place
with controlled humidity to prevent warping and deterioration. Proper shelving and filing systems help in organizing and
accessing paper efficiently.


6.2               Waste
The summary of key impacts and risks of waste generation in different phases of the Kambarata-1 HPP Project are
depicted in the following sub-sections,



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                      Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                        SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                     11 August 2025                            Page | 73
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




6.2.1             Pre-construction Phase
The pre-construction phase of the hydropower project involves several activities that contribute to waste generation,
including vegetation removal and installation of fencing. These activities can lead to significant environmental impacts
and risks, such as:
     •     P4-2-1 (Generation of Organic Waste due to vegetation removal): Clearing vegetation results in organic waste
           and can disrupt local ecosystems, leading to loss of biodiversity and soil erosion.
     •     P4-2-2 (Generation of Construction Waste during installation of fencing): The use of metal for fencing can
           produce construction waste, contributing to pollution in soil and water resources, if not managed properly.

6.2.2             Construction Phase
The construction phase of the hydropower project involves numerous activities that contribute to waste generation, each
with its own environmental impacts and risks, such as:
     •     P4-2-3 (Generation of scrap stones and debris during quarrying for construction aggregates, SCM and crushing
           operations): During quarrying for construction aggregates, SCM (if used as an alternative to fly ash) and crushing
           operations, various types of debris will be generated, including screenings (fine fractions from crushing), scrap
           stones (unsuitable large pieces) etc. These scrap stones and debris can cause land degradation, water pollution
           and air pollution from dust.
     •     P4-2-4 (Generation of Solid & Hazardous Waste at workers’ accommodation camp): Establishing and operating
           workers' camps produce domestic solid waste and potentially hazardous materials. This poses contamination of
           soil and water sources, air pollution from improper waste disposal, health risks to workers and nearby
           communities due to exposure to hazardous materials.
     •     P4-2-5 (Generation of Packaging Waste, Solid and Hazardous Waste at Construction Yard and Material
           Laydown Area): These areas will generate waste from packaging materials, damaged goods, and construction
           debris, which require proper disposal to prevent environmental contamination. The hazardous material stored
           at site may potentially contaminate soil if not stored properly.
     •     P4-2-6 (Potential spillage of construction materials during transportation of construction materials and
           equipment): Spilled materials, such as concrete, asphalt, or chemicals, can contaminate soil and water bodies,
           harming aquatic life and disrupting ecosystems.
     •     P4-2-7 (Generation and disposal of Construction Waste during construction activities for the RCC Dam,
           Penstock, Spillways and other structures associated with Kambarata-1 HPP): These activities will generate
           substantial construction waste, including concrete, metal, and packaging materials. These wastes have several
           impacts including land degradation, water pollution from runoff, air pollution from dust and particulate matter.
           Additionally, improper waste management can contribute to landfill overflow and disrupt local ecosystems.
     •     P4-2-8 (Improper handling, storage and disposal of blasting & explosive wastes): The blasting activities would
           result in generation of explosive and blasting wastes which are hazardous in nature due to their explosivity,
           flammability and toxicity. These wastes when disposed indiscriminately may result in contamination of soil and
           groundwater and also cause toxicity to health due to inhalation & ingestion.

6.2.3             Operation and Maintenance Phase
The Operation and Maintenance phase includes the maintenance of the HPP and associated site offices.
     •     P6-2-9 (Generation of Hazardous Waste during maintenance of Hydropower Plant): During the operation and
           maintenance of hydropower plants, hazardous waste will be generated, including lubricants, hydraulic fluids,
           and other maintenance-related materials. Additionally, periodic cleaning and replacement of parts will
           contribute to the generation of hazardous waste. This may cause potential negative impacts including
           contamination of soil and water sources, air pollution from the release of toxic substances, health risks to
           workers and nearby communities due to exposure to hazardous materials, and potential harm to local wildlife
           and ecosystems
     •     P6-2-10 (Generation of Solid & Hazardous Waste at O&M site offices): During the O&M phase at site offices,
           both general office waste (such as paper, food waste, packaging materials, and discarded supplies) and


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                     Page | 74
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




           hazardous waste (including waste oil from DG sets for emergency power back-up) will be generated. This poses
           contamination of soil and water sources, air pollution from improper waste disposal and health risks to workers
           and nearby communities due to exposure to hazardous materials.

8.                Stakeholders Engagement
The PMO Project Manager and EHS Manager (EHSM), PMC and EPC Contractor’s EHS Manager would engage with the
local community and other stakeholders to disseminate the plan for material & waste management and implementation
mechanism and take into cognizance their valued suggestions and feedback and implementing the Standard Operation
Procedures for transportation, handling & storage of Materials, as well as handling & disposal of Solid & Hazardous Waste
through TSDFs. The engagement with stakeholders would be undertaken at periodic intervals and monitoring results of
soil and groundwater at downstream of the temporary waste storages for solid as well as hazardous waste at sites, would
be communicated to them.



9.                Community Complaints and Grievance
                  Redressal Mechanism
The EPC would be required to implement mechanism to register complains of communities and external stakeholders
associated with waste transportation and disposal and address the same in line with the project-specific GRM. The details
of this process have been specified in the GRM section of this report including setting up of timeline for resolving any
such issues.



10.               Training and Capacity Development Programs
Training modules pertaining to handling, storage, transportation and safe disposal of solid and hazardous waste would
be developed by EPC, EHSS Manager in line with MoE’s EHS policy and impart trainings at periodic intervals to all the staff
and manpower involved in the construction activities and to the O&M staff during operation phase. Trainings would be
undertaken on a weekly basis to cover all the employees and construction workers of EPC.
The workforce would be educated on handling, storing, and transferring fuels and greases and other hazardous materials
along with training on steps to be taken in case of a spill, such as using drip pans or absorbent pads to catch spills and
reporting the spill as per hierarchy. Routine training programs would be organized to reinforce these practices.



11.               Implementation Budget
The indicative budget for implementation of mitigation measures adopted in this ESMP sub-plan for management of
borrow materials is given in Table 27.

                  P5: Muck and Spoil Management Plan (MSMP)

 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                                  Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                                    SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                                 11 August 2025                                             Page | 94
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




Significant quantities of muck and spoil would be generated during the construction of the proposed Kambarata 1 HPP
project structures, including the headrace tunnel, tailrace tunnel, powerhouse, RCC dam and associated facilities such as
the access roads and tunnels. As per the ESIA, the muck and spoil have the potential to impact land as well as surface
water quality, if not managed adequately in an environmentally sound manner, besides posing significant occupational
and community health and safety issues in the project and its impact areas. This Muck and Spoil Management Plan aims
to provide guidelines for implementing the management measures identified to alleviate the impacts of muck and spoil
generated, including implementation strategies of the management measures, including roles and responsibilities for
implementation, their budgetary allocations including both Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) and Operational Expenditure
(OPEX) along with formulation of a periodic monitoring mechanism to track the effectiveness of the management plan.
The EPC Contractor would make this plan ready after an estimate of muck & spoil has been developed and its
characteristics identified for disposal at designated sites to be selected based on the criteria set out in Section 5 of this
sub plan. This would be carried out before the EPC Contractor commences any activities related to drilling and tunnelling
for the Dam construction.



1.                Scope
The Muck and Spoil Management Plan (MSMP) outlines the strategies and measures for managing the muck generated
during the construction phase of Kambarata-1 HPP. It includes estimating the volume of muck, identifying potential reuse
opportunities, selecting appropriate disposal sites, and planning the transportation and dumping processes. The plan also
emphasizes site rehabilitation and compliance with environmental and safety regulations to minimize the impact on the
environment and ensure the safety of workers and nearby communities. By effectively managing muck, the plan aims to
maintain operational efficiency and uphold environmental standards throughout the project.
The Project Management Consultant (PMC) will update the Environmental, Social, Health, and Safety (ESHS)
requirements outlined in the Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA), Environmental and Social Management
Plans (ESMPs), and other ESHS plans, as deemed pertinent. These ESHS requirements will be integrated into the
Employer’s Requirements for the construction contracts for selected EPC Contractor to adhere.
Prior to commencement of the pre-construction and construction phases, the EPC Contractor will finalise and update the
MSMP framework for specific construction zones as part of the Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plan
(C-ESMP), which would be prepared, adopted and updated periodically by the Contractor as per the advice of PMO. Upon
completion of construction and the withdrawal of the EPC Contractor, the Project Proponent (through the PMO) will
assume responsibility for updating the MSMP in the form of an Operational Environmental and Social Management Plan
(OESMP), thereby ensuring that all mitigation and monitoring measures continue to be implemented effectively during
operation.
Both C-ESMP and OESMP documents associated with the MSMP will be subject to periodic review by PMC Engineer and
updates based on audit findings during project implementation.
This plan is applicable to the following impact areas.
     •     The construction site for the dam structures and its associated facilities including access roads and tunnels,
           quarries and borrow pits used as a source of raw materials for dam construction.
     •     The area inundated behind the dam (approximately 61.7 Sq. Km at a full supply level of 1,198 msl) forming the
           reservoir.
     •     Hauling route of vehicles for transporting muck from the source of generation to the designated disposal areas.



2.                Objectives
The objective of the MSMP during the construction phase of the Kambarata-1 Hydropower Plant includes:
     •     Environmental Protection: The primary objective is to safeguard the environment by ensuring that muck and
           spoil would be disposed of in a manner that prevents soil erosion, water contamination, and habitat disruption.
           This involves selecting disposal sites that are away from water bodies and implementing erosion control
           measures, and protection of flora and fauna, and their natural habitats.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                     Page | 95
P4: Material and Waste Management Plan (M&WMP)




     •     Site Rehabilitation: After muck disposal, the plan aims to restore the disposal sites to their natural or near-
           natural state. This includes re-vegetation with native plant species, soil stabilization techniques to prevent
           further erosion, and continuous monitoring to ensure the long-term stability and safety of the rehabilitated sites.
     •     Operational Efficiency: Efficient muck disposal is crucial for maintaining the construction schedule of the
           hydropower project. The plan outlines the logistics of muck transportation, including the use of appropriate
           vehicles and routes to minimize disruptions. It also ensures that disposal activities are coordinated with other
           construction activities to avoid delays.
     •     Safety Compliance: Ensuring the safety of workers and nearby communities is a key objective. The plan includes
           measures to mitigate risks associated with muck disposal, use of engineering control mechanisms to contain the
           scattering of muck, dust control, proper signage, and barriers around disposal sites. Compliance with local and
           international safety standards is emphasized to protect all stakeholders involved.
     •     Resource Utilization: The plan would also explore opportunities to maximize the reuse of excavated material
           within the project. This involves identifying potential uses for the muck, such as backfilling, construction of
           embankments, or other structural applications. By reusing muck, the project can reduce the need for additional
           raw materials, thereby lowering costs and minimizing environmental impact.
By focusing on these objectives, responsible and sustainable muck disposal plans will be ensured, minimizing its impact
on the environment and local communities.



3.                Roles and Responsibilities
This section provides an outline of key roles and responsibilities in the context of this MSMP. A description of the key
position and their responsibilities are provided in Table 28 and the relevant organisation structure for implementation
and supervision of this management plan is represented in Figure 8.

       Entity                       Role                                                                    Key Responsibilities
 Project                  PMO Project                     •     Planning and Coordination: The PM would monitor the implementation of a
 Management               Manager (PM)                          comprehensive muck disposal strategy and coordinate with stakeholders to
 Office                                                         ensure the plan is feasible and compliant with regulations.
 (PMO)/Project                                            •     Regulatory Compliance: Ensuring that all muck disposal activities comply with
 Proponent                                                      local, national, and international environmental regulations and standards.
                                                          •     Resource Management: Overseeing the allocation of resources, including
                                                                manpower, equipment, and materials, to ensure efficient muck disposal
                                                                operations.

                                                                of muck disposal activities, identifying any issues or deviations from the plan, and

5.                Context
The construction activities for the Kambarata-1 HPP, including excavation for the dam foundation, powerhouse
foundation, river water diversion tunnels, tunnel blasting for headrace tunnel (HRT) and tailrace tunnel (TRT) will
generate significant amounts of muck and spoil material. This includes fragmented rock, soil debris, and dust from large-
scale removal, controlled blasting, and drilling operations. Effective management of this material is crucial to minimize
environmental impacts, such as increased sedimentation load in the Naryn River. The excavated material will be either
utilized for rockfill and as aggregate for construction or will be dumped at designated muck disposal sites. The site-specific
muck reuse and disposal plan and selection of muck disposal sites would be undertaken by the EPC Contractor prior to
commencement of construction activities and duly approved by the PMO. The following points will be considered for
identification of the muck disposal sites:
     •     Proximity to Project Area: The dumping sites will be strategically chosen to be as close as possible to the
           hydropower project area. This will minimize the need for long-distance transport of muck, reducing
           transportation costs and environmental impact.
     •     Stability and Safety: The selected sites need to be free from any landslides or creep. Careful consideration will
           be given, to ensure that these sites do not pose risks of toe erosion or slope instability, thereby maintaining the
           structural integrity of the dumping areas. The land to be selected for muck disposal would ideally be in
           environmentally degraded areas, any abandoned quarry/mine areas, areas with low soil productivity having low
           visual impact and low health and safety risks to humans and animals.
     •     Absence of Water Channels: There will be no active channels or streams flowing through the dumping sites. This
           precaution will prevent the risk of water contamination and sedimentation in nearby water bodies.
     •     Distance from Settlements: The dumping sites will be located away from settlement areas. This measure will
           ensure that the disposal activities do not adversely affect local communities, maintaining a safe and healthy
           environment for residents.
However, in identifying the muck disposal location sites, following points must be considered and avoided:
     •     Areas where signs of potential erosion of soil are evident.
     •     Areas located within ~1km upstream from underground water intakes should be avoided.
     •     Areas located in the phreatic zone to be avoided for disposal of muck.
     •     Any areas within the natural habitat of threatened or endangered flora and fauna would be avoided.
     •     Any area within a 500-meter of the sites of archaeological or historical importance would be avoided for muck
           disposal.
     •     Disposal of Muck would be at least 500 m away from agricultural land, human settlements and sensitive
           environmental areas e.g. reservoir area, livestock grazing area etc.
     •     Flood-prone areas with recurrence intervals of less than 20 years, such as ravines, flat lowland plains, marshes,
           etc would be avoided.
A map showing the identified sites for disposal and storage of Muck and Spoil Material along with the No-go areas (e.g.
steep slopes, grasslands, shrublands, land slide prone areas, river water banks, roadsides, settlements etc.) would be
developed by the EPC Contractor on the basis of above-mentioned criteria. Any disposal of Muck and Spoil, based on
their specific characteristics at each of the identified sites shall be progressively monitored as recommended in Table 33
under Section 7.
The projected volume of muck estimated to be generated during construction phase, as per the Table 2-9 of AFRY’s FS
June 2025 Cost Estimate Report7 and given in Table 30 below. However, once the volume and disposal location of muck

6.                Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The summary of key impacts and risks of muck generation, mostly in the pre-construction and construction phases of the
Kambarata-1 Hydropower project, are depicted in the following sub-sections. No significant impacts are envisaged during
the operation phase as long as adequate mitigation measures for the impacts occurring in the pre-construction and
construction phases have been adopted.
The construction of the hydropower project involves numerous activities that may contribute to muck generation, each
with its own environmental impacts and risks, such as
     •     P5-1 (Generation of Muck during excavation work for Dam Foundation): The excavation and blasting work for
           the dam foundation of the Kambarata-1 HPP will generate significant amounts of muck. These include large-
           scale removal of rock and soil to create the foundation, controlled blasting to break up rock, and drilling
           operations for foundation preparation. The muck consists of fragmented rock, soil debris, and dust, which need
           to be managed effectively to minimize environmental impact. These mucks if not managed properly may impact
           the adjoining agricultural land, pastureland and its indiscriminate discharge into the water may increase
           sediment transport in the downstream and may result in soil erosion.
     •     P5-2 (Generation of Muck during foundation excavation of Powerhouse): The excavation work for the
           powerhouse foundation of the Kambarata-1 HPP will also generate substantial muck. These include large
           volumes of rock and soil removed to create the foundation, fragmented rock from controlled blasting, and rock
           cuttings and dust from drilling operations. Effective management of this muck is also crucial to minimize
           environmental impacts which is similar to the impacts of muck generation during excavation work for dam.
     •     P5-3 (Generation of Spoil material during construction of River Water Diversion Tunnels): Excavation,
           controlled blasting and tunnelling of HRT, TRT and Surge Shafts will produce large volumes of spoil material,
           which can lead to sedimentation in the Naryn River, if not managed properly. Also, the muck generated from
           these activities may result in erosion of unstable slopes especially in the fault lines.
     •     P5-4 (Transportation of Muck from the sites of generation to disposal location and disposal): The muck
           generated at construction zones both in the main project structures and for the associated facilities will have
           potential to get contaminated due to the potential presence of natural contaminants in the rocks and debris, or
           from blasting and excavation activities during use of chemicals/hazardous materials. This contaminated muck
           may find its way into the productive land or into the surface water bodies during transportation and
           indiscriminate and/or accidental disposal into the water bodies and soil surface impact surface water quality,
           soil and groundwater quality of the area.
     •     P5-5 (Disposal of Muck to the designated disposal locations): Disposal of muck can increase sediment transport
           downstream which may alter the riverbed morphology, water quality and aquatic habitat. Muck disposal sites,
           if not managed properly, can impact long term stability affecting the environment and local community.

 Operation Phase
 During the operation phase, significant impacts are not anticipated, provided that comprehensive mitigation measures have been effectively implemented during the pre-construction and construction
 phases. These measures are crucial in addressing potential contamination in effected environmental attributes, ensuring that any adverse effects are minimized or eliminated before the project
 becomes operational. However, it must be ensured that all muck and spoil disposal sites shall be properly restored and rehabilitated soon after completion of the construction. By proactively managing
 these impacts, the project can proceed smoothly, maintaining sustainability and operational efficiency throughout its lifecycle.

8.                Stakeholders Engagement
The PMO Project Manager and EHS Manager (EHSM), PMC and EPC Contractor’s EHS Manager would engage with the
local community and other stakeholders to disseminate the muck and spoil disposal plan and implementation mechanism
and take into cognizance their valued suggestions and feedback and implementing the Standard Operation Procedures
for Muck Handling, Storage and Disposal. The engagement with stakeholders would be undertaken at periodic intervals
and monitoring results of soil and groundwater downstream of the disposal sites would be communicated to them.



9.                Community Complaints and Grievance
                  Redressal Mechanism
The EPC Contractor would be required to implement mechanism to register complains of communities and external
stakeholders associated with muck transportation and disposal and address the same in line with the project-specific
GRM. The details of this process have been specified in the GRM section of this report including setting up of timeline for
resolving any such issues.



10.               Training and Capacity Development Programs
Training modules pertaining to handling, storage, reuse, transportation and safe disposal of muck and spoils would be
developed by EPC Contractors EHSS Manager in line with MoE’s EHS policy and impart trainings at periodic intervals to
all the staff and manpower involved in the construction activities. Trainings should be undertaken on a weekly basis to
cover all the employees and construction workers of EPC Contractor.



11.               Implementation Budget
The indicative budget for implementation of mitigation measures adopted in this ESMP sub-plan for management of
Muck and Spoil materials is given in Table 34.

P6: Site Restoration Management Plan (SRMP)




1.                   Scope
This Site Restoration Plan (SRP or the Plan) outlines the measures required to ensure that all temporary and disturbed
areas associated with the construction of the Kambarata-1 Hydropower Project (K1 HPP or the Project) are
decommissioned, stabilized, and restored in a manner that is safe, environmentally sound, and socially responsible.
The SRP is a critical component of the Project’s Environmental and Social Management Plan and supports compliance
with the World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESF), particularly:
• Environmental and Social Standard 1 (ESS1) (Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and
Impacts),
•      ESS3 (Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention), and
•      ESS6 (Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources).
The SRP specifically covers:
• The demolition and removal of temporary facilities such as construction camps, workshops, laydown areas,
batching plants, and other site infrastructure no longer required post-construction;
• Waste removal and disposal, including both non-hazardous and hazardous waste generated during construction
and closeout;
• Grading and stabilization of spoil areas, disturbed land, and excavation zones to reduce erosion risks and visual
impacts;
• Revegetation using native or adapted species to restore ecological function and reduce long-term environmental
degradation;
• Restoration of altered watercourses or drainage paths that were diverted, dammed, or otherwise modified for
construction;
•      Closure of sanitation infrastructure, such as septic tanks, soak pits, or temporary greywater systems;
• Immediate repair of damaged community infrastructure, including irrigation, drainage, and fencing where impacted
by construction activities.
This Plan is primarily focused on restoration activities during the pre-commissioning and construction closeout phases.
This is standard practice for large infrastructure projects, where the SRP is typically implemented toward the end of
construction. However, it is acknowledged that the K1 HPP will be constructed over a period of approximately seven
years, and in some areas, construction works will conclude much earlier. In such cases, site restoration in those specific
zones may be triggered earlier—on a rolling basis—and should still be carried out in accordance with this Plan. The
PMO, PMC Engineer and Contractor are responsible for identifying and scheduling these early restoration opportunities
through progressive rehabilitation.
Any unresolved environmental risks at the time of handover—such as incomplete vegetation cover or areas needing
long-term slope stabilization—will be transferred to the operator and addressed under the operational Environmental
and Social Management System (ESMS), as also outlined in the Project Commissioning and Construction Closeout
Management Plan (PCCCMP).
The SRP directly addresses the following types of environmental and social impacts:
• Visual impacts, such as landscape scarring, exposed spoil heaps, construction debris, or unnatural contours left after
demobilisation, which may degrade the natural aesthetics of the area and affect local perceptions of project quality;
• Safety risks, including open excavations, unstable slopes, unsecured pits, or leftover construction materials that
could pose hazards to workers, community members, or livestock accessing the area after construction.
•      Erosion and sedimentation impacts from bare or poorly stabilized slopes;
•      Long-term land degradation in areas where spoil was dumped or topsoil was removed;
•      Reduced ecological function due to loss of native vegetation or habitat fragmentation;



    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
    Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 114
P6: Site Restoration Management Plan (SRMP)




•      Pollution and health hazards due to residual waste, chemicals, or sanitation infrastructure;
• Community dissatisfaction or loss of access to previously impacted infrastructure (e.g., irrigation lines or pasture
fences);
•      Hydrological risks where construction temporarily altered drainage paths or river margins.
The implementation of the SRP will be led by the Contractor and overseen by the PMO’s environmental and social team.
It will be supported by monitoring, documentation by the PMC Engineer and independent audit to confirm that all
restoration objectives have been met prior to final site handover or transition to operations.



2. Objectives
The objectives of this SRP are to guide and ensure the environmentally sound and socially responsible restoration of all
areas temporarily disturbed during the construction of the Project, in line with applicable national regulations and World
Bank ESF requirements.
Specifically, the Plan aims to:
1.       Ensure safe decommissioning and removal of temporary construction facilities such as camps, batching plants,
         laydown areas, workshops, storage yards, and sanitation infrastructure, with proper disposal or reuse of
         demolition materials.
2.       Restore landform and stabilize slopes in disturbed areas to prevent erosion, landslides, or sedimentation that
         could affect downstream water quality or safety.
3.       Re-establish soil quality and surface cover through re-vegetation using native or locally adapted species, to
         promote biodiversity recovery and minimize long-term land degradation.
4.       Ensure proper waste management and pollution prevention by safely removing residual construction debris,
         hazardous materials, and contaminated soils.
5.       Rehabilitate altered drainage channels or watercourses, including diversion paths, so as to re-establish natural
         flow conditions and protect aquatic ecosystems.
6.       Promote early restoration in areas where construction concludes ahead of overall project completion, enabling
         progressive rehabilitation throughout the multi-year construction program.
7.       Support community satisfaction and land-use recovery by repairing impacted community infrastructure and
         returning lands used under temporary agreements to their original or agreed-upon condition.
8.       Lay the foundation for effective transition to operational environmental management, by ensuring that residual
         environmental risks (if any) are clearly identified and appropriately transferred to the operator’s Environmental
         and Social Management System (ESMS).
Through these objectives, the SRP contributes to the Project’s broader goals of environmental sustainability, responsible
resource use, and social license to operate, while ensuring compliance with national obligations and the environmental
and social requirements of international lenders.



3. Roles and Responsibilities
This section provides the delineation of roles and responsibilities of the personnel responsible for implementing the
overall ESMPs and specifically for the SRP. Successful implementation of the SRP requires clear delineation of roles
across the Project Management Office (PMO), Contractor, environmental and social specialists, and supporting technical
consultants. Responsibilities span planning, execution, supervision, and closeout verification.
The organization chart presented in Figure 9 below showcases the responsibilities for the implementation of the ESMPs
and overall Environment, Social, Health & Safety (ESHS) management of the Project. Table 35 lists out the roles and
responsibilities for Implementation of the SRP.

4. Governing Standards and Additional Guidance
The implementation of the Site Restoration Plan (SRP) for the K1 HPP must adhere to both national regulatory
frameworks and international environmental and social standards. The table below outlines the key Kyrgyz Republic
laws and regulations as well as applicable international standards that govern or provide guidance for the restoration
of disturbed sites, removal of temporary works, revegetation, erosion control, and safe handover of project-affected
areas.

Based on this, approximate areas requiring restoration can be derived using assumed surface footprints for excavation,
equipment laydown, spoil disposal, temporary camps, and access roads. The following land categories have been
identified for site restoration:
•       Spoil and muck disposal areas
•       Contractor and batching plant areas
•       Quarry and borrow pits
•       Laydown areas and material stockpiles
•       River diversion footprints
•       Temporary construction access roads
•       Worker camps and sanitation infrastructure zones
•       Watercourse crossings and reinstated channels
Based on this list, the activities likely to generate restoration needs – potentially leading to environmental and social risks
during preconstruction, construction and operation phases, are outlined below:

    Construction                   Spoil and muck dumping                                      Landscape alteration, instability
                                   Quarrying and borrow pit use                                Slope disturbance, drainage impacts
                                   Installation of temporary facilities                        Waste generation, soil contamination
                                   Watercourse diversion and river training                    Hydromorphological changes
                                   Fuel storage, chemical use                                  Risk of soil and water contamination
    Operation                      Decommissioning of temporary structures                     Residual waste and site scars
                                   Incomplete revegetation                                     Long-term erosion, dust generation

6. Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The construction and pre-commissioning phases of the K1 HPP will lead to significant land disturbance and temporary
infrastructure development, resulting in various environmental and social impacts. The SRP is designed to mitigate these
risks through timely and well-planned restoration interventions. Table 40: A summary of key impacts and risks.Table 40
below provides a summary of key impacts and risks.

                                                                                      fertility.
 Construction                   Erosion and sediment runoff as a result of soil       Disturbed slopes, unprotected spoil mounds, and
                                exposure on slopes and spoil heaps                    incomplete surface cover increase the risk of gullying
                                                                                      and sediment runoff into watercourses.
                                Landscape disfigurement through visual and            Spoil dumps, abandoned pits, and dismantled
                                ecological scarring                                   campsites can leave long-term scars unless restored to
                                                                                      acceptable contours and vegetative cover.
                                Contamination of sites as a result of residual        Improper removal of fuel storage sites,
                                hazardous or chemical substances                      greywater/sewage pits, or workshops can result in
                                                                                      localized contamination of soil and water.
                                Biodiversity impacts from the loss of habitat         Slow revegetation or inappropriate plant selection
                                and delayed regeneration                              may hinder natural habitat recovery, especially near
                                                                                      sensitive zones.
                                Obstructed or modified water flows                    Temporary river diversions, culverts, or altered
                                                                                      drainage paths may not revert to natural flow if not

7. Mitigation and Monitoring
This section outlines the specific mitigation measures and monitoring protocols required to address the environmental
and social risks identified in the SRP for the K1 HPP. The goal is to ensure that all disturbed sites are restored safely and
effectively, and that restoration efforts are measurable, verifiable, and aligned with good international industry practice
and World Bank ESF standards.

The FIDIC PMC Engineer will be responsible for oversight and independent verification of the management measures
outlined. The PMC Engineer will ensure that the Contractor’s and PMOs EHS managers are executing their roles in
alignment with FIDIC guidelines and regulatory requirements. Their role will primarily be focused on compliance
monitoring, quality assurance, and reporting, with a clear responsibility to address any non-compliance.

8. Community Complaints and Grievance Redressal
   Mechanism
The Grievance Redressal Mechanism (GRM) for this Project has been developed based on the Law of the Kyrgyz Republic
“On the procedure for considering citizens' appeals” dated May 4, 2007 No. 67 and the WB ESS10 “Stakeholder
Engagement and Information Disclosure” requirements. It will be a two-level process as given below:
• Level 1 - Local Level: At the village level (Aiyl Okmotu) local contact persons will receive/register grievances from
applicants and inform the K1-PMO. After consideration of grievances through discussion with the relevant K1-PMO staff
and local self-government authorities, response will be given to the complainant within 14 working days. If the
complainant is not satisfied and/or the issue cannot be resolved at the first level of the GRM, the complaint is escalated
to the second (central) level.
• Level 2 – Central Level: At the second level, the K1-PMO responsible staff will receive and register the complaint and
forward it to the K1-PMO Director. The K1-PMO director will assign the PMO staff responsible for resolution of the
complaint and along with other relevant staff, they will review the compliant and take a decision. A response will be
provided to the complainant in writing within 14 working days. Complaints requiring a collegial decision will be reviewed
by a GRM committee established by the Ministry of Energy.
The grievance can be filed in person through a form which will be available at a designated place in the Aiyl Okmotu, or
through a representative, by telephone, by text message, by email or through a website designated for grievance
redressal. All grievances filed will be officially entered in a register and all the further processes till the decision will be
recorded.
The GRM classifies grievances and appeals into 10 distinct categories. This classification system is used to register and
track grievances systematically and ensures that the complaint reaches the right person immediately to avoid delays in
the redressal process.
The details of the Grievance Redressal Mechanism is provided in the Document ‘Stakeholder Engagement Plan and
Grievance Redressal Mechanism’.



9. Training and Capacity Development Programs
Successful implementation of the SRP requires that all parties involved—particularly the Contractor, PMO staff, and field
personnel—possess the necessary technical knowledge and awareness to carry out restoration tasks to an acceptable
standard. This section outlines the key training and capacity development activities that will ensure environmental, health
and safety, and restoration objectives are met.
The trainings proposed here are targeted, role-specific, and designed to build both technical proficiency and institutional
readiness. They reflect the nature of the risks and responsibilities outlined in the SRP and draw from international good
practice as recommended under the World Bank’s ESF and Hydropower Sustainability Guidelines.


9.1 Objectives of the Training Program
The objectives below define what the SRP training program aims to achieve in supporting effective and safe restoration
across the Project. These outcomes will guide the design and delivery of all training sessions:
•       Ensure site personnel are familiar with SRP objectives, sequencing, and quality expectations;
• Build technical capacity in land restoration techniques including grading, topsoil management, and native species
revegetation;
•       Raise awareness about environmental protection, pollution prevention, and community safety during site closure;
•       Support field-based decision-making during progressive and final restoration;
•       Prepare the PMO to verify restoration activities and monitor compliance;



    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
    Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                     Page | 126
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



•       Enable transition to the operational ESMS with clear recordkeeping and performance documentation.


9.2 Key Training Topics and Rationale
The following training topics have been selected based on the specific roles, risks, and responsibilities outlined in the SRP.
Each is tailored to ensure the right people receive the right knowledge at the right time:
Table 43: Training topics for effective implementation of the SRP.

    Training Topic                              Target Audience                       Rationale / Justification
    General SRP Induction                       Contractor field teams, PMO           Ensures shared understanding of SRP goals,
                                                staff                                 implementation roles, and restoration sequencing.
    Topsoil Management and                      Contractor supervisors, field         Addresses technical tasks such as proper stripping, storage,
    Erosion Control                             workers                               reuse, and slope stabilization.
    Native Species Revegetation                 Contractor supervisors,               Builds capacity to achieve sustainable revegetation
    Techniques                                  environmental specialists             outcomes using local species.
    Waste Removal and Sanitation                EHS staff, site managers              Ensures environmentally sound removal of hazardous and
    Decommissioning                                                                   non-hazardous wastes, septic pits, etc.
    Drainage and Runoff                         Construction teams, PMO               Prepares teams to manage hydrological risks at restored
    Management                                  technical leads                       sites (e.g., blocked culverts, diversions).
    Progressive Restoration and                 Contractor’s planning and             Encourages forward planning to avoid piling up all
    Early Closeout Zones                        logistics team                        restoration at project end.

    Land Return                                 Officer                               site return or handover.

    Monitoring                                  monitors                              revegetation, erosion, and site safety.
    ESMS Handover Procedures                    PMO management, future                Prepares for formal documentation and tracking of any
                                                operator liaison                      residual restoration obligations post-commissioning.



9.3 Training Delivery
Trainings will be delivered through practical and role-specific methods, including workshops, toolbox talks, and field
demonstrations. The program will be led by PMO staff and Contractor teams, with support from technical specialists
where required. Delivery responsibilities will be based on topic relevance—e.g., topsoil and erosion control training by
environmental specialists, waste handling by the Contractor’s EHS Manager, and revegetation by a
biodiversity/restoration expert. Sessions on stakeholder engagement and handover to operations will be facilitated by
the PMO’s liaison and management teams.
Training will be timed to coincide with demobilisation and restoration schedules, with refresher sessions as needed. All
sessions will be documented, and attendance tracked to ensure accountability.



10. Implementation Budget
This section outlines the estimated budget required to implement the SRP for the K1 HPP. Table 44 provides a summary
of costs associated with physical restoration activities, monitoring, training, and closeout verification.


10.1Approach to Budgeting
The costing approach draws on quantity estimates from the Kambarata-1 Feasibility Study carried out by AFRY (Ministry
of Energy of the Kyrgyz Republic, Dam Options Report – August 2024, KAM-FS-DOR_001, Annex 4 – Cost Estimates) and
reflects local Kyrgyz site conditions, including terrain challenges, access limitations, and seasonal constraints.



    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                Client Reference No. 5018007
    Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                  SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                               11 August 2025                           Page | 127
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



Key assumptions and costing notes include:
• Areas requiring restoration were estimated based on excavation volumes, spoil placement zones, borrow pits, and
construction facilities.
• Unit rates are based on prevailing regional norms and past project experience in mountainous areas, adjusted for
limited access (including use of pack animals or helicopter lifts in some zones).
• Some costs—especially for environmental oversight and specialised training—are expressed as lump sums or as a %
of direct restoration costs.
• Costs associated with the operational phase (e.g. long-term erosion monitoring or vegetation maintenance) are not
duplicated here where they are expected to be included in the future operator’s Environmental and Social Management
System (ESMS). These are noted accordingly.
• Note: This estimate does not include operational-phase maintenance costs (e.g. long-term vegetation survival checks
or slope monitoring), which are assumed to fall under the scope of the future operator’s ESMS. A general rule of thumb is
that annual maintenance of restored areas may cost 1–2% of the initial restoration investment, or approximately
$20,000–40,000/year, depending on terrain and residual risk.
•       Disclaimer:
The budget presented above is based on the feasibility design stage, using the Bill of Quantities provided in the
Kambarata-1 Feasibility Study (AFRY, 2024). As such, it reflects the best available estimates at this early stage of design.
These figures are indicative only and should be treated as guidelines to inform preliminary planning and resource
allocation. The budget must be reviewed and updated as the detailed engineering design progresses and construction
methodologies and footprints are further refined.

11. Annex A – Demobilisation Schedule and Mapping
  Requirements
To guide the systematic closure and site restoration process, the Plan includes this sample demobilization schedule
outlining typical sequencing and timing of key decommissioning activities. While the exact schedule will be finalized by
the Contractor in coordination with the PMO and PMC Engineer during the final year of construction, this template
provides an indicative structure that all contractors will be required to customize.
In addition, schematic demobilization maps must be submitted by each contractor as part of their site-specific restoration
plan. These maps will clearly identify:
•       Locations of all temporary structures (e.g. worker camps, batching plants, spoil areas);
•       Areas to be dismantled, backfilled, or regraded;
•       Routes for material haulage and waste removal;
•       Safety fencing and exclusion zones;
•       Reinstatement zones for revegetation and erosion control.
•       These maps must be reviewed and approved by the PMO and PMC Engineer prior to initiation of site closure works.
