P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)




1.                Scope
This Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP or the Plan) sets out the framework for managing
occupational health and safety risks associated with the design, construction, and operation of the Kambarata-1
Hydropower Project (K1 HPP or the Project). The Plan applies to all direct impact areas, including the main construction
footprint, workers’ accommodation and welfare facilities, material storage and handling zones, and associated
infrastructure sites.
The objective of the OHSMP is to prevent, minimize, and manage occupational health and safety risks throughout the
project lifecycle. In line with World Bank Environmental and Social Standards and applicable national regulations, the
Plan outlines measures to protect worker health and safety, address hazards related to hazardous materials, services,
and construction activities, and ensure emergency preparedness and response.
The Plan also defines the transitional responsibilities across project phases. During pre-construction and construction,
the EPC Contractor will develop a Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) that includes detailed
OHS procedures tailored to specific work fronts and temporary facilities consistent with the project’s Labor Management
Procedures (LMP). Upon completion of construction, the Project Proponent, through the PMO, will update and
operationalize this OHSMP under the Operational Environmental and Social Management Plan (OESMP), ensuring
continuity in implementation, compliance, and improvement during the operational phase.
This Plan applies to the following direct impact areas:
     •     Pre-Construction and Construction Phase:
                 o     K1 HPP construction site including the dam, powerhouse, and ancillary facilities;
                 o     Associated facilities such as access roads, transmission lines, bridge crossings, quarry and borrow areas;
                 o     Workers’ camps, including accommodation, sanitation, and recreational facilities;
                 o     Materials storage yards and hazardous materials handling zones;
                 o     Construction base and plant installations.
     •     Operational Phase:
                 o     Inundated reservoir area (~61.7 km² at FSL 1,198 masl);
                 o     Operational dam and powerhouse facility including access infrastructure;
                 o     Operational footprint of associated facilities such as roads and transmission corridors.



2. Objectives
The objective of the OHSMP for the Kambarata-1 HPP is to:
     •     Preventing injuries and accidents:
                 o     Risk assessment and development of measures to reduce them.
                 o     Ensuring safe working conditions (tools, equipment, workplace).
                 o     Conducting briefings and training on occupational health and safety.
                 o     Use of personal protective equipment (PPE).
     •     Compliance with regulatory requirements and legislation:
                 o     Studying and applying relevant laws and regulations.
                 o     Conducting regular inspections and audits.
                 o     Timely elimination of identified violations.
     •     Creating a safety culture:



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                         Page | 132
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o     Involvement of all project participants in the process of ensuring health and safety.
                 o     Encouragement of safe behavior.
                 o     Regular discussion of health and safety issues at meetings and conferences.
     •     Ensure labor and working conditions:
                 o     Promote fair treatment, non-discrimination and equal opportunity.
                 o     Project , contracted, and primary supply workers, including vulnerable workers such as women, persons
                       with disabilities, children and migrant workers.
                 o     Prevent the use of all forms of forced labor.
                 o     Provide workers the opportunity to raise through accessible means workplace concerns.
     •     Continuous improvement of the Occupational Health and Safety system:
                 o     Analysis of incidents and accidents.
                 o     Development and implementation of new measures to improve safety.
                 o     Continuous improvement of the qualifications of specialists in health and safety.

                                                                           enhance its safety record with its stakeholders and workers.



5. Context
The construction and operation of the Kambarata-1 HPP will entail various activities that may have adverse effects on
occupational health and safety in terms of health problems due to air and water pollution, heavy physical work, lifting
and moving heavy objects, working at heights, risk of falls, noise, vibration, temperature changes, chemical factors,
exposure to dust (silica dust), solvents, paints and other chemicals, biological factors, risk of contracting infectious
diseases (e.g. hepatitis), ergonomic factors, awkward postures, repetitive movements, insufficient lighting, psychosocial
factors. High stress levels, tight deadlines, road safety due to increased traffic volumes, etc. Table 48 presents the
activities that may have an impact on occupational health and safety.
Table 48: Project activities leading to impacts on occupational health and safety

 Project phases          Project activities
 Construction            Tunnelling Work: The activities during excavation to establish TRT, HRT may impact occupational health and
 phase                   safety through various risks such as poor air quality, inadequate lighting, difficult access for emergency
                         evacuations, ground instability, fire hazards, high noise levels, psychological stress, and the need for
                         continuous training. Additionally, blasting and explosions pose significant dangers, requiring strict control
                         measures to prevent accidents and ensure worker safety.
                         Installation of Coffer dam and construction inside Coffer dam: During construction of Coffer dam, workers
                         will be at certain risks such as exposure to contaminated water, confined spaces, high noise and vibration
                         levels, electrical hazards and potential flooding.
                         Construction of RCC Dam: Workers may face exposure to dust, chemicals and concrete, high noise levels and
                         vibration from heavy machinery. Confined spaces and working at heights increase the risk of falls and
                         accidents. Additionally, the handling of heavy materials and equipment poses physical strain and injury risks,
                         including collisions with moving machinery.
                         Quarry and Crushing Operations: These operations may impact occupational health and safety through
                         several risks. Workers may face exposure to dust, noise, and vibration, which can lead to respiratory issues
                         like silicosis, hearing loss and musculoskeletal disorders. Blasting operations pose significant dangers,
                         including injury from flying material and potential explosions. Additionally, handling heavy materials and
                         working near moving equipment increase the risk of collisions and injuries.
                         Hazardous Operations in Workshops and Plant sites: Workers are often exposed to hazardous chemicals,
                         which can lead to respiratory issues, skin conditions, and long-term health problems. The use of heavy
                         machinery and equipment in workshops may increase the risk of injuries from collisions and moving parts.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                 Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                   SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                11 August 2025                             Page | 138
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



 Project phases          Project activities
                         Additionally, the risk of fires and exposure to toxic substances requires stringent safety protocols and
                         continuous monitoring.
                         Construction of Transmission line/s: Workers face exposure to high voltage, which can lead to electrical
                         hazards and electrocution. Working at heights and in confined spaces poses significant fall and accident risks.
                         Additionally, the use of heavy machinery and equipment increases the risk of injuries from collisions and
                         moving parts.
                         Access road and bridge construction: Workers may face exposure to dust from stockpiles, which can lead to
                         respiratory issues like silicosis. Handling bitumen poses risks of burns and inhalation of toxic fumes, leading to
                         respiratory problems and skin irritation.
 Operation               Operation & Maintenance of Powerhouse including turbines: Workers may face exposure to high voltage,
 phase                   which can lead to electrical hazards and electrocution. Additionally, handling hazardous materials and
                         ensuring proper ventilation are crucial to prevent respiratory issues and chemical exposure.
                         Repairing of associated structures of Dam: The use of heavy machinery and equipment during repairing and
                         rehabilitation work may increase the risk of injuries from collisions and moving parts. Workers may face
                         exposure to dust, chemicals, and high noise levels, which can lead to respiratory issues, skin conditions, and
                         hearing loss.
                         Operation & Maintenance of Transmission Line: Workers may face exposure to high voltage, which can lead
                         to electrical hazards and electrocution. Cable replacements may also result in high noise generation from
                         winching activities, which may result in workers getting exposed to such noise.


All of the above activities may have impacts on occupational health and safety, which are discussed in the following
sections.
The OHSMP is linked to several other management plans/sub-plans with interrelated impacts and mitigation plans. The
interrelated sub-plans are summarized in the table below.



Table 49: Links to other management plans

6. Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The summary of the main health and safety impacts and risks of the workers in line with the provisions under clause 24.1
to 30.3 of WBG ESS2 (Labour and working condition) requirements at various stages of the Kambarata-1 HPP is presented
in the following subsections:


6.1 Pre-construction Phase
Ensuring the safety of workers before construction activities commence, in the pre-construction phase is a crucial step in
the project implementation. The impacts that may arise from different project activities are illustrated below,
     •     P7-1: Unknown health status of workers that can exacerbate all risks: The unknown health status of workers
           can significantly exacerbate risks. Without accurate health information, it becomes challenging to identify and
           mitigate potential hazards effectively. This uncertainty can lead to increased vulnerability to workplace
           accidents, illnesses, and overall reduced productivity.
           The presence of radioactivity in surface water has been reported in 2014 FS study and other secondary literature,
           which has been mentioned in section 8.6.3.4.2. Hence, the EPC Contractor would confirm the presence of
           radioactivity consistently over a period of time before commencing construction to avoid any health risks to the
           construction workforce.
     •     P7-2: Improper Planning of Site Clearance and site preparation activities: Without a well-structured plan, there
           is a higher likelihood of overlooking critical safety protocols, which can result in increased workplace accidents
           and health issues. Additionally, inadequate planning can cause confusion among workers, reduce efficiency, and
           hinder the timely response to emergencies.
     •     P7-3: Inappropriate Personnel on site: When workers lack the necessary skills, training, or qualifications, they
           are more likely to make errors that can lead to accidents, injuries, and even fatalities. Additionally, unqualified
           personnel may not adhere to safety protocols, increasing the risk of hazardous incidents.
     •     P7-4: Improper Equipment Deployment: When equipment is not correctly installed, maintained, or used, it can
           lead to malfunctions, accidents, and injuries. This not only puts workers at risk but also disrupts operations and
           reduces productivity.
     •     P7-5: Missing records: Without accurate and complete records, it becomes difficult to track incidents, monitor
           compliance, and identify areas needing improvement. This lack of documentation can lead to repeated mistakes,
           unaddressed hazards, and potential legal issues.


6.2 Construction Phase
Construction activities such as site preparation, excavation, concreting, operation of construction machinery and
equipment, vehicle traffic and temporary accommodation of workers pose potential risks to the health, safety, security



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1            Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                              SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                           11 August 2025                            Page | 140
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



and welfare of construction workers. The impacts on health and safety of workers during various project activities are
illustrated below,
     •     P7-6: Impact during Tunnelling works: The activities during excavation to construct TRT, HRT may impact
           occupational health and safety through various risks such as,
                 o     Injury or Reduced Visibility from Blasting: Blasting activities can cause flying debris and dust, leading to
                       potential injuries and reduced visibility, which can further increase the risk of accidents.
                 o     Collision with Moving Machinery: The presence of heavy machinery in confined spaces increases the
                       risk of collisions, which can result in serious injuries to workers.
                 o     Weak/Unstable/Collapsing Ground or Rocks: Excavation can destabilize the ground or rocks, leading to
                       collapses that pose significant risks to the safety of workers and equipment.
                 o     Fire/Smoke: The use of explosives and machinery can ignite fires, producing smoke that can cause
                       respiratory issues and hinder evacuation efforts.
                 o     Falling Objects and Material: During tunnelling, loose rocks and materials can fall, posing a threat to
                       workers below and potentially causing severe injuries.
                 o     Air Pollution/Dust: Excavation generates dust and other pollutants, which can lead to respiratory
                       problems and other health issues for workers.
                 o     Noise and Vibration: The use of heavy machinery and blasting creates high levels of noise and vibration,
                       which can cause hearing loss and other health problems over time.
                 o     Water and Mudflow: Tunnelling can intersect with underground water sources, leading to water ingress
                       and mudflows that can create hazardous working conditions.
                 o     Failure of Communication Systems: Inadequate communication systems can lead to delays in
                       emergency response and coordination, increasing the risk of accidents and injuries.
     •     P7-7: Impact during installation of Coffer dam and construction inside Coffer dam: During construction of
           Coffer dam, workers will be at certain risks such as,
                 o     Improper/Unsafe Conditions of Riverbed: The riverbed may have uneven terrain, hidden obstacles, or
                       unstable areas that can pose significant risks to workers, including trips, falls, and equipment
                       malfunctions.
                 o     Washing Away or Drowning of Personnel During Work Over Water: Working over water increases the
                       risk of personnel being washed away or drowning, especially during unexpected water surges or strong
                       currents.
                 o     Injury Due to Fall from Height: Construction activities often involve working at heights, which can lead
                       to serious injuries if proper fall protection measures are not in place.
                 o     Exposure to Gases/Fumes in Confined Spaces: Confined spaces within the cofferdam can accumulate
                       harmful gases and fumes, posing respiratory hazards and potential poisoning to workers.
                 o     Exposure to Noise and Vibration: The use of heavy machinery and construction activities generates high
                       levels of noise and vibration, which can lead to hearing loss and other health issues over time.
                 o     Electrical Shock Hazard: Electrical equipment used in construction can pose shock hazards, especially
                       in wet conditions, increasing the risk of electrocution.
                 o     Risk of Flooding Should the Cofferdam Fail: If the cofferdam fails, it can lead to sudden flooding,
                       endangering workers and equipment, and causing significant disruptions to the construction process.
                 o     Failure of Communication Systems: Inadequate communication systems can hinder coordination and
                       emergency response, increasing the risk of accidents and injuries during construction activities.
     •     P7-8: Impact during construction of RCC Dam: Various occupational health concerns may arise during
           construction activities of RCC Dam such as,
                 o     Exposure to Concrete: Workers handling concrete are at risk of skin irritation, chemical burns, and
                       respiratory issues due to prolonged contact with wet concrete and inhalation of dust and fumes.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1    Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                      SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                   11 August 2025                          Page | 141
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o     Injury from Collision with Moving Machinery: The presence of heavy machinery on construction sites
                       increases the risk of collisions, which can result in severe injuries.
                 o     Injury Due to Fall from Heights: Construction activities often involve working at elevated heights, which
                       can lead to serious injuries if proper fall protection measures, such as harnesses and guardrails, are not
                       in place.
     •     P7-9: Impact from Quarry and Crushing Operations: During procurement of raw materials from quarry site and
           crushing operations may impact OHS of workers in various ways such as,
                 o     Blasting: Blasting operations can generate flying debris and shockwaves, posing significant risks of
                       injury to workers.
                 o     Excess Air/Dust Pollution/Silicosis: Quarrying and crushing activities produce large amounts of dust,
                       which can lead to respiratory issues such as silicosis.
                 o     Exposure to Noise and Vibration: The use of heavy machinery and blasting operations generates high
                       levels of noise and vibration, which can cause hearing loss and other health issues over time.
                 o     Injury from Flying Material or Moving Equipment: Workers are at risk of being struck by flying debris or
                       moving machinery, which can result in serious injuries.
     •     P7-10: Impact form Hazardous Operations in Workshops and Plant sites: The impacts from various hazardous
           operations in workshops and plant sites are as follows,
                 o     Explosion or fire: Workshops and plant sites often handle flammable materials and operate machinery
                       that can ignite fires or cause explosions. These incidents can result in severe injuries, fatalities, and
                       extensive property damage.
                 o     Injury due to Fall from heights: Working at elevated heights in workshops and plant sites poses
                       significant risks of falls, which can lead to serious injuries or fatalities.
     •     P7-11: Impact from Construction of Transmission line/s: During construction of Transmission Line, various OHS
           concerns may arise such as,
                 o     Injury due to Fall from heights: Working on transmission lines often involves operating at significant
                       heights, which increases the risk of falls.
                 o     Injury from Collision with Moving Machinery: The use of heavy machinery in transmission line
                       construction can lead to collisions, resulting in severe injuries.
                 o     Exposure to concrete: Handling concrete can cause skin irritation, chemical burns, and respiratory issues
                       due to dust and fumes.
                 o     Damage due to Explosion or fire: The presence of flammable materials and electrical equipment
                       increases the risk of explosions or fires.
                 o     Electric shock: Working with electrical transmission lines poses a high risk of electric shock or
                       electrocution.
     •     P7-12: Impact during Access Road and bridge construction: Various OHS concerns may arise during construction
           of access road and bridge. These are as follows,
                 o     Dust from stockpiles: Stockpiles of construction materials can generate significant amounts of dust,
                       which can lead to respiratory issues and other health problems for workers.
                 o     Burn or fume inhalation during handling of bitumen: Handling hot bitumen can expose workers to burns
                       and inhalation of hazardous fumes, which can cause respiratory issues and other health problems.
                 o     Injury from Collision with Moving Machinery: The presence of heavy machinery on construction sites
                       increases the risk of collisions, which can result in severe injuries.
                 o     Injury due to Fall from heights: Construction activities often involve working at elevated heights, which
                       can lead to serious injuries if proper fall protection measures, such as harnesses and guardrails, are not
                       in place.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                         Page | 142
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)




6.3 Operation Phase
The Operation and Maintenance phase includes the maintenance of the HPP and associated site offices.
     •     P7-13: Impact from maintenance work at powerhouse including turbines: Maintenance work at a powerhouse
           involves several occupational health and safety concerns, including electrical hazards from high-voltage
           equipment, exposure to hazardous substances like oils and chemicals, and risks associated with confined spaces
           such as toxic gases and oxygen deficiency. Workers may also face mechanical hazards from heavy machinery,
           fire and explosion risks due to flammable materials, and ergonomic issues from repetitive motions and heavy
           lifting.
     •     P7-14: Impact from repairing work of associated structures of Dam: Exposure to hazardous substances like oils,
           chemicals can lead to health issues. Mechanical hazards from heavy machinery can cause entanglement and
           crushing injuries.
     •     P7-15: Impact from operation and maintenance work of Transmission Line: Workers may encounter significant
           risks when exposed to high voltage, which can lead to severe electrical hazards, including the possibility of
           electrocution. This exposure can occur during various tasks, such as maintenance or repair work on electrical
           systems. The dangers associated with high voltage include electrical burns, cardiac arrest, and other serious
           injuries that can be life-threatening. Additionally, the process of replacing cables often involves the use of
           winching equipment, which can generate substantial noise levels. Prolonged exposure to loud noise can also
           cause stress and fatigue, further impacting workers' overall health and safety.



7. Mitigation and Monitoring
This section addresses the measures that need to be adopted to mitigate workers’ adverse health and safety impacts
identified because of the project implementation. The monitoring mechanisms that need to be implemented would be
aligned to the provisions under clause 24.1 to 30.3 of WBG ESS2 (Labour and working condition) requirements and are
described in this section.


7.1 Mitigation
The mitigation measures proposed to mitigate the identified occupational health and safety impacts are summarized
below.
     •     Mitigation of occupational health and safety risks to the workforce
                 o     The Contractor shall prepare and implement an occupational health and safety plan (covering
                       communicable diseases including COVID-19 prevention) as part of the OHSMP.
                 o     The contractor shall assign qualified and experienced staff to the construction site.
                 o     The contractor shall conduct a workplace hazard analysis for each type of construction activity, which
                       will result in specific training activities for workers.
                 o     The contractor shall conduct Process Hazard Analysis before undertaking any activities having high
                       health and safety risk potential (e.g., blasting activities).
                 o     The Contractor shall conduct regular training on health and safety aspects. The Contractor shall train
                       all construction workers in (a) the maintenance of equipment and machinery, (b) the specific hazards
                       of their work, (c) first aid, (d) basic sanitation and health, and (e) environmental protection. The training
                       will be conducted prior to the commencement of any subproject activities.
                 o     The Contractor shall display all health and safety information at each construction sites.
                 o     Provision of drinking water in line with the standards set out in Kyrgyz Republic’s Regulation on drinking
                       water quality. Installation of RO Plant for supplying of drinking water to Workforce Camp
                 o     The Contractor shall regularly test workers for infectious diseases, including sexually transmitted
                       infections.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1     Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                       SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                    11 August 2025                          Page | 143
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o     The Contractor shall ensure that appropriate PPE is provided to all workers and visitors, and that PPE is
                       used properly. No worker (or even visitor) will be allowed onto the site without the required PPE
                       (earplugs, safety shoes, helmets, masks, glasses, etc.).
                 o     The Contractor shall ensure that firefighting equipment is available at each construction site, in camps
                       and especially near fuel storage facilities.
                 o     Contractor drivers shall maintain low speeds when driving through/near populated areas.
                 o     The Contractor shall ensure that all equipment and machinery provided are safe and reliable for use by
                       workers. All electrical equipment shall be tested before use to ensure that it is fit for its intended
                       purpose. Persons handling electrical equipment shall be thoroughly instructed on all possible hazards
                       associated with the equipment. The Contractor shall ensure that equipment and containers are
                       properly labelled at construction and storage locations.
                 o     The Contractor shall provide his workers with first aid equipment. First aid equipment, including an
                       adequate supply of sterile dressings and cotton wool, shall be kept in an easily accessible place at each
                       work plant. First aid facilities shall be provided at work sites and in construction camps. The Contractor
                       shall engage a qualified first aid specialist(s). The location and telephone numbers of the nearest
                       hospital shall be posted at appropriate places at work sites and in construction camps.
                 o     The project and its contractors shall not use child or forced labor.
                 o     The contractor shall maintain an emergency management and reporting system.
                 o     Drivers and operators of vehicles and material handling equipment must have a medical certificate,
                       undergo training and testing, and be of a minimum age as required by state rules and regulations.
                 o     The Contractor would make arrangements to have an ambulance available onsite for 24x7 hours and
                       tie-up with the nearest Hospital (e.g., Kara-Jygach village hospital located approx. 15 km west of K1 HPP
                       and Toktogul District Hospital located approx. 40km west of K1 dam) throughout the construction phase
                       to ensure that any worker and staff of PMO, PMC Consultant and EPC Contractor requiring medical
                       attention (which cannot be serviced by First-aid camp) receive the same at the earliest possible time. A
                       paramedic and two (02) health workers would be deployed at the Occupational Health Care Centre to
                       attain any ailing or injured workers at preliminary level. The above-mentioned hospitals would be
                       audited prior to commencement of construction activities by the EPC Contractor to determine the
                       adequacy of infrastructure of these facilities and essentially upgrade to ensure proper treatment of the
                       workforce for K1 HPP project.
                 o     The contractor shall provide adequate sanitary conditions for workers through provisions of adequate
                       toilets/urinals and drinking water facilities at site. Wastewater and household waste will be treated and
                       recycled for non-potable uses at site. The contractor shall provide separate toilets and bathing areas
                       for men and women. At work zones, adequate mobile toilets/bio toilets would be provided for effective
                       management of wastewater.
                 o     The Contractor shall provide adequate security personnel and fencing to prevent unauthorized entry
                       into the facilities and campus area.
                 o     An emergency management plan shall be prepared to respond to all foreseeable emergencies, including
                       health incidents, hazardous waste spills, fires and other emergencies, and shall provide for consultation
                       with emergency services, the public, workers and other interested parties as part of the plan.
                 o     Provide training, discussions and briefings to workers on safety precautions and on responsibility for
                       their own safety and the safety of others.
                 o     Machine operators shall be suitably qualified, hold a driving license and be over 18 years of age. This
                       shall be regularly monitored by the contractor's Health and Safety Officer.
                 o     Organizing regular safety checks of vehicles and materials and assigning responsibility for carrying out
                       checks.
                 o     Ensuring control and execution of material extraction operations by trained and experienced personnel.
                 o     Developing procedures and providing instructions for emergency evacuation, as well as providing a list
                       of 24-hour emergency contacts.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1    Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                      SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                   11 August 2025                        Page | 144
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o     Conducting trainings to raise awareness about HIV/AIDS and STIs and encouraging voluntary and
                       confidential testing for HIV and STIs.
                 o     All workers to be engaged must sign a Code of Conduct prepared by the PMO, which will form part of
                       their contract and outline their rights and responsibilities.
                 o     Contractors shall be required to record wages of various levels, skilled/professional/unskilled workers,
                       subject to monitoring. They shall be required to pay at least the minimum wage or higher depending
                       on the position and qualifications.
                 o     The contractor shall establish a sexual harassment policy and an appropriate, impartial complaint and
                       investigation system.
                 o     Work permits to be issued for all hot works and cold works, and any other work having potential health
                       and safety risks like working at height, confined space entries, scaffolding etc. Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
                       would be undertaken prior to commencement of any construction activities.
                 o     The project has a “no child labor” policy. Contractors shall maintain records of workers, including age
                       verified by ID cards to ensure that child labor is not used. This includes maintaining records from
                       subcontractors.
                 o     The contractor shall draw up a contract with the workers for their employment and shall also keep a
                       full and accurate record of the employment of the workforce on the site. The records shall include
                       names, ages, genders, hours worked and wages earned.
                 o     The contractor must provide complete documentation on health and safety (for example: an
                       introductory briefing log, a health and safety log at the workplace, and must also maintain:
                             ▪     Introductory. Conducted with each new worker before starting work. It tells about the basic
                                   safety rules at the enterprise and first aid measures.
                             ▪     Primary. Familiarizes the employee with the specific working conditions of his workplace, with
                                   special hazards and ways to prevent them.
                             ▪     Repeat. Conducted periodically (usually once every six months) to repeat and update the
                                   knowledge of employees.
                             ▪     Unscheduled. Necessary in the event of a change in technology, equipment or after an
                                   accident at work.
                             ▪     Targeted. Conducted before performing certain types of work, especially those associated
                                   with special risks.
                             ▪     Briefings are the first line of defense. They help maintain worker awareness of risks and rules
                                   of safe behavior.
                 o     All work on site in hazardous or harmful locations must be carried out in accordance with the work
                       permit, which would be issued under the supervision of PMC Engineer.
                 o     The hazardous material and waste storage area would display Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS) for
                       all chemicals in both English and local language.
                 o     The contractor must develop a code of conduct for workers and familiarize workers with it during
                       training.
                 o     The contractor must provide workers with sufficient accommodation to avoid the need to live with the
                       local community. Accommodation for the workforce and resident staff must comply with the provisions
                       of EBRD/IFC Worker’s Accommodation: Procedures and Standards 2009.
                 o     All persons living in the local community must be transported to and from the site daily by the
                       contractor's transport in accordance with the worker movement plan. Transportation of migrant
                       workers must also be arranged to avoid the use of public transport.
                 o     Sexual exploitation, violence and harassment will not be tolerated. The contractor must work with
                       managers, local women's committees and district authorities to ensure that all complaints are dealt
                       with in accordance with the law.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1     Client Reference No. 5018007

 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                    11 August 2025                        Page | 145

                 o     Conduct pre-employment medical examinations of all workers, including screening for communicable
                       diseases such as tuberculosis, sexually transmitted diseases (STDs), etc.
                 o     Conduct regular medical examinations of workers to ensure that they are not carriers of any
                       communicable diseases.
                 o     Hunting/catching/fishing wild and/or domestic animals is prohibited for all project personnel.
                 o     Deployment of appropriate law enforcement and community engagement strategies to prevent crime
                       and social disorder. Implementation of measures such as security personnel, community policing and
                       conflict resolution programmes.
                 o     All the workforces would be provided with trainings on good ergonomic practices.
                 o     At the construction sites, adequate HSE professionals would be deployed to ensure that the work is
                       undertaken safely.
                 o     A formal complaints mechanism will be established to allow community members to report any issues
                       or concerns related to the project. This mechanism will ensure prompt and transparent resolution of
                       complaints and will be reviewed regularly by the project management team. This will be integrated
                       within the GRM at project level.
                 o     Alternative sources of potable water for the workforce such as groundwater sources or springs in the
                       adjoining would be identified by the EPC contractor for ensuring proper quality free of any radioactivity.
                       The EPC would also be required to carry out blood tests for radioactivity pre-employment of the
                       construction workforce and consequent regular testing for workers who come in direct contact with
                       the river water. If radioactivity levels are found to be alarming based on the health checkups of the
                       workers, then the specific sources would be found out by engaging expert agencies from Kyrgyz
                       Republic and material containing radioactive substances (considering absence of any natural sources of
                       radioactive emissions), need to be suitably collected and disposed to reduce such radioactivity
                       emissions from such sources. The monitoring measures and blood test for workforce in regard to
                       radioactivity has been discussed in detail in ESMP Sub Plan 03: WQRMP.
     •     Mitigating the effects of increased road traffic
                 o     Maintain roads in good condition and impose a speed limit of 20 km/h at intersections in built-up areas.
                       Before construction begins, identify “black zones” where the risk of accidents is higher.
                 o     Install speed limiters, road signs, street lighting at major intersections and traffic lights at busy
                       intersections near the site, as congestion is expected at these locations.
                 o     Engage traffic wardens in built-up areas near the site to ensure proper traffic management.
                 o     Train all drivers in safe driving rules and techniques.
                 o     Ensure compliance with axle load limits depending on the type of road.
                 o     Regular vehicle maintenance to reduce pollution and install appropriate safety equipment (mobile
                       phone, first aid kit, fire extinguisher, automatic reversing alarm, etc.). All vehicles must have “Pollution
                       Under Control” certificates.
                 o     Plan the movement of vehicles and equipment (in columns if necessary) taking into account the daily
                       routine of the population living along the roads.
                 o     Inform the public of anticipated vehicle movements.
                 o     Avoid peak hours when transporting hazardous materials to minimize associated risks.
                 o     Schedule large vehicles transporting plant equipment to avoid congestion and inspect roads before
                       such movements.
                 o     Sprinkle water along highways in populated areas to suppress dust.
     •     Mitigation of the consequences of construction works
                 o     The EPC contractor shall ensure that all areas where construction work is taking place are properly
                       marked with signs.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1    Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                      SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                   11 August 2025                          Page | 146
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o     Adequately deploy security personnel to restrict unauthorized persons from entering active
                       construction areas.
                 o     Implement a code of conduct for workers, including restricting workers to designated areas, prohibiting
                       littering, lighting fires, trespassing, and prohibiting residence on construction sites.
                 o     Eliminate hazardous conditions on construction sites that cannot be effectively controlled by site access
                       restrictions, such as closing openings to small confined spaces, providing escape routes through larger
                       openings such as trenches or excavations, or fencing off hazardous materials.
                 o     The contractor must observe safety when working in confined spaces by using a multi gas-meter to
                       check for any toxic gases prior to commencing work, ensuring conditions that may result in ignition or
                       explosion are identified and mitigated, and utilizing adequate safety equipment such as respirators
                       when oxygen levels are assessed to be low.
                 o     Vector containment measures shall be taken at work sites and work camps to ensure that they do not
                       spread to the local community.
                 o     Contractors shall have their own Emergency Response Plans consistent with the Project's Overall
                       Emergency Response Plan for risks specific to their type of work and area.

& reporting plan has been described in detail in Table 50 and Table 51 respectively.

P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)




8. Complaints and Grievance Redressal Mechanism
The EPC Contractor would be required to implement mechanism to register complains of communities and external
stakeholders associated Occupational Health and Safety Risks to the workforce and address the same in line with the
project-specific GRM. The detailed process to address complaints and grievance redressal from workers has been shown
below,
     •     Reporting Mechanisms:
                 o     Grievance Boxes: Suggestion boxes will be placed in accessible locations to allow workers to submit
                       anonymous complaints and feedback.
                 o     Open-door Policy: An open-door policy will be encouraged to foster open communication with senior
                       management.
                 o     Hotline Numbers: Dedicated phone lines will be provided for confidential grievance reporting.
     •     Investigation and Resolution:
                 o     Immediate Supervisor or HR: Concerns will be reported to immediate supervisors or the HR department
                       for prompt attention.
                 o     Formal Grievance Process: A structured process will be followed, including investigation, decision-
                       making, and an appeal procedure.
                 o     Mediation and Conciliation: Resolution will be facilitated through dialogue and negotiation.
     •     Monitoring and Follow-up:
                 o     Periodic Surveys: Regular opinion surveys will be conducted to gauge workplace satisfaction and
                       identify areas for improvement.
                 o     Exit Interviews: Feedback will be collected from departing employees to improve policies and practices.
     •     Confidentiality and Non-Retaliation:
                 o     Confidential Reporting: The identity of the complainant will be protected, maintaining confidentiality
                       unless legally required to disclose.
                 o     Anonymous Complaints: Anonymous reporting will be allowed, ensuring concerns are given due
                       consideration.
                 o     Non-Retaliation Policy: Employees will be protected from retaliation for reporting grievances.
     •     Support Services:
                 o     Social Worker or Designated Person: A professional will be appointed to handle cases of sexual
                       exploitation, abuse, and harassment.
                 o     Warm Referrals: Referrals to service providers will be offered to support individuals affected by
                       incidents.



9. Training and Capacity Development Programs
Arrangements will be made to provide training on the risks and impacts on the health and safety of workers in connection
with the K1 HPP project. Training will be provided to all workers and it will be ensured that contractors also provide
similar training to their own OHS personnel. Refresher courses will also be provided periodically and as required. The
training will cover relevant aspects such as information on the prevention of infectious diseases and sexually transmitted
infections, prohibition of sexual exploitation, violence and harassment, respect for the privacy of workers, especially
women. Also, training, talks with workers and briefings on safety procedures and on responsibility for their own safety
and the safety of others will be provided.
Some of the planned trainings are listed below.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                       Page | 161
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



Worker training: The Contractor shall not allow the Contractor's and Subcontractor's employees, as well as drivers and
forwarders of vehicles delivering materials and/or equipment to the construction sites, to enter the construction or any
other production sites until they have undergone mandatory training in accordance with applicable laws and regulations,
including but not limited to the following:
     •     Introductory training for visitors;
     •     Training for safe execution of work at the Company's construction sites;
     •     Training for contractors: This training is valid for 12 months, after which it is necessary to undergo training again;
     •     On-the-job training on following aspects,
                 o     Safety Protocols: Training on PPE use, emergency procedures, and hazard identification.
                 o     Equipment Operation: Hands-on training for machinery and equipment.
                 o     Toolbox Talks: Regular, brief safety meetings to discuss specific hazards, safety practices, and updates
                       relevant to the day's tasks.
                 o     Task-Specific Skills: Learning specific techniques required for job roles.
                 o     Worksite Orientation: Familiarization with site layout and key areas.
                 o     Quality Standards: Understanding and maintaining quality expectations.
                 o     Environmental Practices: Education on waste management and pollution control.
                 o     Communication: Effective reporting and communication skills.
                 o     Continuous Improvement: Encouraging innovation and efficiency.
Before undergoing training and upon employment, all employees must undergo a medical examination for their
professional suitability. A copy of the employee's health certificate is provided for permanent recording in a central
repository, and the original is kept in the office at the construction site.
Before anyone is allowed to perform work or to move independently between sites within the project area, they must
have completed the relevant project training. This will ensure that all project personnel are familiar with the relevant
safety requirements and site requirements.
Persons temporarily or occasionally involved in project work must have completed the same training and comply with
the same requirements. The Contractor must ensure that specific training is provided to discuss the hazards specific to
the particular site and any work that affects the Company's existing production facilities.
The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that its personnel have the required training requirements and that they have
the required skills and qualifications. The Contractor must plan its work appropriately to ensure that personnel are
trained in a timely manner when significant changes occur at the proposed site, such as commissioning.


9.1 Construction Worker Induction Training
The followings are the key steps to be taken during the Induction Training of workers to be engaged at construction sites,
     •     Induction Training for Construction Workers of Contractors:
                 o     Introduction: A brief greeting and introduction of the trainer or training team.
                 o     Importance: Emphasizing the significance of induction training for ensuring safety and productivity on
                       the construction site.
                 o     Objective: The goal of the presentation is to equip each construction worker with the essential
                       knowledge to work safely and efficiently.
     •     General Safety Rules on the Construction Site:
                 o     PPE Requirements: Mandatory wearing of personal protective equipment (PPE) such as hard hats,
                       gloves, safety glasses, and safety shoes. This section includes statistics on injuries caused by the lack of
                       PPE.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                         Page | 162
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o      High-Risk Areas: Identification and marking of high-risk areas on the construction site.
                 o      Evacuation Procedures: Detailed procedures for evacuation in case of emergencies like fire, earthquake,
                        or gas leak, including designated assembly points.
     •     Use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):
                 o      Overview: A detailed overview of each type of PPE, including correct selection, fitting, care, and
                        replacement.
                 o      Demonstration: Practical demonstration of the correct use of PPE.
                 o      Consequences: Discussion on the consequences of improper use or lack of PPE, supported by real case
                        examples.
     •     Working at Height:
                 o      Safety Rules: Safety rules for working at height, including the use of safety harnesses, fences, and
                        scaffolding.
                 o      Equipment Checks: Importance of checking equipment for working at height before use.
                 o      Load Limits: Permissible loads on scaffolding and platforms.
                 o      Accident Statistics: Statistics of accidents when working at height and their causes.
     •     Electrical Safety:
                 o      Dangers: Explanation of the dangers of electric shock.
                 o      Safe Practices: Rules for working with power tools and electrical equipment.
                 o      Protective Gear: Use of dielectric gloves and mats.
                 o      Prohibitions: Prohibition of using damaged cables and sockets.
                 o      Emergency Actions: Actions to take in case of electric shock.
     •     Fire Safety:
                 o      Prevention: Measures for preventing fires, including proper storage of flammable materials and
                        smoking in designated areas.
                 o      Firefighting Equipment: Use of primary fire extinguishing equipment such as fire extinguishers and fire
                        hydrants.
                 o      Emergency Response: Actions to take upon detecting a fire, including calling the fire department and
                        evacuation procedures.
                 o      Fire Exits: Location of fire exits and fire extinguishing equipment on the site.
     •     First Aid:
                 o      Principles: Basic principles of first aid for injuries, burns, poisoning, and electric shock.
                 o      First Aid Kit: Contents of the first aid kit and rules for its use.
                 o      Contacts: Contact details of medical personnel and emergency services.
                 o      Practical Training: Practical training in first aid, such as cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).
     •     Prevention of Soil and Water Pollution:
                 o      Conservation: Importance of saving electricity and water.
                 o      Environmental Responsibility: Responsibility for adhering to environmental standards.
     •     Rights and Obligations of the Builder:
                 o      Employment Contract: Basic provisions of the employment contract, including remuneration and work
                        schedule.
                 o      Safety Rights: Rights to safe working conditions.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1        Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                          SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                       11 August 2025                      Page | 163
P7: Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan (OHSMP)



                 o     Responsibilities: Responsibilities for compliance with safety rules and labor discipline.
                 o     Reporting: Procedure for reporting violations and hazards in the workplace.
     •     Conclusion and Questions:
                 o     Review: A brief review of the key points covered in the introductory training.
                 o     Q&A: Answers to questions from participants.
                 o     Confirmation: Confirmation of understanding of the material and signing of the necessary documents.
                 o     Closing: Wishes for safe and productive work.

             •   Using OH&S checklists:
             •   What is a checklist: A tool for systematically checking compliance with OH&S requirements.

Annex A - Strategic Plan for Improved Environmental Performance of K1 HPP by Greening Selected Degraded

  Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                                   Client Reference No. 5018007
  Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
  Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                                  11 August 2025                                           Page | 175
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




The Kambarata-1 Hydropower Project (K-1 HPP) is a planned high-head storage hydropower scheme on the Naryn River
in the Kyrgyz Republic. Located in a steep and glaciated mountainous terrain, the project comprises a 260.8 m high
concrete arch dam and deep tunnel infrastructure, with a reservoir expected to inundate approximately 61.7 km² at full
supply level. Construction of the dam, tunnels, power station, and associated access infrastructure will involve substantial
earthworks, including excavation, blasting, tunnelling, spoil disposal, and vegetation clearance. These activities pose
significant erosion and sediment risks, particularly during the construction and initial reservoir filling phases.
The Catchment Area Treatment (CAT) and Reservoir Rim Management Plan has been developed to identify and mitigate
these risks by outlining a programme of biological and engineering measures targeted at stabilising vulnerable slopes,
preventing sedimentation of the reservoir, and restoring disturbed areas. The plan forms a component of the project's
Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) and links directly to other plans including the Muck and Spoil
Management Plan, Water Quality and Resource Management Plan, and Site Restoration Plan.
At this stage, the CAT and Reservoir Rim Management Plan has been prepared to support the feasibility-stage ESIA and
reflects available information at the time of reporting. It will be updated once AFRY's final feasibility study is completed,
including erosion risk mapping (e.g. through RUSLE or equivalent methods), geotechnical assessment of rim slopes, and
finalisation of muck disposal and rehabilitation design. In addition, a separate Catchment Area Treatment Plan
commissioned by the World Bank, which includes a RUSLE-based prioritisation of afforestation and slope rehabilitation
activities across key Naryn sub-basins, has been prepared to provide technical depth and broader catchment
management strategies. This detailed strategic treatment plan is included in Annex A and complements the construction-
phase interventions set out in this document.



1.                Scope
This Catchment Area Treatment and Reservoir Rim Management Plan applies to:
     •     All zones within and surrounding the Kambarata-1 Hydropower Project that may be affected by erosion, slope
           instability, and sediment mobilisation due to construction activities.
     •     Catchment areas identified in the WB Consultant’s plan (Annex A) as critical sources of sediment, prioritised
           through RUSLE-based erosion mapping.
     •     Reservoir rim slopes likely to be destabilised by drawdown or construction-related disturbance.
     •     Muck disposal zones, spoil sites, quarries, access roads, and other working areas requiring slope stabilisation
           and restoration.
The scope includes two interrelated elements:
     •     Construction-phase erosion and slope management, implemented by the EPC contractor under PMO oversight,
           and aligned with feasibility-level risks and subplans.
     •     Catchment-scale treatment strategies, addressing longer-term sediment control and ecosystem rehabilitation
           needs in degraded upstream micro-catchments, based on the WB Consultant’s spatial prioritisation.



2.                Objectives
The objectives of this Catchment Area Treatment and Reservoir Rim Management Plan are to:
     •     Mitigate sediment mobilisation and erosion risks associated with dam construction, tunnel excavation, and
           ancillary infrastructure.
     •     Stabilise vulnerable slopes within the reservoir rim and in construction-impacted zones to reduce landslide and
           sedimentation hazards.
     •     Rehabilitate and revegetate degraded areas affected by spoil disposal, access road development, and vegetation
           clearance.
     •     Integrate site-based slope stabilisation with catchment-scale erosion control measures informed by RUSLE-
           based prioritisation.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                    Page | 176
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




     •     Provide a unified, phased framework for the implementation of bioengineering and civil works, combining World
           Bank consultant recommendations (Annex A) with ESMP requirements.
     •     Support environmental compliance under Kyrgyz national law, World Bank ESS1 and ESS6, and relevant GIIP
           standards (e.g., IFC PS6, IHA Hydropower Sustainability Guidelines).
The objectives and intervention priorities outlined in this document are fully aligned with those in Annex A, reflecting a
unified approach based on the most recent technical assessment and spatial analysis conducted by the World Bank
Consultant team.

This plan is grounded in a suite of national laws, international standards, and good practice guidelines. These provide the
regulatory and operational framework for erosion control, ecological rehabilitation, and slope stability measures
associated with the Kambarata-1 HPP.
Key regulations, standards and guidelines that have informed this plan and that offer additional guidance are outlined in
Table 54 below.
Where discrepancies exist between local and international standards, the more stringent requirement will apply.

5.                Baseline Conditions and Project Risk Profile
The Kambarata-1 HPP is located in a steep, glaciated mountain terrain characterised by complex geology, high relief, and
intense seasonal runoff from snowmelt and glacial meltwater. The project site is subject to a range of natural erosion
processes, including sheet erosion, rill and gully formation, and slope failure. Human disturbance during the construction
phase, including vegetation clearance, spoil disposal, tunnelling, and road construction, will exacerbate these risks.
Erosion hazards are most acute in the following zones:
     •     Reservoir Rim: Subject to slope instability from water level fluctuations during impoundment and drawdown.
           Steep natural slopes and disturbed excavation faces increase the risk of landslides.
     •     Spoil and Muck Disposal Zones: Often located on engineered terraces that require stabilisation. Without proper
           compaction, drainage, and cover, they are prone to gully erosion and mass movement.
     •     Construction Zones and Access Roads: These create large exposed surfaces that increase runoff and sediment
           transport, especially during spring melt or storm events.
     •     Upper Catchment Slopes: Contribute background sediment loads to the reservoir, particularly from degraded
           micro-catchments identified in Annex A.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                  SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                               11 August 2025                            Page | 179
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




Without mitigation, these conditions could lead to reduced reservoir storage, infrastructure damage, downstream water
quality deterioration, and long-term landscape degradation. The integrated CATP aims to address both site-specific and
catchment-wide risks using nature-based and engineered solutions.


5.1               Construction Phases and Thematic Linkages
The construction and operation of the Kambarata-1 Hydropower Project involves substantial earthworks and landscape
modification across multiple phases. In the pre-construction phase, vegetation clearance, slope excavation, and initial
access road development may contribute to increased erosion and localised slope instability, particularly on steep
hillsides or river-adjacent slopes.
During the construction phase, large volumes of spoil and muck generated from tunnelling, foundation excavation, and
road construction will require placement and containment. This poses a heightened risk of sediment transport into the
Naryn River and its tributaries if not properly managed. Reservoir impoundment is also expected to affect slope stability
in rim areas, particularly near muck disposal zones.
Biological and engineering measures included in this plan are designed to stabilise slopes, reduce sediment loads, and
restore areas that have been disturbed during construction activities. These interventions align with site-specific risks
and mitigation objectives identified in the Muck and Spoil Management Plan, the Water Quality and Resource
Management Plan, and the ESMP.
This plan is closely linked to other thematic environmental management plans, as shown in table below.

Table 55: Links to other Management Plans
  Management Plans linked with the Waste
                                                                                   Description of the Linked Management Plan
      Management Plan (M&WMP)
 Environmental and Social Management                      Provides overarching structure for implementation, monitoring, and adaptive
 Plan (ESMP)                                              management of CAT interventions.
 Muck and Spoil Management Plan                           Implements measures to manage and rehabilitate spoil/muck generated from
                                                          major works (e.g. tunnels, dam, access roads).
 Water Quality and Resource Management                    Addresses runoff management, sediment control, and prevention of slope-
 Plan                                                     induced water pollution.
 Site Restoration Management Plan                         Provides guidelines for rehabilitation of temporary works areas, supporting
                                                          vegetation regrowth and erosion control.
 Biodiversity Management Plan                             Guides restoration of vegetated buffers and riparian habitats affected by erosion
                                                          control activities.



6.                Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The principal impacts and risks associated with erosion, slope instability, and sediment mobilisation at the Kambarata-1
HPP are concentrated across the project lifecycle, particularly during construction and early operation. If left unmanaged,
these risks could undermine infrastructure safety, impair reservoir function, degrade aquatic ecosystems, and affect
downstream users.
The figures presented in this Plan—including treatment area estimates, costs, and spatial targeting—are based on SMEC’s
preliminary erosion modelling and reconnaissance. These will be refined through detailed design and finalised in
coordination with the PMO and relevant implementing partners.
Key Risks:
     •     Sedimentation of Reservoir: Accumulated eroded material from construction zones, unstable slopes, and upper
           catchments will reduce reservoir storage capacity and operational life.
     •     Slope Failure and Landslides: Excavated or disturbed slopes, particularly along the reservoir rim and spoil
           disposal sites, pose high failure risk under rainfall, drawdown, or seismic conditions.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                  Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                    SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                 11 August 2025                        Page | 180
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




     •     Increased Turbidity and Downstream Water Quality Impacts: Suspended sediments and runoff from disturbed
           areas can impair aquatic habitats, drinking water supply, and downstream hydropower operations.
     •     Erosion from Spoil Heaps and Muck Disposal: Improperly managed spoil heaps and unprotected muck disposal
           areas can become chronic sources of gully erosion and sediment discharge.
     •     Compromised Road Access and Infrastructure Stability: Uncontrolled runoff from unsealed or poorly drained
           access roads can destabilise embankments, create maintenance hazards, and impair emergency response.
Secondary Risks:
     •     Delayed Site Rehabilitation: Without timely implementation of restoration measures, disturbed areas may
           remain sources of ongoing degradation.
     •     Reduced Biodiversity: Soil erosion and runoff can degrade riparian buffers and prevent reestablishment of native
           vegetation.
These risks justify a proactive, site-specific, and catchment-integrated approach to erosion control and land rehabilitation
as laid out in this CATP. Risk rankings and treatment priority zones are further detailed in Annex A.



7.                Mitigation and Monitoring
The following sections describe the biological and engineering control measures adopted to address these risks in line
with ESMP mitigation objectives.
The mitigation measures of the assessed impacts and the corresponding monitoring & reporting plan have been described
in detail on Table 25 and Table 26 respectively,
Monitoring results will inform CESMP updates and be reported in line with ESMP and lender requirements. Adaptive
measures (e.g. reinforcement, replanting, drainage realignment) shall be implemented where monitoring indicates
underperformance or renewed erosion risk.
Treatment bundles applied under this CATP are aligned with Annex A’s design standards and are tailored by slope class
(see Section “Treatment Standards and Data Confidence”). These include a combination of civil and vegetative measures,
with specific techniques selected based on slope angle, soil condition, and erosion severity.
Note: Where references are made in this plan to catchment-scale rehabilitation, erosion risk mapping, or landscape
treatment prioritisation, please refer to Annex A for technical details, spatial targeting, and associated cost tables.
Where monitoring indicates underperformance in treated slopes (e.g., vegetation dieback, runoff-induced erosion, or
structural failure of bunds), adaptive measures shall be implemented. These may include modification of slope
treatment bundles, substitution of species, or structural reinforcement based on Annex A guidelines. Corrective actions
should be documented in CESMP revisions.
