8.                Implementation Schedule
Implementation of the Catchment Area Treatment Plan will be phased in alignment with construction activities, reservoir
filling, and catchment-level rehabilitation targets outlined in Annex A. Key implementation phases and activities are
summarised below.

           Phase                          Timeline                                         Activities                           Lead Entities
 Phase 1: Pre-                   Prior to site                    •    Final geotechnical investigations for rim and spoil     PMO, AFRY,
 Construction                    mobilization                          zones                                                   EHSM, CEHSM
                                                                  •    Update CATP with AFRY feasibility outputs- Integrate
                                                                       CATP actions into CESMP
 Phase 2: Early                  Months 1–12                      •    Implement erosion control on access roads and           CEHSM, CE, CSS,
 Construction                                                          camps- Construct initial drainage structures            PMC
                                                                  •    Begin rim slope stabilization near tunnel portals and
                                                                       power station
 Phase 3: Core                   Months 13–36                     •    Full implementation of spoil terrace protection and     CEHSM, CE,
 Construction                                                          rim treatments                                          PMC, IESC
                                                                  •    Afforestation in offsite catchments (Annex A priority
                                                                       areas)
                                                                  •    Ongoing maintenance and corrective works
 Phase 4: Reservoir              During reservoir filling         •    Monitor rim slopes and sediment influx                  CEHSM, EHSM,
 Impoundment                                                      •    Adapt rim treatments based on slope performance         PMC, IESC
                                                                  •    Stabilize exposed drawdown zones
 Phase 5: Site                   Construction                     •    Decommission temporary facilities and roads-            CEHSM, EHSM,
 Restoration                     completion (Years 4–5)                Regrade, topsoil, and re-vegetate disturbed areas       IESC
                                                                  •    Finalize afforestation and post-works inspections


Implementation will be reviewed semi-annually with updates integrated into the CESMP. Schedule may be revised subject
to site conditions, reservoir filling rate, and final construction timelines.



9.                Stakeholder Engagement
The PMO Environmental and Social team, together with the PMC and the EPC Contractor’s EHS Manager, will engage
local stakeholders during implementation of this plan. Engagement will focus on explaining slope stabilisation activities,
proposed rim treatment measures, and drainage control works. Feedback from local communities, particularly those near
muck disposal areas or access roads, will be documented and addressed. Relevant monitoring results (e.g. visual slope
inspections or revegetation progress) will be periodically shared to build trust and transparency.
Stakeholder engagement for the Catchment Area Treatment Plan will be guided by the project's Stakeholder Engagement
Plan (SEP) and will align with the broader ESIA and ESMP consultation process. Engagement activities aim to enhance
transparency, secure local support, and integrate community knowledge into erosion control and restoration activities.
Key objectives include:
     •     Informing local communities and resource users about CATP implementation zones, timelines, and safety
           protocols.
     •     Encouraging participatory planning in areas where slope stabilisation or restoration intersects with traditional
           land uses.
     •     Coordinating with local authorities, forestry agencies, and pasture management bodies in the implementation
           of afforestation and slope treatment measures.
     •     Incorporating feedback mechanisms and grievance redress channels into CATP implementation.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                  SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                               11 August 2025                         Page | 184
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




Stakeholder engagement outcomes will be documented and incorporated into CESMP revisions. Adaptive measures may
be introduced based on feedback received during implementation.



10.               Community Complaints and Grievance
                  Redressal Mechanism
Grievances related to slope stabilisation works, erosion risks, runoff, or reservoir rim treatment will be addressed through
the project-wide Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM). Communities may submit complaints regarding earthworks,
disposal activities, or land restoration. The EPC Contractor is responsible for recording and responding to site-level
complaints and escalating unresolved issues to the PMO. All grievances will be tracked and reported as part of ESMP
monitoring.



11.               Training and Capacity Development Programs
Training modules will be developed by the EPC Contractor’s EHS Manager to build site team capacity on implementing
erosion control, slope stabilisation, and vegetation restoration measures. Trainings will cover topics such as:
     •     Safe installation and inspection of drainage systems
     •     Application of erosion control mats and bioengineering techniques
     •     Maintenance of bunds, check dams, and vegetative buffers
Trainings will be provided during induction and refresher sessions will occur at least quarterly. Records will be maintained
under the ESMP training register.



12.               Treatment Standards and Data Confidence
This CATP references intervention types and prioritised zones based on erosion risk analysis provided in Annex A. This
analysis applies the Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE) to estimate potential soil loss and identify high-risk areas
using GIS-based spatial modelling.
The treatment area and unit cost estimates presented in this plan are indicative and will be refined during detailed design
and implementation in consultation with the PMO and implementing partners.
The following elements of the Catchment Area Treatment Plan will require field verification and refinement during
detailed design and early implementation:
     •     Site access conditions, including slope stability and transport feasibility, which may affect construction logistics
           and unit costs;
     •     Local ground conditions, such as slope gradient, soil depth, and vegetation cover, which influence the suitability
           and durability of proposed treatments;
     •     Treatment zone boundaries and priority rankings, as defined through RUSLE-based erosion risk modelling, which
           should be validated through field observation;
     •     Feasibility of bioengineering techniques and required adjustments based on site-specific terrain, land use
           constraints, and availability of planting material.
Standard intervention "treatment bundles" are to be applied as follows:

 Slope Class                   Treatment Bundle                                    Description
 >30° (Very Steep)             Gabion toe walls + slope drains + vetiver           Mechanical plus bioengineering with surface runoff
                               contour hedges                                      control
 20–30° (Steep)                Bench terracing + live staking + erosion            Engineered terraces with deep-rooted vegetative
                               control matting                                     reinforcement



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1            Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                              SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                           11 August 2025                           Page | 185
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




 <20° (Moderate)               Hydroseeding + contour trenching                    Soft engineering with rapid ground cover establishment


Implementation of the Catchment Area Treatment Plan (CATP) will be the responsibility of the EPC contractor. This
includes the preparation of a detailed implementation plan specifying treatment locations, species to be planted,
sourcing arrangements (e.g. nurseries), and site preparation methods.
The contractor’s plan will require formal approval by the local Forestry Department, and implementation will be subject
to any conditions they impose.
Oversight of CATP implementation will be carried out by the PMC Engineer, with support from the PMC’s environmental
and biodiversity specialists and the PMO’s ESHS team.
While the CATP identifies indicative treatment zones and unit costs, these will be refined during detailed design and
updated by the contractor in coordination with the PMO, the PMC Engineer, and relevant line agencies.
Some treatment areas are located away from the core construction footprint. However, the contractor is required to
integrate these works into their delivery plan and pricing. Specific logistics, phasing, and nursery arrangements will be
finalised post-contract award.



13.               Implementation Budget
13.1              Justification and Detailed Rationale for the Costing
                  of the Catchment Area Treatment Plan
13.1.1            Phased Approach
The design of the program is based on a phased, adaptive management approach that balances the need for early,
intensive interventions with long-term sustainability through monitoring and maintenance.
As described in Annex A, the 2,118 ha selected for treatment includes segments that fall within the 500 m buffer around
the reservoir rim, particularly on steeper and moderately degraded slopes. However, the treated area does not form a
continuous band around the reservoir. Final prioritisation was driven by erosion risk (moderate to very severe), slope
stability, and feasibility of access.
The implementation is structured into three distinct phases as shown in Table 58 below. This phased structure ensures
that resources are efficiently allocated, with major investments concentrated in Years 1–2 for physical interventions, and
lower-cost monitoring and adaptive management in Years 3–7.

Table 58: Three phase implementation of the Catchment Treatment Plan
 Phase                  Year(s)      Activities                                          Rationale
 Phase 1:               Years        Large-scale     afforestation      (2,118    ha),   This is the most critical phase, front-loading
 Planting Phase         1–2          hydroseeding (500 ha), installation of check        interventions to establish vegetative cover, reduce
                                     dams (100 units), silt fences (10,000 m), topsoil   erosion risks, and stabilize exposed areas during
                                     application (5 ha), borrow pit stabilization (4     peak construction. The design assumes 1000
                                     pits),   drip    irrigation     systems,    field   seedlings/ha, consistent with WB Good Practice
                                     infrastructure (nurseries, containers, sanitary     Note recommendations.
                                     blocks).
 Phase 2:               Years        Annual monitoring of vegetation survival,           The focus shifts to sustaining the system, ensuring
 Maintenance &          3–7          weeding, fence and irrigation system repairs,       high survival rates (>85%), and maintaining
 Monitoring                          targeted replanting (~10–15% in Year 4),            structural integrity. Costs and intensity are
                                     adaptive erosion control measures (e.g.,            reduced, but essential for long-term impact.
                                     repairing check dams, replacing silt fences),
                                     biodiversity monitoring.

 Phase                  Year(s)      Activities                                              Rationale
 Phase 3: Final         Year 7       Independent third-party audit, stakeholder              Final assessment to ensure compliance with WB
 Audit and                           consultations, and final program closure                ESS6, national regulations, and project-specific
 Reporting                           reporting.                                              environmental commitments.


13.1.2            Target Areas, Interventions, and Methods
Erosion-prone sites were selected based on soil loss modelling (RUSLE), slope class, and land cover degradation, as
presented in Annex A. These areas form the basis for the intervention types proposed below.
     •     Kekemeren @ Kojomkul Sub-Basin: Prioritized for afforestation due to steep slopes and proximity to the
           construction site.
     •     Naryn @ Kek-Irim Sub-Basin: Includes riparian zones and moderately degraded slopes requiring hydroseeding
           and targeted erosion control.
The prioritised zones lie entirely within Sub-basins 7 and 8 (Kekemeren @ Kojomkul and Naryn @ Kek-Irim), which are
directly upstream of the proposed Kambarata-1 reservoir and fall within the defined area of influence for the ESIA. While
additional degraded areas exist elsewhere in the Naryn basin, this plan is intentionally focused on those most relevant to
potential project impacts — particularly sedimentation risk and slope instability — and where implementation is feasible
within the EPC contractor’s scope.
These priority areas were identified through spatial erosion risk modelling (RUSLE) and field interpretation, with selection
criteria including slope steepness, erosion severity, vegetation condition, and accessibility. Implementation was further
guided by logistical feasibility and the potential for direct oversight by the PMO and PMC.
Although afforestation, hydroseeding, and erosion control measures are standard in catchment rehabilitation, their
application here is tailored to sub-basin characteristics. The proposed interventions include:
     •     Afforestation of 2,118 ha using native species suited to the region’s ecological conditions: Tien Shan Spruce
           (Picea schrenkiana), Juniper (Juniperus semiglobosa, J. turkestanica), Walnut (Juglans regia), Maple (Acer
           turkestanicum), Birch (Betula spp.), Poplar (Populus spp.), and Elm (Ulmus spp.).
     •     Hydroseeding on 500 ha of steep, shallow-soil slopes, where tree planting is impractical. These areas are
           typically adjacent to, but do not form a continuous buffer along, the reservoir rim
     •     Erosion Control Structures: Check dams in gullies and ephemeral streams (100 units), silt fences on critical slopes
           (10,000 m), and drainage structures in borrow pits.
     •     Infrastructure: Two nurseries (2 ha each) are proposed for seedling propagation, supported by drip irrigation
           systems (gravity-fed and solar), containerised field offices, sanitary blocks, off-road vehicles, and field
           equipment. The current provision is expected to support phased seedling production over multiple years, based
           on typical nursery productivity (approximately 1,000 seedlings per hectare) and a planting density of 1,000
           seedlings per hectare. A detailed nursery capacity assessment will be conducted during micro-planning to
           confirm sufficiency for the 2,118 ha afforestation target. Where needed, additional seedlings may be sourced
           from approved third-party nurseries, subject to Forestry Department oversight
     •     Capacity Building: Local community engagement, training programs for nursery operations, planting techniques,
           maintenance protocols, and biodiversity monitoring.

13.1.3            Cost Structure and Distribution
The budget is designed to reflect the realistic distribution of costs over time, based on the logical sequence of activities
as follows:

Table 59: Cost Distribution
                Phase                       Years         % of Total Budget                                    Rationale
 Planting Phase                              1–2               ~75–80%             High upfront investment to establish core interventions
                                                                                   (planting, structures, logistics, equipment).

14.               Plan Review and Updating
This CATP is a dynamic document intended to be updated as new technical information becomes available and project
conditions evolve. Plan updates will occur in the following instances:
     •     Upon finalisation of AFRY’s feasibility study, including geotechnical risk zonation, updated muck disposal
           strategies, and reservoir rim treatment design;
     •     When new RUSLE-based erosion risk mapping, micro-watershed rehabilitation targets, or field verification
           results are incorporated into CESMP preparation;
     •     Following major design revisions that affect spoil disposal locations, access road alignment, or construction
           sequencing;
     •     In response to findings from monitoring activities, audits, or stakeholder feedback;
     •     As part of the adaptive management framework under the ESMP and lender oversight.
Plan revisions will be coordinated by the PMO EHS Manager in consultation with AFRY, the EPC Contractor’s EHS team,
and the IESC. Updates will be recorded in the CESMP revision log and disclosed to relevant stakeholders as part of project
transparency obligations.

P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




1.                Introduction and Study Context
 Kambarata 1 HPP, a 1880 MW 260.80 m high curved Roller Compacted Concrete (RCC) dam, is proposed to be
constructed on the Naryn River upriver of the existing Kambarata 2 HPP, in Jalalabad oblast of the Kyrgyz Republic. Given
its strategic significance for national energy security, K-1 is expected to play a vital role in meeting long-term power
demands and fostering regional energy trade.
The ecological implications of large dam projects—especially in fragile mountain environments such as the Tien Shan
range—require proactive and scientifically grounded interventions. Here, options to treat vulnerable areas emerges as a
strategic tool not only for managing erosion control that is immediate environmental risks but also for enhancing long-
term climate resilience, hydrological stability, and ecosystem health. Traditional CAT plans focus primarily on controlling
sediment inflow into the reservoir to preserve storage capacity, the case of Kambarata 1 is uniquely positioned. Feasibility
studies indicate that sediment deposition will be largely confined to the dead storage zone, posing no major threat to
reservoir operation or generation efficiency in the short to medium term. Yet, the environmental significance of
catchment rehabilitation extends far beyond sediment control. This offers a unique opportunity to reposition the
planning toward maximizing co-benefits—particularly carbon sequestration, slope stabilization, and ecosystem
restoration.
Mountain catchments like that of Kambarata are increasingly vulnerable to climate-induced changes, including altered
precipitation regimes, accelerated glacial melt, and intensifying erosion processes (IPCC, 2019). Studies from ICIMOD and
the Mountain Research Initiative (MRI) emphasize that high-altitude regions in Central Asia are experiencing rising
incidences of landslides, rockfalls, and flash floods, driven by unstable slopes, extreme weather events, and thawing
permafrost. These stressors necessitate the application of nature-based solutions (NbS) for landscape stability, slope
protection, and water resource buffering (ICIMOD, 2020; IPCC AR6 WGII, 2022).
The current Plan elements are grounded in and supported by satellite-based mapping of Digital Elevation Models (DEM),
soil characteristics, land use/land cover, and rainfall erosivity across two sub-basins—Kekemeren @ Kojomkul and Naryn
@ Kek-Irim. These layers were analyzed for the Kekemeren @ Kojomkul and Naryn @ Kek-Irim sub-basins—
representative areas within the larger catchment. This study forms the scientific basis for prioritizing treatment areas and
recommending nature-based interventions suited to the high-altitude, rocky terrain where nearly 40% of the catchment
comprises stony outcrops. By investing in the ecological integrity of the catchment, this Strategic Plan aims to deliver
long-term environmental dividends including improved slope stability, enhanced hydrological regulation, climate
mitigation, and support for local biodiversity—well-aligned with global best practices in sustainable hydropower
development (World Bank, 2020; UNEP, 2021).
The Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) estimates that the construction phase of K-1 could result in
approximately 2.465 million tCO₂e of greenhouse gas emissions over a seven-year period. Previous feasibility study
considered a Rock filled Earth Core Dam (2014). The ESIA estimates that using an ECRD would have resulted in 25%
lower GHG emissions for construction than the RCC section and CFRD would have resulted in 15 % lower GHG Emissions.
Since the RECD was part of the Options Study, it is surmised that about 15 % to 25% of the emissions of the total
2.465million tonnes is additional to what would have happened if any other section were used. So, when the project
manages its direct impacts, this becomes an important consideration. In this context, landscape-scale vegetative
interventions within the catchment offer a dual opportunity: to reduce erosion from vulnerable areas and to enhance
carbon sequestration through afforestation assisted natural regeneration, and other nature-based solutions. These
actions directly support climate co-benefits while retaining habitat mosaics that could support local fauna and improve
overall ecosystem functionality. Vegetation-based interventions in such terrains not only help reduce shallow landslide
risks but also improve soil cohesion, water infiltration, and dry-season base flow (FAO, 2016). Therefore, this strategic
plan aims that the carbon sequestration of at least 0.321 to 0.493 million tCO₂e which comes due to change in dam
section to RCC is achieved.
The plan has identified areas with high soil erosion intensity in the sub-basins contiguous with the submergence area
using satellite imagery. It has been cross-checked to the extent of overlap with the submergence area and buffer high
resolution map prepared for FS update by AFRY. The plan describes plantation and protection measures to minimize
erosion, enhancing the forest cover with increasing soil holding capacity reducing total sediment flow in the reservoir
and sequestering CO2 as biomass.
In sum, the overall aim is to have a climate-smart, ecosystem-centric strategy aligned with the broader sustainability
goals of the Kambarata 1 HPP. It aims not only to mitigate environmental impacts but also to transform the treated and




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                   Page | 198
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




adjacent parts of the landscape into active carbon sinks, biodiversity buffers, and possibility of green livelihood zones for
local communities.



2.                Objectives
This plan targets greening of degraded parcels with high erosion potential in the nearest sub-basins to the submergence
area for K1 HPP and carbon sequestration. The action plan has been prepared with the following objectives:
     •     Micro-Watershed Prioritization for Targeted Treatment
            To delineate priority areas within the catchment and close to the reservoir, using erosion modeling (RUSLE) and
            topographic indices, ensuring focused interventions in areas most vulnerable to surface runoff and slope
            degradation. This spatial approach strengthens upstream landscape management and safeguards the project’s
            downstream hydrological functions.
     •     Forest Landscape Restoration for Carbon and Biodiversity Benefits
            To rehabilitate degraded forest and scrubland areas through afforestation with native species, assisted natural
            regeneration, and enrichment planting. These activities contribute to enhanced carbon sequestration, support
            local biodiversity conservation (ESS6), and incidentally improve ecological connectivity across fragmented
            habitats.
     •     Vegetative Erosion Control for Slope and Soil Stability
            To stabilize slopes and reduce sheet and rill erosion by increasing vegetative ground cover through green
            measures such as vetiver planting, contour hedgerows, and bio-fencing. These interventions aim to enhance
            soil retention capacity and preserve ecosystem services, in line with ESS6 objectives of maintaining natural
            resource functions.
     •     Ecosystem and Habitat Maintenance for Long-Term Resilience
            To incidentally also protect newly created riparian buffers, through increased vegetal cover and ground
            moisture retention. These zones can function as biodiversity refuges, pollinator pathways, and climate-
            buffered microhabitats, contributing to the sustainable management of living natural resources (ESS6).



3.                 Overview of the Naryn Basin
The proposed Kambarata 1 dam site is located in the Naryn River basin, which covers 57,600 km² in central Kyrgyzstan.
The elevation ranges from below 1000 m in the West to above 4500 m in the East. The land-cover is predominantly
grassland, with some forests in mountain valleys and rocky, bare ground as well as glacier areas at high altitudes.
The FS Study update (AFRY 2025) divided entire catchment area is divided in 12 sub water basins (Figure 1-1). The 12 sub
water sheds (also called sub catchment) are defined based on topography and flow direction of surface water. Each sub
water shed boundary follows ridge lines where surface run off diverge. These areas are naturally draining and water flows
freely from higher elevation to lower one, ultimately reaching the main river. The name and area of each sub basin with
elevation is presented in Table 1-1.

4.                Topography and Drainage
The Naryn River basin in Kyrgyzstan is a region of high mountain topography, with elevations ranging from 370 m to 5125
m above sea level and is characterized by a complex network of river channels and tributaries. The river, which originates
in the Tian Shan mountains, drains into the Fergana Valley and eventually joins the Syr Darya River. The basin's drainage
is heavily influenced by snowmelt from the surrounding mountains, particularly during the spring and summer months.
The drainage map of the Naryn Basin is shown in Figure 1-2.




Figure 1-2: Drainage map of the Naryn Basin

(Source: AFRY, 2025 and map developed for this report)



5.                Soil
The Naryn River basin in Kyrgyzstan features diverse soil types, primarily chestnut and grey-brown desert soils, along with
some valuable chernozems and Turanian dark grey soils. The basin's climate, marked by extremes and its complex
topography contribute to the variations in soil composition across the region. Taxonomically these are Azonal intrazonal
soils, Soils of foothills, Soils of high mountains, Soils of intramontane basin and Soils of mid mountains. The soil map is
presented in Figure1-3.

6.2               Land use Categories and Erosion
The erosion acts differently in different land-use types. It is important to understand the nature of erosion in a land-use
class to further plan for treatment.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1         Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                           SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                        11 August 2025                              Page | 203
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




     •      Agricultural Land
            The Agriculture land covers about 3995 sq. km area constituting 6.94% of the total Basin area .This land use class
            is more prone to erosion.
     •      Settlements
            The Settlements covers about 8054 sq. km area constituting 13.98% of the total Basin area.
     •      Vegetation / Pasteur land
            The Vegetation / Pasteur land covers about 14347 sq. km area constituting 24.91% of the total Basin area.
     •      Barren land
            The Barren land covers about 23145 sq. km area constituting 40.18% of the total Basin area. This Land use is
            mostly exposed rock and less prone to erosion.
     •      Snow
            The snow covers about 5712 sq. km area constituting 9.92% of the total Basin area.
     •      Water bodies
            Around 2347 sq. km area constituting 4.07% of the Basin area is classified under water bodies. The category
            needs no treatment except that the unstable bank shall be provided stream bank stabilization through
            protection measures whenever required.



7.                Slope
The slope of a watershed plays a key role in controlling the soil and water retention thereby affecting the land-use
capability. The percentage of the slope in a watershed determines the soil erosion susceptibility and forms the basis for
classifying different of the watershed into suitable classes for formulating effective soil erosion conservation measures.
Broadly, the following slope classes and ranges (Table 1-3) are adopted to classify the slopes for the present study.

Table 1-3: Naryn Basin Area Under Different Slope Classes
   Sl. No               Slope Range (Degrees)                         Description       Area (sq. km)                 Area (%)
      1                           0-3.6                            Very Gentle Slope       8531.56                     14.81
      2                          3.6-7.6                              Gentle Slope         7044.33                     12.22
      3                          7.6-12.4                           Moderate Slope         7541.97                     13.09
      4                          12.4-18                          Moderately Steep         8523.15                     14.79
      5                           18-24                                   Steep            8598.46                     14.95
      6                         Above 24                               Very Steep         17360.53                     30.14
                                            Total                                           57600                       100

(Source: Calculation developed for this report)
The data shows that about 54.91% area lies between very gentle to moderately steep slope category of slope and balance
45.09% falls from steep to very steep slope category. The Slope map of the Naryn Basin is presented in Figure1-5.

9.                   Soil Erosion Intensity and Selection of
                     Treatment Area within Catchment
The area selected for the treatment lies partially in Kekemeren @ Kojomkul Sub Basin No. 8 and Naryn @ Kek-Irim Sub
Basin No. 7. The priority areas within this smaller focus area have been selected based on the following basis:
•                    Proximity to the Dam Location and Buffer Area of Submergence
•                    Having slope range 7.6 to 24
•                    Having Degraded Forest Land Use
•                    Having good accessibility
The Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE) Method has been used for calculation of soil loss in the sub basins.
RUSLE helps assess land degradation through soil related measures. RUSLE estimates long-term annual soil loss due to
erosion across different land uses and land management activities. The RUSLE equation, developed by the U.S.
Department of Agriculture, estimates average annual soil erosion as follows: A = R x K x L x S x C x P, where, A = average
annual soil loss, R = rainfall erosivity, K = soil erodibility, L = slope length, S = slope, C = cropping, P = conservation
practice.
The overall methodology used in the present study is schematically represented in Figure 1-7.




Figure 1-7: The overall methodology used in the present study

In this study, RUSLE was used for the assessment of annual soil loss. RUSLE was designed to predict long-term annual
averages of soil loss. A modern computer interface makes RUSLE easily used and uses physically meaningful input values
that are widely available in existing databases or can be easily obtained from DEM and satellite images. RUSLE is the best
available practical erosion prediction model that can be easily applied at the local or regional level. In addition to this,
many parameters such as slope, aspect, etc. derived from DEM and LULC (land use land cover) from satellite images can
be easily integrated with RUSLE.
The following maps have been prepared for the selected area of Kekemeren @ Kojomkul and Naryn @ Kek-Irim sub
basins:
•                    Digital Elevation Model (DEM): For slope and flow accumulation.

categories is 607.62 Sq. Km. However, these classes have been spread in very wide area of 2811.50 Sq. Km. and may not
be equally accessible or available for green protection measures (due to existing land use like agriculture, for example)

•                    Category of Erosion Intensity determined by using RUSLE
•                    Proximity to the submergence area / Buffer zone of 500 m along the periphery of Reservoir
•                    Land Use checked using National Forestry Inventory in Kyrgyzstan available at https://nfi2-kyrgyzstan.info/
                     which has a link to GIS Data Base.
•                    The preferred area for plantation activities falls in Slope Range 7.6 to 24 to minimize overlap with pastures.

10.               Activities to be undertaken
10.1              Normal Afforestation
The forestation will include rising of multi-tier mixed vegetation of suitable local species in the steep and sensitive
catchment areas of rivers/streams with the objective of keeping such areas under permanent vegetative cover.
Furthermore, degraded areas would also be brought under vegetation cover. Suitable trees of economic value to local
people shall be raised in the degraded forest areas near to villages with the objective of supplementing income of the
villagers. The major tree species suggested for afforestation include:
     •     Tien Shan Spruce (Picea schrenkiana)
                 o     Native to: Tien Shan mountains
                 o     Habitat: High mountain zones (1,500–3,000 meters)
                 o     Importance: Dominant conifer in mountain forests; used for timber.
     •     Juniper (Juniperus spp.)
                 o     Species: J. semiglobosa, J. turkestanica, J. seravschanica
                 o     Habitat: Mountainous and dry slopes
                 o     Cultural significance: Often used in traditional rituals and medicine.
     •     Walnut (Juglans regia)
                 o     Found in: Arslanbob forest, one of the largest natural walnut forests in the world.
                 o     Economic importance: Valued for nuts and high-quality wood.
     •     Apple Trees (Malus spp.)
                 o     Wild relatives: Malus sieversii, thought to be the ancestor of domestic apples.
                 o     Habitat: Mid-mountain forests
     •     Maple (Acer turkestanicum and others)
                 o     Habitat: Mixed forests in mountain regions
                 o     Features: Broadleaf species, colorful in autumn
     •     Birch (Betula spp.)
                 o     Found in: Cool, moist mountain areas
                 o     Use: Wood, ornamental purposes
     •     Poplar (Populus spp.)
                 o     Use: Fast-growing trees often used in reforestation and windbreaks
     •     Elm (Ulmus spp.)
                 o     Habitat: River valleys and lower elevations
                 o     Use: Shade and timber

11.               Cost Analysis of Different Works Under
                  Biological Measures
11.1              Plantation
The area for plantation has been considered based on the Category of Erosion Intensity, Degraded Forest Land Use,
Accessibility. The major indigenous species suggested are Tien Shan Spruce (Picea schrenkiana), Juniper (Juniperus spp.),
Walnut (Juglans regia), Apple Trees (Malus spp.), Maple (Acer turkestanicum and others), Birch (Betula spp.), Poplar
(Populus spp.), Elm (Ulmus spp.), etc.
The common activities of the forestry models were fencing, seedling plantation, use of NTFPs (walnuts and pistachio) and
watch & ward. The likely costs include seedlings for afforestation, the labor cost of planting, loosening of the soil after
planting, fencing and related labor costs. An example of walnut forest afforestation is provided in the Table 1-9 below:

11.2              Carbon Sequestration by Plantation in Catchment
                  Area
The total area under proposed plantation is estimated between 877 and 1347 ha with various proposed species as defined
above. The role of CO2 uptake in forests extends far beyond mere carbon sequestration. It is intimately connected to
trees’ growth and developmental dynamics, primarily through its influence on biomass accumulation. Biomass, in this
context, refers to the sum of all organic material that constitutes a tree’s physical structure – a tangible indicator of its
growth. Trees distribute this biomass in two primary compartments: Above-Ground Biomass (AGB) and Below-Ground
Biomass (BGB). Above-ground biomass encompasses the tree’s parts visible above the soil line, including the trunk,
branches, leaves, and any fruits or flowers. Below-ground biomass consists of the roots, which, though less visible, are
equally crucial for the tree’s stability and nutrient uptake.
This distribution of biomass is central to understanding how trees store carbon. The CO 2 absorbed from the atmosphere
is assimilated into both AGB and BGB. As a tree matures, its capacity to sequester carbon increases, making older trees
more efficient at absorbing CO2. This relationship highlights the significance of forest conservation and the role mature
trees play in mitigating climate change. By understanding and supporting these natural processes, we can enhance the
capacity of forests to act as carbon sinks, thereby contributing to global efforts to balance our planet’s carbon cycle and
combat the escalating challenges of climate change.
To accurately determine the amount of CO 2 absorbed by trees, two critical measurements are taken directly from the
tree: its diameter, measured in cm, and its height, measured in meters. These measurements are essential for calculating
both the Above-Ground Biomass (AGB) and Below-Ground Biomass (BGB). These biomass values are calculated based on
a specific formula that incorporates these two parameters.
AGB = 0.25 x D2 x H
Where:



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                 Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                   SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                11 August 2025                                   Page | 217
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




     •     AGB: Above-Ground Biomass (pounds).
     •     D: The tree diameter measured 1.37 meters from the ground (inches). This measurement is used globally as a
           standard to get better results. However, you can still use the formula if your tree is below 1.37 meters.
     •     H: The tree height (feet).
The overall green weight of the biomass is estimated to be 120% of the AGB value, based on the assumption that the
BGB, which comprises the tree’s root system, accounts for approximately 20% of the AGB. Therefore, BGB can be
calculated as follows:
BGB = 0.2 × AGB
From these formulas, we can calculate the total biomass from a tree:
Total Biomass (TB) = AGB + BGB = AGB + 0.2 x AGB = 1.2 × AGB
On average, a tree consists of 72.5% dry matter and 27.5% moisture content. To calculate the tree’s dry weight, we could
multiply the total weight of the tree by 72.5%.
Total Dry Weight (TDW) = TB × 0.725
Carbon occupies 50% of the total dry weight. Therefore,
Total Carbon (TC) = TDW × 0.5
With the value of total carbon, we can calculate the value of CO2 equivalent sequestered on a tree. CO2 has one molecule
of Carbon and two molecules of Oxygen. The atomic weight of Carbon is 12, and the atomic weight of Oxygen is 16. The
weight of CO2 in trees is determined by the ratio of CO2 to C is 44/12 = 3.67. Therefore, to determine the weight of carbon
dioxide sequestered in the tree, multiply the weight of carbon in the tree by 3.67.
CO2 weight = TC × 3.67
It is worth noting that the CO2 weight above represents the CO2 sequestered in the entire lifetime of the tree. To ascertain
the annual or yearly rate of CO2 sequestration, divide the total weight of CO2 absorbed by the tree’s age.
For calculation of carbon sequestration, the carbon absorption by walnut tree has been considered and 18.3 tCO2e / ha /
year* value has been taken with a consideration that 278 walnut trees planted in a hectare. The total proposed plantation
under this plan is between 877 and 1347 ha with a density of 278 trees per hectare. This can achieve the targeted total
carbon sequestration under plantation activities between 0.321 and 0.493 million ton CO2 over a period of 20 years. The
carbon sequestration may vary from plant to plant and its density per hectare. It is to be noted that the actual final carbon
sequestration depends on types of species and its density selected for the plantation.
*Source: World Bank,(2023)



12.               Cost of Other Components of Proposed
                  Activities
Apart from the forestry works in the degraded area there are other aspects of the proposed activities to be addressed
and their cost included in the overall cost estimate of the plan. The documentation, monitoring and evaluation are the
integral ingredients, which have to be considered and included.


12.1              Provision for Micro Plans
Based on the ground truth reality in each of the authority zone under different priority zones, comprehensive micro plan
for execution of the work must be prepared. The micro plan for each priority zone shall be prepared in consultation with
the members of concerned authority with due regards to the environmental functions and productive potential of the
forests and their carrying capacity. The key components of micro plan include detailed mapping / survey and analysis of
the proposed area, ground truthing of the proposed area for treatment, community participation and awareness, etc.
The standard procedures in line with Forestry Code (FC) will be followed as described concisely below:




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                   Page | 218
P8: Catchment Area Treatment Plan




Afforestation works can be carried out only by special programs and projects established by the authorized forest
department (Leskhozes).
In accordance with the Forest Code, the project will through the Leskhozes, comply with the technical requirements
related to forest rehabilitation and afforestation, referred to in the FC as “Reproduction of Forests”. In compliance with
Article 91 of the Forest Code forest rehabilitation is to be carried out on land of the forest fund ensuring that: i)
Reforestation be conducted of land after felling concentrating on valuable tree species; ii) Lands not covered by trees and
bushes need to be rehabilitated; iii) Afforestation activities need to focus on the improvement of the composition of tree
species and increase of forest productivity, protection and environmental functions; and iv) Actively engage in the
conservation of genetic resources and forest biodiversity.
During preparation of the microplans for each area, consultations among the PMO, representatives of the AAs, Forest
Fund officials, and Forestry Agency as appropriate, can choose most appropriate modality for developing the particular
parcels of land from the indicative menu below
(a) Leskhoze-driven investments in high-altitude spruce and juniper forests (long rotation timber forest); (b) Collaborative
Forest Management (Leskhozes and rural poor households) through long-term leasing of walnut forests on SFF lands
from Leskhozes to households; and (c) Leskhozes tree-planting on State Forest Fund (SFF) and State Land Fund (SLF) lands
(mix of poplar, willow etc.) that will be leased annually to AF beneficiaries for sustainable fuel wood.
Source: IFAD (2020)


12.2              Provision for Proper Documentation
Emphasis should be laid on the publicity of the work proposed under the plan and work carried out on annual basis so
that transparency is maintained and proper documentation of the work is carried out for future reference, and testing
the efficacy of the work in due course of time. The documentation would inter alia include implementation report,
progress reports, photography, videography with dates and locations etc.


12.3              Provision for Monitoring and Evaluation
The success of implementation of a proposed Plan can be fathomed by increase in vegetal cover on hill slopes and the
enhancement. Various biological measures have been aimed at treating degraded and potential areas of severe to very
severe soil erosion by increasing soil holding capacity and thus reducing sediment flow in the water.
A close watch on annual basis shall be maintained in respect of such areas where habitat improvement works have been
carried out to verify the work executed on site itself and also to ascertain the rate of survival of plants and / or any
damage to the new work. For monitoring of works under forestry operation, the use of remote sensing technique by
using digital satellite imagery with high resolution should be made obligatory. The monitoring through satellite scene
should be done before commencement of works and in a block of two years after completion of plantation plan. The
work of monitoring of various works under the this plan should be entrusted to an external agency which has long
experience of carrying out similar work on land use data and evaluating environment impact.


12.4              Implementation of Proposed Activities
The designated Environmental Officer of PMO would coordinate for the implementation of the proposed Plan. The
Environment Officer would evaluate/monitor financial aspects. The modalities of financial disbursement need to be
worked out. The implementation of proposed activities should have enough flexibility and should be subject to changes
as per requirements of specific ecosystem and periodic gains.


12.5              Project Monitoring and Reporting Procedures
Meetings would be held every three months to resolve problems arising in plan implementation. A Joint committee may
be formed with the Environment Cell of Project Proponent and Concerned Authority; the team members must ensure
implementation and monitoring of the works and review the progress from time to time. Quarterly progress reports and
completion certificates would be submitted to Project Proponent for evaluation and disbursement of finance. In addition,
the work done should be published through public awareness campaigns. Visual and print media may be used. Such
efforts would resolve conflicts which otherwise are potential sources for project delays.

1.                Introduction
This Environmental Flow Management Plan (EFMP) has been prepared to support the implementation of the Kambarata-
1 Hydropower Project (Kambarata-1 HPP). It is based on the Environmental Flow Impact Assessment (EFIA) presented in
Appendix 10 C of the ESIA and complements the Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA).
The EFMP outlines the management approach, mitigation measures, and institutional responsibilities associated with
temporary flow-related risks during non-routine project phases and peaking operations.
Although no environmental flow releases are required under normal operating conditions since the reach is permanently
impounded by the backwater of Kambarata‑2 HPP, with no residual free‑flowing channel or riverine ecology, the EFIA
identifies specific scenarios where downstream conditions may be temporarily affected. These include river diversion,
initial reservoir impoundment, emergency or planned drawdowns, seasonal refilling, and peaking operations. In such
cases, uncoordinated flow changes may result in reservoir instability, shoreline erosion, or short-term sediment and
water level fluctuations within the Kambarata-2 reservoir.
This EFMP is designed as an adaptive plan, to be updated as needed. Its implementation will be integrated into the
overarching Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) and coordinated between the Kambarata-1 HPP
operator, the Kambarata-2 HPP operator, and the Project Management Office (PMO).
It is noted that the EFMP findings are based on available modelling and assessments as of early 2025. Final conclusions
on flow thresholds, sediment dynamics, and bank stability will be updated as further work is carried out to finalise
reservoir operations and basin wide studies and downstream riparian studies are carried out.. Until that stage, the
measures outlined herein should be considered provisional and subject to adaptive revision



2.                Purpose and Scope
This Environmental Flow Management Plan (EFMP) has been developed to support the implementation of the
Kambarata-1 Hydropower Project (Kambarata-1 HPP). It outlines the measures required to manage environmental flow-
related risks within the hydraulically impounded reach between the proposed Kambarata-1 dam and the existing
Kambarata-2 Hydropower Project (Kambarata-2 HPP).
As confirmed in the Environmental Flow Impact Assessment (EFIA), no environmental flow releases are required under
normal operating conditions, since the entire reach is permanently inundated. However, under certain non-routine
operational scenarios, such as initial reservoir impoundment, seasonal refilling, peaking operations, or emergency
drawdowns, temporary flow regulation may be required to maintain downstream water level stability and avoid shoreline
impacts in the Kambarata-2 reservoir.
This EFMP provides a structured framework for managing such risks through targeted mitigation measures, coordinated
reservoir operations, and adaptive monitoring protocols. It is embedded within the broader Environmental and Social
Management Plan (ESMP) and will be reviewed periodically as new hydrological, ecological, social, geotechnical, or
operational data become available. As the impounded reach lacks a defined river channel, the EFMP does not apply
conventional environmental flow methodologies but instead focuses on flow coordination and slope protection within a
linked reservoir system.
While this EFMP focuses on operational flow management within the hydraulically impounded reach, it is informed by
the broader basin-scale analysis presented in the Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA). The CIA identifies transboundary
water availability risks and recommends the development of a Joint Impoundment Planning Framework to coordinate
reservoir filling across the Naryn Basin. Although such planning mechanisms fall outside the scope of this EFMP, their
relevance to non-routine flow scenarios, particularly initial impoundment, is acknowledged and supported through the
mitigation measures outlined herein.



3.                Objectives
The primary objective of this Environmental Flow Management Plan (EFMP) is to ensure that water level stability is
maintained within the Kambarata-2 HPP reservoir, particularly during phases of the project when upstream flow
continuity may be temporarily interrupted. Although the river reach between Kambarata-1 and Kambarata-2 is



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 222
P9: Environmental Flow Management Plan




permanently impounded, temporary changes in discharge—such as during initial impoundment, seasonal refilling, or
emergency drawdowns—may affect downstream reservoir conditions.
Specific objectives of the EFMP include:
     •     Preventing rapid drawdown or unstable water level fluctuations in Kambarata-2 HPP during non-routine
           operational events and peaking operations;
     •     Minimising physical impacts on reservoir margins, including slope instability and shoreline erosion;
     •     Maintaining coordination between the Kambarata-1 and Kambarata-2 HPP operators to support stable cascade
           operation and downstream reservoir management;
     •     Enabling adaptive management through defined thresholds, monitoring requirements, and responsive
           mitigation measures during uncertain or evolving hydrological conditions.
     •     Applying ramping protocols to regulate discharge variability during peaking operations, thereby protecting
           Kambarata-2 reservoir stability and minimising shoreline erosion and turbidity.
     •     Ensuring that project-level safeguards complement basin-level coordination mechanisms recommended in the
           CIA, including the development of a Joint Impoundment Plan to be agreed among riparian states.



4.                Regulatory and Policy Framework
This EFMP has been developed with reference to relevant regulatory and policy instruments that guide environmental
flow management in large hydropower projects. These include:
     •     The World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESF), specifically Environmental and Social Standard 6
           (ESS6) on biodiversity conservation and the sustainable management of living natural resources;
     •     IFC Performance Standard 6 (PS6), which outlines the importance of maintaining ecosystem services, including
           the regulation of natural flow regimes to support aquatic and riparian habitats;
     •     The World Bank Environmental Flows Handbook (2018), which provides guidance on risk-based and scenario-
           specific environmental flow planning.
While these frameworks are primarily designed for river systems with defined channels and natural flow regimes, their
principles have been adapted to the unique conditions of the Kambarata-1 to Kambarata-2 reach. This segment is
permanently impounded, lacks a free-flowing river channel, and functions hydraulically as a linked reservoir system. As
such, conventional environmental flow methodologies do not apply.
In this context, the EFMP focuses on:
     •     Maintaining water level stability within the Kambarata-2 reservoir;
     •     Minimising shoreline erosion and slope instability during non-routine operational events; and
     •     Ensuring coordinated cascade operation during impoundment, drawdown, and seasonal flow variation.
The EFMP is based on international good practice and tailored to the specific hydrological and operational conditions of
the Kambarata-1 to Kambarata-2 impounded reach.



5.                Key Risks and Impact Scenarios
The EFIA identified five non-routine operational scenarios where temporary flow regulation, reservoir coordination, or
shoreline protection may be required to manage downstream risks within the hydraulically connected Kambarata-2
reservoir. These include:
     •     During construction, the Naryn River will be diverted around the Kambarata-1 dam site. However, this reach lies
           entirely within the backwater zone of the existing Kambarata-2 reservoir and will remain inundated under
           baseline and diversion-phase conditions. As such, the river will not be dewatered, and continuous flow will be
           maintained. A longitudinal section showing the dam faces, reservoir extents, and hydraulic profile is provided in
           the Environmental Flow Impact Assessment (Appendix 10-C) to support this conclusion.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                   Page | 223
P9: Environmental Flow Management Plan




     •     Initial Reservoir Impoundment: As inflows are withheld during the filling of the Kambarata-1 HPP reservoir,
           discharges to Kambarata-2 HPP will cease unless managed releases are implemented. This could result in a rapid
           drawdown of the Kambarata-2 HPP reservoir, with potential consequences for aquatic species, reservoir water
           quality, and power generation. Depending on the timing of the impoundment (e.g. whether it begins during the
           spring freshet), the filling period may range from two to seven months.
     •     Emergency or Planned Drawdowns: In the event of planned maintenance or dam safety-related emergencies,
           sudden reservoir releases may be required. These have the potential to cause abrupt water level changes in
           Kambarata-2 HPP, which could affect ecological stability and the integrity of the reservoir shoreline.
     •     Seasonal Refilling of Kambarata-1: AFRY’s modelling confirms that reduced discharges (~250 m³/s) to
           Kambarata-2 will continue during seasonal refilling of K-1. Although no additional environmental flow releases
           are required, temporary drawdown at K-2 HPP may still occur if inflows are low and generation continues.
           Operational coordination will be needed to maintain reservoir level stability.
     •     Peaking Operations: peaking operations at Kambarata-1 HPP have the potential to result in short-term discharge
           variability resulting in abrupt water level fluctuations in the upper portion of Kambarata-2. These fluctuations
           are likely to be most significant when K-2 HPP is drawn down and has reduced buffering capacity. Effects may
           include slope destabilisation, resuspension of deposited sediments, and pressure on downstream operations.
           Apply defined ramping protocols to Kambarata-1 turbine discharges during load-following operation. This will
           minimise abrupt water level fluctuations in Kambarata-2, reduce shoreline erosion and slope instability, and
           control turbidity associated with sediment resuspension. Thresholds will be defined by the K‑1 HPP operator
           (lead) and K‑2 HPP operator (coordination), and the E&S Team (monitoring), and will be reviewed annually.



6.                Mitigation and Management Measures
Unlike most environmental flow plans, this EFMP is not designed to maintain downstream instream flows in a free-flowing
river. The Kambarata-1 to Kambarata-2 reach is fully impounded, and natural river processes no longer operate within
this segment. The objective of this EFMP is to manage construction and operational phase impacts on the water level
regime of the Kambarata-2 reservoir. This includes maintaining near-steady water levels to minimise shoreline slumping,
prevent turbidity events, and coordinate reservoir filling and discharges between the two facilities. Accordingly, flow
control is focused on level regulation and event-based risk management rather than routine environmental flow release.
This approach is based on the findings of the Environmental Flow Impact Assessment (Appendix 10-C), which confirms
that the reach between Kambarata-1 and Kambarata-2 is fully impounded. The EFIA includes a longitudinal profile
demonstrating that Kambarata-1’s tailwater discharges directly into the Kambarata-2 reservoir, with no intervening free-
flowing channel. As such, there is no requirement for operational ecological flow releases, and this EFMP focuses solely
on managing water levels and shoreline risks within the K-2 HPP reservoir system.
During construction, the Naryn River will be diverted around the main dam site. However, the diversion works will take
place within an area already hydraulically controlled by the backwater of the Kambarata‑2 reservoir. Continuous flow will
be maintained through the diversion tunnels, and because there is no free‑flowing reach to be dewatered, ecological
risks such as stranding or channel degradation do not arise. The primary management requirement during this phase is
to monitor K‑2 HPP reservoir levels and ensure diversion and cofferdam operations are coordinated to avoid abrupt
changes in water levels.

7.                Monitoring Programme
Monitoring during non-routine operational events is essential to assess environmental and operational risks, verify
compliance with EFMP measures, and enable adaptive response. The monitoring programme will include:
     •     Continuous telemetry of water levels in both Kambarata-1 HPP and Kambarata-2 HPP, with alert thresholds
           linked to potential drawdown risks;
     •     Measurement of flow volumes released from Kambarata-1 HPP, including during temporary releases, initial
           impoundment, or discharge ramping events;
     •     Event-based monitoring of water quality parameters (e.g. turbidity, dissolved oxygen) during impoundment,
           rapid drawdown, or discharge fluctuations, particularly in the upper Kambarata-2 reservoir;
     •     Visual inspection of shoreline and reservoir rim conditions in the upper Kambarata-2 reservoir, including signs
           of slope instability, erosion, or sediment mobilisation during water level fluctuations;
     •     Coordination with the Biodiversity Management Plan (BMP) to track any indicators of aquatic or riparian
           ecosystem stress, especially in response to unplanned drawdown or peaking operations.
Data will be reviewed weekly during periods of active flow management and summarised in monthly E&S reports.
Baseline shoreline conditions will be documented prior to impoundment, and event-based monitoring will continue
through the first operational year. Monitoring protocols will be reviewed after the first year of operation or following any
major hydrological deviation.



8.                Institutional Responsibilities and Coordination
Implementation of the EFMP will require coordinated action among several key stakeholders:
     •     The Kambarata-1 HPP Operator will be responsible for executing flow regulation measures, including controlled
           impoundment, application of ramping rules, and communication of planned releases.
           Note: While the Operator may shadow the impoundment process, primary responsibility for planning and
           executing the initial impoundment lies with the PMO and the Contractor until formal handover occurs.
     •     The Kambarata-2 HPP Operator will provide real-time water level data and input into operational decisions that
           may affect reservoir performance or ecological conditions.
     •     The Project Management Office (PMO) will oversee coordination, reporting, and activation of EFMP measures,
           ensuring timely implementation and cross-plan consistency.
     •     During initial impoundment, the PMO holds ultimate authority to initiate reservoir filling, subject to verification
           of regulatory, environmental, and safety preconditions. The PMO is supported by the PMC Engineer and the
           Contractor.
     •     FIDIC PMC Engineer provides independent oversight of environmental flow-related mitigation, ensuring that the
           Contractor and PMO implement measures in line with FIDIC contractual obligations and international ESHS
           standards. The PMC Engineer verifies compliance, quality assurance, and addresses non-conformances,
           reporting regularly to the PMO and the World Bank.
     •     For impoundment, the PMC Engineer will review and approve the Contractor’s Impoundment Plan, which will
           include “Hold Points” at defined elevations requiring inspection and formal clearance prior to continuation. The
           PMC Engineer may engage specialist advisors or liaise with the World Bank as needed.
     •     The Environmental and Social (E&S) Team will monitor compliance, review monitoring results, and provide
           technical guidance for adaptive response where needed.
     •     The Lenders’ Engineer or Independent Environmental Consultant will periodically verify the implementation and
           effectiveness of EFMP measures, particularly during the initial years of operation.
While the EFMP outlines the overarching management framework for reservoir level regulation and risk mitigation,
relevant measures will be incorporated into the site-specific Construction Environmental and Social Management Plan


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007

(CESMP) to be prepared by the Contractor. The CESMP will include method statements and work procedures for
implementing EFMP actions during construction. The PMO, with support from the PMC Engineer and E&S Team, will
verify that EFMP measures are appropriately embedded in the CESMP and implemented in line with lender and regulatory
requirements.



9.                Review and Adaptive Management
The EFMP is designed as a living document and will be updated at key project milestones or when triggered by operational
or environmental changes.
Subsequent reviews will be undertaken every three years or earlier if triggered by:
     •     A shift in operational mode (e.g. introduction of peaking operations);
     •     Observations of ecological impacts or bank instability;
     •     Revisions to design thresholds or operational rules that affect flow regulation or reservoir levels.
Lessons learned during impoundment and early operations will inform adaptive revisions to monitoring frequencies,
trigger thresholds, and mitigation measures to ensure ongoing risk management and compliance.



10.               Linkages to Other Management Plans
The EFMP complements several other management plans developed under the ESIA, including:
     •     Surface and Groundwater Management Plan;
     •     Catchment Area Treatment Plan;
     •     The Biodiversity Management; and
     •     The overarching Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP), into which the EFMP is embedded as a
           topic-specific sub-plan with its own implementation, monitoring, and compliance framework.
These linkages ensure that flow-related risks are not assessed in isolation but are addressed within an integrated
environmental and social risk management system.
Additionally, the EFMP complements the CIA, which provides basin-scale analysis of hydrological risks and transboundary
water availability. While the EFMP focuses on operational safeguards within the impounded reach, the CIA outlines
broader planning needs, including the development of a Joint Impoundment Planning Framework, to mitigate cumulative
downstream impacts. This linkage ensures that site-specific flow management is aligned with regional water resource
coordination.

1.                Scope
This Biodiversity Management Plan (hereinafter referred to as the ‘BMP’ or the ‘Plan’) defines the requirements and
procedures for managing biodiversity impacts and risks during the following 3 key project phases for the Kambarata 1
Hydropower Project (hereinafter referred to as ‘K1 HPP’ or the ‘Project’):


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                                  Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                                    SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                                 11 August 2025                                           Page | 229
P10: Biodiversity Management Plan




     •     Phase 1 – Pre-Construction Phase, including site clearance
     •     Phase 2 – Construction Phase, including initial reservoir filling
     •     Phase 3 – Operation Phase, including maintenance
The Pre-construction Phase refers to the initial preparatory phase during which the Engineering and Geological Surveys
towards the Project are conducted, access roads are laid, the various construction sites are prepared, systems for water
supply, power supply and sewerage are installed and systems for aggregate production and concrete mixing are
established.
The Construction Phase refers to the subsequent phase during which the various construction sites and the
corresponding labour camps are established and made operational, the tunnelling for the HRT and TRT is carried out, the
river is diverted, the dam is constructed, the power generation and evacuation facilities are installed, and the initial filling
of the reservoir takes place.
The Operation Phase refers to the phase of active power generation by the Project and includes the associated
maintenance of Project infrastructure and regulation of flows.
The scope of the BMP includes compliance with the following World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESF)
requirements:
     •     Environmental and Social Standard 1 (ESS1): Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks
           and Impacts
     •     ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions
     •     ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
     •     ESS4: Community Health and Safety (mainly with respect to ecosystem services)
     •     ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (mainly related to economic
           displacement resulting from loss of access to ecosystem services)
     •     ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
     •     ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
The BMP outlines the following aspects:
     •     Biodiversity management measures to be implemented during the Pre-construction, Construction and
           Operation phases of the Project.
     •     Organizational responsibilities for implementing and reporting on the said measures.
     •     Training or capacity development requirements.
     •     Indicative budget for implementation.
     •     Monitoring and evaluation programmes to guide adaptive management of biodiversity impacts and risks
           throughout the life of the Project.



2.                Objectives
The BMP aims to ensure that biodiversity aspects, in terms of species, habitats and ecosystem services, associated with
the area of influence of the Project are managed in alignment with the biodiversity guidance provided by the World Bank
ESF.
The objective of the BMP is to reasonably ensure the following outcomes:
     •     Significant Project-related biodiversity impacts or risks are managed in alignment with the prescribed mitigation
           hierarchy of avoidance or minimization of impacts, followed by offsetting of any residual impacts through
           conservation or restoration
     •     Any critical habitat known or precautionarily assumed to overlap the area of influence of the Project is mitigated
           to ensure net gain for the corresponding biodiversity values.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                      Page | 230
P10: Biodiversity Management Plan




     •     Any Project-related loss of significant natural habitat is mitigated to ensure no net biodiversity loss.
     •     Significant Project-related impacts or risks with respect to any species of conservation concern are mitigated as
           per good international industry practice.
     •     Significant Project-related impacts on modified habitats are mitigated in line with the applicable good practice
           guidance.
     •     Any highly valued priority ecosystem services with respect to the Project are maintained or enhanced.

This section presents the delineation of roles and responsibilities of the personnel responsible for implementing the
overall ESMP, including the BMP.

s and overall Environment, Social, Health & Safety (ESHS) management of the Project is presented below.

                                                                         aspects.
                                                                    •    Ensure field-level compliance with biodiversity management measures.

guidelines with which the BMP has been aligned.

 International Standards and Guidelines
 9.            WB ESS 6: Biodiversity                 ESS6 recognizes that protection and conservation of biodiversity and sustainable
               Conservation and                       management of living natural resources are fundamental to sustainable development.
               Sustainable Management of              It recognizes the importance of maintaining core ecological functions of habitats,
               Living Natural Resources               including forests, and the biodiversity they support.
                                                      The main objectives of ESS6 are to conserve biodiversity, apply the mitigation hierarchy
                                                      and the precautionary approach in the design and implementation of projects that
                                                      could have an impact on biodiversity, promote sustainable management of living
                                                      natural resources, support livelihoods of local communities, including Indigenous
                                                      Peoples, and ensure inclusive economic development, through the adoption of
                                                      practices that integrate conservation needs and development priorities. The
                                                      requirements of ESS6 are applicable to all projects that potentially affect biodiversity or
                                                      habitats, either positively or negatively, directly or indirectly, or that depend upon
                                                      biodiversity for their success.
                                                      ESS6 considers direct, indirect, and cumulative project-related impacts on habitats and
                                                      the biodiversity they support. It considers threats to biodiversity from habitat loss,
                                                      degradation and fragmentation, invasive alien species, overexploitation of resources,
                                                      hydrological changes, nutrient loading, pollution, incidental take and projected climate
                                                      change impacts. It will determine the significance of biodiversity or habitats based on
                                                      their vulnerability and irreplaceability at a global, regional, or national level and will also
                                                      consider the differing values attached to biodiversity and habitats by project-affected
                                                      parties and other interested parties.
 10.           EIB Standard 4 –                       Standard 4 emphasises that the protection and conservation of biodiversity and
               Biodiversity and Ecosystems            ecosystems, and maintaining their ecological functions and processes, are fundamental
                                                      to environmental and social sustainability. This standard sets out the requirements and
                                                      measures that the promoter must adopt throughout the different stages of a project
                                                      supported by the Bank to achieve a “no loss” of biodiversity and — where required —
                                                      a net positive impact. Standard 4 also addresses the sustainable management and use
                                                      of living natural resources, such as plants, trees and forests, and recognises the need to
