5.                Context
The biodiversity screening conducted as part of the Screening and Scoping towards the ESIA identified 4 potential critical
habitat (CH) triggers – 3 plant species and 1 fish species - with respect to the Study Area. The 2 seasonal biodiversity
baseline surveys conducted in the Study Area have confirmed occurrence of the 3 plant species therein, while confirming
availability of suitable habitat therein for the 1 fish species. However, data gaps regarding the population sizes of the said
species in the Study Area have prevented CH determination. Adopting a precautionary approach, in alignment with the
reference frameworks governing the ESIA, the Study Area has been assumed to contain, or be situated within, CH for the
said 4 species (Table 64). The BMP includes mitigation measures that would reasonably achieve Net Gain (NG) for the
said 4 precautionarily assumed CH qualifying (CHQ) species.

Table 64: List of Precautionarily Assumed CHQ Species
 SN     Scientific Name                                           Common Name                                   IUCN Red List Status
 1      Ammopiptanthus nanus                                      Dwarf Ammopiptanthus                          CR
 2      Crataegus knorringiana                                    Knorring’s Hawthorn                           CR
 3      Aspiolucius esocinus                                      Pike Asp                                      EN
 4      Tulipa toktogulica                                        Toktogul Tulip                                EN


The biodiversity baseline surveys have also confirmed presence of natural habitat – mainly riparian forest, shrubland,
cold desert, rocky areas and inland wetland - within the Study Area. GIS-based habitat-mapping has enabled
quantification of the areas of the said habitat-types situated within the overall Study Area, as also, within the predicted
submergence and sanitary zones. This has provided the approximate areas of the corresponding Project-related loss of
the said natural habitat-types (Table 65). The BMP includes mitigation measures that would reasonably achieve No Net
Loss (NNL) with respect to the said natural habitat.

Table 65: Details of Natural Habitats within the Submergence Zone
 SN      Habitat Type                 Habitat Class                          Total Area (sq. km)
 1       Terrestrial                 Grassland                                               49.07




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1                   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                                  11 August 2025                      Page | 235
P10: Biodiversity Management Plan




 SN      Habitat Type                 Habitat Class                          Total Area (sq. km)
                                      Rocky Areas                                             2.34
 2                                    Shrubland                                               0.07
 3                                    Forest                                                  0.06
 4                                    Desert - Cold                                           0.45
 5       Aquatic                      Inland Wetland                                          9.26


Based on inputs received from stakeholders, the grassland habitats of the Study Area are utilized for grazing livestock by
the Affected Community, which highly values them as a fodder-source. Part of the said grassland habitats or the local
community’s access routes to the same are expected to be impacted by the Project-related submergence, amounting to
Project-related loss of, or loss of access to, the concerned highly valued provisioning ecosystem service. The said loss is
sought to be mitigated through a Pasture Restoration Programme, which though linked to this BMP, is beyond its scope.
The biodiversity baseline surveys have identified within the Study Area, the occurrence of 13 plant species with
geographical ranges restricted to all or part of West Asia, Central Asia and western China. As per inputs received from
representatives of the Government of the Kyrgyz Republic, the said species are nationally considered native or endemic
to Kyrgyzstan and hence, have cultural value for the Kyrgyz people. 2 of the said plant species are also listed in the Red
Book of the Kyrgyz Republic, with such listing amounting to legal protection at the national level. Significant populations
of the said 13 plant species are expected to be impacted by the Project-related submergence, amounting to Project-
related loss of a cultural ecosystem service. The BMP includes measures designed to mitigate the said loss.
The biodiversity baseline surveys have also recorded within the Study Area 14 faunal species, including 5 mammals, 8
birds and 1 fish species, all listed in the Red Book of KR, indicating legal protection at the national level. However, there
is limited data available on the usage of habitats within the Study Area by the said 13 mammal and bird species. The BMP
includes measures designed to enhance Study Area specific data on the 13 mammal/bird species. The fish species is
nationally valued as being native to Kyrgyzstan, amounting to a cultural ecosystem service. The BMP includes measures
to enhance the said cultural service.
Table below presents details of the said 13 legally protected and/or culturally valued plant species and 14 legally
protected faunal species of Kyrgyzstan.

6.                Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
Based on the ESIA, significant Project-related impacts or risks have been identified with respect to the following
biodiversity values:
      •     Potential Critical Habitat (CH) for 4 precautionarily assumed CH qualifying (CHQ) species.
      •     Significant Natural Habitat.
      •     Provisioning Ecosystem Service.
      •     Cultural Ecosystem Service.
The following sub-sections summarize the impacts or risks foreseen.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1         Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                           SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                        11 August 2025                                Page | 237
P10: Biodiversity Management Plan




6.1               Impacts on Terrestrial Biodiversity
The following types of impacts on terrestrial and aquatic biodiversity are foreseen from the proposed pre-construction
and construction phase activities:
The Project-related activities during the said phases are likely to lead to the following general impacts on terrestrial
species, habitats and ecosystem services:
     •     Loss of terrestrial habitats, including natural habitats and potential critical habitats, owing to removal or
           submergence/inundation of vegetation.
     •     Fragmentation of terrestrial habitats owing to construction of Project-related linear structures, such as roads,
           pipelines and power transmission lines.
     •     Degradation of terrestrial habitats, including natural habitats and potential critical habitats, owing to excavation,
           dumping, erosion, deposition or compaction of soil, as also, inappropriate waste storage and disposal.
     •     Unintentional introduction or spread of invasive alien species owing to movement of project-related personnel,
           vehicles and machinery.
     •     Disturbance, injury or mortality of flora and fauna owing to Project-related structures and activities or noise,
           vibration, artificial illumination, emissions, effluents, dust or muck generated therefrom.
     •     Reduction in local populations of terrestrial flora and fauna species owing to incidental utilization by Project
           personnel for personal use, such as food or fuel.
     •     Loss or loss of access to terrestrial ecosystem services, including highly valued provisioning services, owing to
           removal, submergence or degradation of terrestrial vegetation.


6.2               Impacts on Aquatic Biodiversity
The Project-related activities during the said phases are likely to lead to the following general impacts on aquatic species,
habitats and ecosystem services:
     •     Loss or degradation of aquatic habitats, including natural habitats and precautionarily assumed critical habitat,
           owing to conversion of lotic habitat to lentic habitat.
     •     Degradation of aquatic habitats, including natural habitats and precautionarily assumed critical habitat, owing
           to Project-related pollution or contamination.
     •     Fragmentation of aquatic habitat owing to construction of Project-related infrastructure.
     •     Reduction in local populations of fish species owing to incidental utilization by Project personnel for food or
           sport.


6.3               Cumulative Impacts
In addition to the above construction-related impacts, the Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) identified a range of
biodiversity-relevant risks associated with the operation of Kambarata-1 HPP in combination with other cascade dams,
particularly Toktogul. These are distinct from site-level impacts and relate to hydrological regime change, ecological flow
requirements, and transboundary effects.
Table 67: Confirmed and Potential Biodiversity-related Cumulative Impacts (from CIA)

   Biodiversity Receptor /              Potential Impact
   VEC

                              species. Spawning opportunities already constrained by lack of suitable sediment substrate
                              between K-1 and Toktogul.
 Riparian and Floodplain      Reduced flood pulses limiting regeneration of riparian vegetation; potential loss of natural habitat

7.                Mitigation and Monitoring
This section outlines the measures proposed to manage the identified biodiversity impacts and risks in line with ESMP
mitigation objectives.
The following sub-sections outline the proposed essential and supporting mitigation measures.


7.1               Essential Mitigation Measures
7.1.1             Supplementary Focused Surveys
Commission appropriately timed seasonal biodiversity surveys focused on identifying and documenting the following
aspects in the Study Area:
Individuals or populations of the following 3 precautionarily assumed CHQ species situated within or adjacent to each
proposed Project-related construction site or laydown area, followed by mapping the encountered individuals/
populations and marking transplantable individuals.
CR Ammopiptanthus nanus (Dwarf Sandlip)
CR Crataegus knorringiana (Knorring’s Hawthorn)
EN Tulipa toktogulica (Toktogul Tulip)
Seeds or bulbs of the 13 legally protected and/or culturally valued plant species (listed in Table 5-3) for conservation
breeding.
Any breeding habitats of globally threatened and/or legally protected mammal and bird species within the Project
footprint, followed by timely reporting to governmental wildlife management authorities/ agencies for appropriate
conservation action aligned with national wildlife regulations.
Presence of EN Aspiolucius esocinus (Pike Asp) in the Study Area, through eDNA studies focused on habitat-types and
features known to be preferred by the species, such as fast-flowing, turbid freshwater.
Numbers and distributions of Invasive Alien Species (IAS) in the Study Area to inform an effective IAS Management
strategy for the Project, particularly its pre-construction and construction phases.

7.1.2             No Net Loss of Natural Habitat
In alignment with ESS6 requirements, the measures proposed for the Project-related natural habitat loss aim to
reasonably ensure no net biodiversity loss with respect to the Project-related submergence of natural riparian forest
(0.06 sq km), shrubland (0.07 sq km), cold desert (0.5 sq km) and rocky areas (2.5 sq km).
The proposed mitigation measures are as follows:
     •     The seeds or bulbs of the 13 native species, which comprise trees, shrubs and herbs associated with the natural
           habitat types of the Project Site, would need to be bred to develop healthy saplings.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                    Page | 239
P10: Biodiversity Management Plan




     •     Sites containing habitat and microhabitat types observed to be suitable to the 13 species would need to be
           identified for plantation of the saplings.
     •     Compensatory plantation of the saplings would need to be carried out at the said sites, ideally in numbers
           comparable to those recorded in the Project Site, subject to availability of space and access.
     •     The plantation should be monitored to document the survival of the saplings and replace any that fail to survive.
     •     The monitoring frequency during the initial 2 years of the plantation needs to be at least once per season in the
           spring, summer and autumn seasons, but can be reduced to twice per year thereafter.
Note: Any plantations required towards the BMP could be nested into the Catchment Area Treatment Plan for the Project.


7.1.3             Net Gain for 3 CHQ Plant Species*
     •     Detailed seasonal surveys would need to be conducted to map the extent and number of the 3 CHQ flora species
           namely CR Ammopiptanthus nanus, CR Crataegus knorringiana and EN Tulipa toktogulica, as also, to collect
           seeds or bulbs of the said species for conservation breeding.
     •     The number of individuals and habitat characteristics of the said species would need to be recorded and
           locations of occurrence documented, along with the associated habitat and microhabitats.
     •     A translocation protocol would need to be developed to ensure that as many CHQ individuals as possible from
           the submergence zone are translocated and transplanted at appropriate alternative sites in the Study Area.
     •     Regular monitoring should be conducted to document survival of the transplanted individuals and plant nursery-
           grown saplings to replace individuals that fail to survive.
     •     For species or populations for which translocation is not feasible, seeds or bulbs should be collected and
           propagated in designated nurseries.
     •     Saplings generated in the nurseries should be planted in the nearest possible comparable habitats, subject to
           availability of land and access.
*Subject to findings of Supplementary Focused Surveys and CH Determination


7.1.4             Net Gain for 1 CHQ Fish Species*
     •     An eDNA Study focused on the CHQ fish species EN Aspiolucius esocinus (Pike Asp) would be conducted in the
           Study Area, as well as 5 km river-sections upstream and downstream of the Study Area, to ascertain presence
           and approximate numbers of the species therein.
     •     Areas outside the Study Area having habitat features suitable to the species, such as fast-flowing turbid water,
           would need to be identified as Alternative Pike Asp Habitats.
     •     A translocation protocol should be developed to ensure safe translocation of any individuals encountered to
           Alternative Pike Asp Habitats.
     •     Any individuals encountered within the submergence zone or trapped in reservoirs/ river-sections downstream
           of K1 would need to be safely translocated upstream of the reservoir and any other dam proposed in the Upper
           Naryn or its tributaries.
     •     A Conservation Hatchery for controlled propagation and release of the species in the Naryn River Basin upstream
           of the Study Area may be explored in consultation with appropriate SMEs and stakeholders.
*Subject to findings of Supplementary Focused Surveys and CH Determination




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                        Page | 240
P10: Biodiversity Management Plan




7.2               Supporting Mitigation Measures
7.2.1             Chance Find Protocols
     •     Chance Find Protocols (CFP) should be instituted at the Project Site for globally threatened, legally protected
           and/or culturally valued species identified for the Study Area.
     •     At a minimum, the CFPs should include the 4 CHQ species, as also, the 3 IUCN-assessed globally threatened,
           legally protected and culturally valued species, namely VU Malus sieversii (Siever’s Apple), EN Neophron
           percnopterus (Egyptian Vulture) and VU Panthera uncia (Snow Leopard).
     •     The CFPs should outline procedures to be followed in case of a Chance Find Event of each CHQ species, including
           contact details of the appropriate Project or governmental authorities to be informed.
     •     All on-site Project personnel should be trained to implement the CFP in the event of encountering any of the
           said species at the Project Site at any juncture during the life of the project.
     •     Presence of appropriate experts should be ensured before and during Project-related vegetation removal or
           river channel interventions to check for presence of the applicable species immediately prior to removal of any
           vegetation towards site clearance, with appropriate relocation of any individuals encountered.
     •     The Chance Find Protocol documents, signage or other materials, should be subject to periodic review and
           update.

7.2.2             Invasive Alien Species (IAS) Management
     •     Protocols should be instituted and implemented for prevention of introduction or spread of IAS via Project-
           related materials, vehicles and personnel.
     •     Protocols should be established for appropriate handling and disposal of any IAS individuals encountered during
           site clearing activities.
     •     Project personnel, as well as the local community must be sensitized against unintentional or intentional
           introduction or spread of IAS of flora and fauna into the habitats of the Study Area.
     •     Any Project-related plantation or fisheries development in the Study Area must strictly exclude any alien species.

7.2.3             TL-related Collision/Electrocution Risk Management
     •     Install Bird Flight Diverters (BFD) devices on Project-related power transmission lines.
     •     Select bird-friendly transmission tower/ pole designs, subject to feasibility.
     •     Address residual electrocution mortality risk by installing perch-excluders or bird-guards on cross-arms of TL
           poles, inserting insulation sleeves on jumper wires and conductor-sections adjacent to TL poles and use of
           suspended insulators.
     •     Conduct fatality monitoring at an appropriate frequency in all Project-related transmission corridors.


Table 68 provides a consolidated overview of the mitigation measures and the corresponding monitoring and reporting.
Monitoring results will inform updates and be reported in line with ESMP and lender requirements. Adaptive measures
shall be implemented where monitoring indicates underperformance of mitigation measures or identifies previously
unforeseen or renewed risks.

8.                Linkages to Other Management Plans
The BMP has linkages with the following other sub-plans developed under the overarching Environmental and Social
Management Plan (ESMP):
      Air Quality Management Plan
      Water Quality and Resource Management Plan
      Materials and Waste Management Plan
      Muck and Spoil Management Plan
      Site Restoration Management Plan
      Occupational Health and Safety Management Plan
      Catchment Area Treatment Plan
      Environmental Flow Management Plan
      Labour Management Plan
The said other plans include mitigation measures that contribute to biodiversity impact and risk management.




9.                Training and Capacity Development
Training modules are expected to be developed by the EPC Contractor’s EHS Manager for capacity building of their
relevant personnel in implementing the applicable biodiversity mitigation measures.
Trainings are likely to be required on aspects such as:
     •     Avoidance of unplanned extraction of biological resources.
     •     Phased removal of vegetation during site clearing.
     •     Preclusion of unintentional or deliberate introduction of alien species.
Trainings would be provided at the time of induction, as well as, during the employment, ideally with a quarterly
periodicity for refresher sessions, with maintenance of detailed records on the day, time/ duration, content and
attendance per training session.



10.               Implementation Budget
This section presents an indicative budget for the BMP activities, based on currently available information on the Project
and estimates received from national biodiversity experts involved in the ESIA, as well as other stakeholders.
Note: Costs associated with specific environmental and social management actions would reflect in the respective sub-
plans having linkages to the BMP and would be accounted for in the corresponding other sub-plan budgets.

P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




1.                Scope
The purpose of the Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan (CHSSMP) is to implement all reasonable
precautions to protect the health and safety of nearby communities and villages, to protect against and mitigate security
risks that could threaten communities, employees, facilities, and ability to operate, and to ensure that the Project
complies with the requirements of the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Framework 2016 (ESS 4: Community
Health and Safety) and Environmental, Health, & Safety General Guidelines (World Bank, April 30, 2007, Section 3.0).
The plan addresses critical aspects such as community health and safety, with specific measures tailored to the
hydroelectric plant's infrastructure design and safety standards. It includes provisions to manage hazardous materials,
ensure the safety of services, and mitigate risks related to traffic and road safety during construction and operation
phases. Emergency preparedness and response strategies are integral to managing potential incidents, while efforts to
reduce community exposure to health risks are prioritized. Additionally, the plan incorporates measures to protect
ecosystem services and ensures that security personnel are deployed responsibly, following human rights principles and
proportional to the nature and gravity of the security situation. The Plan also provides a clear set of actions and
responsibilities for the control of impacts affecting the health and safety of the communities.
This plan is applicable to following direct impact areas,
  •     During Construction Phase
        ➢     The construction site for the dam and Hydro-power plant
        ➢     Construction sites for Associated Facilities such as access roads, transmission line, bridge as well as quarries
              and borrow pits used as a source of raw materials for dam construction.
        ➢     Workers’ Camp for housing construction workers
        ➢     Settlements and marketplaces where interactions between workers and local people may lead to cultural
              conflicts
        ➢     Major settlements on the North-South Highway near to the access road intersection which will experience
              high incremental traffic volume leading to road safety issues.
  •     During O&M Phase
        ➢     The area inundated due to the dam (approximately 61.7 km2 at a full supply level of 1,198 msl) forming the
              reservoir.
        ➢     Dam and Hydro-power plant site.
        ➢     Resettlement Area

2. Objectives
The objectives of the CHSSMP are to:
  •     Identify impacts on community health, safety and security due to the Project and suggest mitigation measures to
        avoid or reduce impacts and enhance any positive impacts.
  •     Identify strategies that provide adequate health related information and prevention measures through which
        communities can manage their own health and safety in an optimum manner.
  •     Describe additional measures required to implement Good International Industry Practice (GIIP).
  •     Continuously identify, evaluate and prioritise the risks and impacts of Yara Dallol BV’s activities on the health,
        safety and security of local communities.
  •     Outline the roles and responsibilities for implementing the CHSSMP, including monitoring and evaluation.

3. Project Brief
The proposed Kambarata-1 HPP project is expected to be one of the largest hydropower projects in the Kyrgyz Republic
as well as Central Asia, with an ultimate capacity to generate approximately 1,880 MW of power and produce around
5,961GWh of energy. The Project will be constructed in the middle section of the Naryn River and is located 14.5 km



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                     Page | 256
P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




upstream of the existing Kambarata HPP-2 dam. A RCC (Roller Compacted Concrete) gravity dam (curved) has been
proposed as the most suitable inline hydraulic structure to be built across the Naryn River for water impoundment.
The proposed height of the non-overflow section of the RCC Gravity Dam is proposed to be 260.8 m. The HPP building
will be located on the right bank of the river and installed with four Francis turbines. A total of 6,318 hectares of land has
been allocated for the project by the Kyrgyz Republic. It is estimated that around 600 workers will be engaged during the
first year of construction and 7,000 workers during the peak period of dam construction. The construction period will be
approximately 104 months or 8.5 years.
The Project including the reservoir area falls in Toktogul and Toguz Toro districts in Jalalabad Region and is spread across
four Aiyl Aimaks, which are Toluk, Sary Kamysh, Imanaliev and Atai and 10 villages. The nearest major settlements to the
Project Site are Kara-Jygach and Uch-Terek.

of the ESMPs and overall Environment, Social, Health & Safety (ESHS) management of the Project is presented below:

6. Context
The construction and operation of Kambarata-1 Hydropower project will lead to various activities which may have
adverse impacts on the local communities in terms of health issues due to air and water pollution, inter-cultural conflict
due to worker influx, traffic safety due to increase in traffic volume, increase in Gender-based Violence, Sexual
Harassment Exploitation & Abuse, safety and security risks due to use of security forces etc. The activities which may lead
to impact on community health, safety and security are given below.

7. Summary of Key Impacts and Risks
The Project site (Dam and HPP) is situated in a gorge with steep slopes on both banks. The site is generally not accessed
by the local people. Due to the steep topography, there are no settlements in the vicinity of the Project site. Similarly,
the reservoir, in most places, is located in a ravine with no settlements on either banks. The land which will be submerged
due to the reservoir, is mostly used for seasonal grazing by villagers from four Aiyl Aimaks: Toluk, Sary Kamysh, Imanaliev
and Atai. As mentioned in previous sections, the activities which are envisaged to have adverse impact on local
communities in terms of health, social interactions, safety and security are transport of raw material, labour influx, use
of security personnel etc during construction phase. The following sections gives a summary of the key impacts and risks
associated with the Project.


7.1               Baseline Health Condition
The baseline health conditions were ascertained from secondary sources such as District Passports and primary data
collected through household socio-economic surveys, Focus Group Discussions and Key Informant Interviews. Prevalence
of non-communicable diseases was found in the area. Incidence of Hypertension (especially in the high-altitude villages),
cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, anaemia and respiratory diseases are quite high. In Toluk and Kyzyl-Ozgorush, diarrheal
diseases among children are a major issue due to poor quality drinking water and poor sanitation facilities.
Some of the healthcare issues identified in the affected Aiyl Aimaks are as follows:
  • Shortage of medical personnel and resources such as medical equipment
  • Infrastructure deficiencies such as dilapidated buildings and lack of basic facilities such as drinking water, sewage
    network, etc
  • Difficulties in physical access to the health centres due to bad road conditions, lack of emergency transport facilities
    and long distance from nearest healthcare facilities.
  • Lack of practice for preventive health measures such as better nutrition due to poor economic conditions.


7.2               Baseline – Safety and Security
All project affected communities are remote and largely self-contained communities, that experience very low levels of
crime. It is difficult to obtain formal statistical records on crime rates, so insights were gained through some Key
Informant Interviews to verify that crime rates were low. The key concern expressed about the project was in relation to
traffic safety and if vehicles are forced to use the Alternate North South Highway, this could impact on community safety.
This is due to the fact this road is in poor condition as a result of a number of areas impacted by rockfalls, making two
way traffic not possible in some areas. Hence additional non-local traffic poses a community safety risk.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1       Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                         SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                      11 August 2025                       Page | 262
P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




7.3               Key Impacts and Risks
7.3.1 Pre-Construction Phase
As the settlements are far from the project construction site, no impact on Community Health, Safety and Security is
envisaged in the pre-construction phase.
7.3.2 Construction Phase
The main impacts during the construction phase will be from high labour influx and increase in traffic density due to
transportation of construction materials and manpower. The local population will be moderately vulnerable to threats
as a result of impacts on their safety, health and safety, ambient noise levels; mobility of people and services; access to
property, economic activity, social services; disruption of services, etc. Construction workers may potentially bring
infectious diseases into the community.
P11-1 Impacts due to Labour Influx
The project is expected to employ over 7,000 people during peak construction period. Although contractors for the
project will be encouraged to recruit workers from the local community, it is expected that major portion of the workers
will be from outside, keeping in mind the low skill set of the local population. In addition to that there will be addition of
other people such as vendors, shop owners etc further increasing the population in the area. This substantial increase in
population in an area with low population density will place considerable strain on local resources, infrastructure, and
social dynamics.
In addition to the workers, there may be also other Project Induced population immigration of unemployed or
underemployed people looking for job opportunities. This group will be unorganized and will seek residence in the nearby
villages, further increasing the strain on local infrastructure and resources as well as increasing the risk of social tension.
The concentration of such large number of workers/populations in these areas is likely to result in several community
health and safety issues, including:
  • P11-1.1: Risk of conflicts between local community and the workers, especially near to the market areas such as
    Uch Terek and Kara-Jigach, which are the main market areas nearest to the construction site. Free movement of
    migrant workers from different cultural backgrounds in the local communities may increase risks of gender-based
    violence and sexual harassment, exploitation and abuse.
      P11-1.2: The workers will need to utilize the local markets in Kara Jaigach and Uchterek for daily necessities, which
      will put pressure on local resources. For larger shopping or bulk purchasing, workers will need to travel to Toktogul,
      with Kazarman being less accessible alternative due to road conditions. Strain on public services and infrastructure,
      especially in terms of drinking water, which is scarce in this area.
  • P11-1.3: Increased risk of infections and diseases, including:
     − Waterborne diseases associated with poor sanitary conditions at construction sites and in workers’
       accommodation.
     − Sexually transmitted infections and HIV/AIDS.
  • P11-1.4: Increased risk of gender-based violence, sexual exploitation and abuse, sexual harassment.
P11-2 Impacts due to Employment and Deployment of Security Personnel
The project will hire or contract security personnel to protect their employees, facilities, assets, and operations. While
the baseline safety and security situation in the project area is low risk, experience around the world shows that tensions
between such large projects and communities over any issues can quickly and easily become a security issue. Small
conflicts over any type of environmental or social concern—water, lack of perceived benefits, pollution, or working
conditions—can suddenly turn into a protest at the project gates or a blockade of an access road. The proper actions of
security personnel in the context of worker gatherings or strikes or resettlement of households is also essential to
ensuring the rights and safety of local communities and to the reputation and operations of developers, contractors or
lenders.
The hiring and use of security personnel (potentially from outside of the local communities) can result may result in the
following community health, safety and security issues:
  • P11-2.1: Disproportional and/or excessive use of force by security personnel, threatening the liberty and safety of
    community members or workers, or leading to community unrest and tension.


 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                      Page | 263
P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




  • P11-2.2: Risk of conflicts between local community and security personnel, similar to P11-1.1.
P11-3 Impacts due to Increased Traffic Volume
There will be drastic increase in the traffic volume on the local roads, including the North-South Highway, due to heavy
vehicles carrying raw materials such as cement, fly-ash, stone aggregates, steel, etc for construction as well as
machineries for the Power Plant. It is envisaged that materials will be coming from the directions of Bishkek as well as
Osh. The impacts expected due to incremental traffic movement are as follows:
  • P11-3.1: Increased risk of accidents involving residents.
  • P11-3.2: Health impact due to harmful effects of dust emissions, noise, vibration from vehicle movements.




Figure 14: North–South Highway Segment and Traffic Monitoring Points

P11-4 Impacts from Construction Activities
  • P11-4.1: There will be major excavations at the Plant construction site, which may be a safety issue for community
    people and animals. Although there are no settlements near to the site, there are movements of people near these
    areas mainly to access pastures. Open trenches may pose a safety risk to the public if not managed properly.
  • P11-4.2: Remains of building materials, debris, cuttings and other waste can create obstacles and nuisances for
    local populations and block natural drainage and irrigation channels.
  • P11-4.3: The construction activities will involve transportation and handling of various hazardous materials such as
    fuels and inflammable gases such as acetylene and LPG. These hazardous materials and waste, if not handled and
    disposed of properly, may pose a risk to the environment and to human health and safety.
  • P11-4.4: Stone mining activities for aggregates will be done at the vicinity of the construction site. As these activities
    will be mainly done on the slopes of the mountains and there are trails used by the locals in these areas, there may
    be safety concerns leading to accidents, if the areas are not properly demarcated and barricaded. Mining activities
    can also have significant environmental impacts such as noise, vibration, dust, smoke and ground disturbance and
    consequently health issues.




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                    Page | 264
P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




7.3.3 Operation and Maintenance Phase
  • P11-5: During the O&M Phase, the main concern will be safety issues related to access to pastureland and other service
    delivery systems. The setting up of a reservoir of about 80km will increase the risk of community safety and security.
    During operations, deployment of security guards may lead to excessive or disproportionate use of force on
    communities in cases of conflict.



8. Mitigation and Monitoring
This section deals with the measures to be adopted to mitigate the adverse community health, safety and security
impacts identified due to the Project. The monitoring mechanisms to be implemented are also described in this section.


8.1               Mitigation
The mitigation measures suggested to alleviate the identified impacts on community health, safety and security is
summarised below.
Mitigation for Impacts due to Labour Influx
Some of the measures to mitigate the adverse impacts of labour influx are as given below:
  • Conducting training and capacity building activities including an Induction Training which will include information
    on (1) prevention of infectious diseases and sexually transmitted infections, (2) prohibition of sexual exploitation,
    violence and harassment, (3) respect for the privacy of residents, especially women, (4) familiarization with local
    cultural practices, rituals and heritage.
  • Code of conduct for workers should be developed by the Contractor and workers should be oriented during
    trainings.
  • Workers' accommodation areas shall be adequately provided by the Contractor so that there are no requirements
    of staying with local communities. The accommodation should be in line with EBRD norms. The provision of good
    quality accommodation, services and facilities is likely to reduce the need for mixing with the local population.
  • All persons residing in the local community shall be transported to and from the site daily by the contractor's
    transport in accordance with the worker transit plan. Transportation of migrant workers should also be arranged,
    to avoid use of public transport.
  • Sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment shall not be tolerated. The Contractor shall work with management
    leaders, local women's committees and district authorities to ensure that any complaints are dealt with in
    accordance with the law.
  • Conduct pre-employment health screenings of all the workers including checks for communicable diseases like
    tuberculosis, sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) etc.
  • Conduct regular health check-ups of workers to ensure that they are not carriers of any infectious diseases.
  • A ban on hunting of wild and/or domestic animals (hunting/catching/fishing) shall be imposed for all project
    personnel.
  • Deployment of proper law enforcement and community engagement strategies to prevent crime and social unrest.
    Implement measures like security personnel, community policing, and conflict resolution programs.
  • Maintain a structured communication framework by organizing regular and periodic meetings with key
    stakeholders, including local community members, representatives from the local Kanish (village elders), Aiyl
    Okmotu (village administration), and other relevant local governance bodies.
  • A formal grievance mechanism to be established to allow community members to report any concerns or issues
    related to the project. This mechanism will ensure that grievances are addressed promptly and transparently and
    will be regularly reviewed by the project management team. This will be integrated as part of the Project level
    GRM.
Mitigation for Impacts due to Employment and Deployment of Security Personnel
       •     Assess level and types of security risks that may require action by security personnel and develop proportional
             preventative measures, security responses, arrangement and operating procedures. This should be done in a



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                   Page | 265
P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




             continuing and adaptive manner.
       •     Conduct vetting of security personnel and/or companies for history of past abuse or dishonesty.
       •     Include relevant policies, procedures and Codes of Conduct in contractual agreements for security personnel
             and/or companies that provide clear guidance.
       •     Train all security personnel, companies and other staff who may have security-adjacent functions on
             appropriate behavior and use of force.
       •     Equip security personnel to do their jobs properly and safely (uniform, identification, communication device,
             and non-lethal weapons, if necessary and appropriate)
       •     Monitor security personnel performance to ensure that policies and procedures remain relevant and that
             personnel are aware of and following them.
       •     Engage and communicate with public security authorities and local communities as needed
       •     Assess, manage and respond to any security-related incidents, accidents, and grievances received through the
             project grievance mechanism
Mitigation for Impacts due to Increased Traffic Volume
  • Maintaining roads in good condition and set a speed limit of 20 km/h in the road intersections of populated areas.
    Before initiation of the construction activities, identification of ‘black zones’, where the risk of accidents is more,
    should be identified.
  • Installing speed breakers, traffic signages, streetlights at major intersections and traffic signals at busy intersections
    near to the site as there will be congregation of vehicles at this point.
  • Engaging traffic marshals in populated areas near to the site for proper traffic management.
  •   Training all drivers in safe driving rules and techniques
  •   Monitoring compliance with the maximum axle load depending on the type of road
  •   Performing regular maintenance of vehicles to reduce pollutions and installing appropriate safety equipment (cell phone, first
      aid kit, fire extinguisher, automatic reversing alarm, etc.). All vehicles should have ‘Pollution Under Control’ certificates.
  •   Planning the movement of transport and equipment (in columns, if applicable) considering the daily routine of the population
      living along the roads.
  •   Inform the population about the expected movements of vehicles.
  •   Avoid peak hours when transporting hazardous materials to minimize associated risks.
  •   Scheduling the movement of oversized vehicles carrying Plant equipment to avoid creating traffic congestion and checking
      roads before carrying out such movements.
  •   Water sprinkling in populated areas along the routes for dust suppression.

Mitigation for Impacts due to Construction Activities
  • The EPC Contractor shall ensure that all areas where construction is in progress is properly barricaded with
    signages.
  • Appropriate deployment of security personnel to restrict unauthorized persons from entering the active
    construction zones.
  • Implement a code of conduct for workers, including the restriction of workers to designated areas, a ban on littering,
    lighting fires, trespassing and a ban on living on construction sites.
  • Elimination of hazardous conditions on construction sites that cannot be effectively controlled by site access
    restrictions, such as closing openings to small, confined spaces, providing escape routes for larger openings such as
    trenches or excavations, or enclosing hazardous materials.
  • Measures should be taken to prevent the spread of disease vectors at work sites and workers camps to ensure that
    they do not spread to the communities.
  • The Contractors shall have their own Emergency Response Plans, in line with the Overall Emergency Response Plan of
    the Project, for risks specific to their work type and area.
Mitigation for Impacts due to Blasting
  • The timings and schedule for blasting should be provided to the communities well in advance.
  • Controlled blasting will be done. Water sprays, dust suppressants or coatings should be used to reduce dust



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                            Page | 266
P11: Community Health, Safety and Security Management Plan




      emissions from the blast site and access roads. Blast mats, berms or barriers should be used to reduce noise and
      vibration.
  •   Avoidance of blasting in windy conditions to prevent the spread of hazardous materials.
  •   Deployment of security near blasting sites to avoid people from straying into the area during blasting operation.
      Proper warning signals to be given and the significance of the signals should be conveyed to the people during
      community engagements.
The details of the mitigation measures and monitoring protocol are provided below.

9. Community Complaints and Grievance Redress
   Mechanism
The Grievance Redressal Mechanism for this Project has been developed based on the Law of the Kyrgyz
Republic “On the procedure for considering citizens' appeals” dated May 4, 2007 No. 67 and the WB ESS10
“Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure” requirements. It will be a two-level process as given
below:
Level 1 - Local Level: At the village level (Aiyl Okmotu) local contact persons will receive/register grievances from
applicants and inform the K1-PMO. After consideration of grievances through discussion with the relevant K1-
PMO staff and local self-government authorities, response will be given to the complainant within 14 working
days. If the complainant is not satisfied and/or the issue cannot be resolved at the first level of the GRM, the
complaint is escalated to the second (central) level.
Level 2 – Central Level: At the second level, the K1-PMO responsible staff will receive and register the complaint
and forward it to the K1PMO Director. The K1-PMO director will assign the PMO staff responsible for resolution
of the complaint and along with other relevant staff, they will review the compliant and take a decision. A
response will be provided to the complainant in writing within 14 working days. Complaints requiring a collegial
decision will be reviewed by a GRM committee established by the MoE.
The grievance can be filed in person through a form which will be available at a designated place in the Aiyl
Okmotu, or through a representative, by telephone, by text message, by email or through a website designated
for grievance redressal. All grievances filed will be officially entered in a register and all the further processes till
the decision will be recorded.
The GRM classifies grievances and appeals into 10 distinct categories. This classification system is used to
register and track grievances systematically and ensures that the complaint reaches the right person
immediately to avoid delays in the redressal process.
Sexual Exploitation and Abuse/Sexual Harassment Grievances
A clear and transparent GRM process for receiving, investigating, and resolving Sexual Exploitation and
Abuse/Sexual Harassment (SEA/SH) grievances is set out in the Labor Management Plan (LMP). The GRM will
ensure access and confidentiality for the consideration of special grievances, allowing complainants to voice
their concerns without fear of retaliation. These grievances will be investigated promptly, and all responsible
parties will be held accountable. The K1-PMO will appoint SEA/SH focal points within the PMO and among
contractors to oversee the implementation of SEA/SH prevention measures and handle grievances.
The details of the Grievance Redressal Mechanism is provided in the Document ‘Stakeholder Engagement Plan
and Grievance Redressal Mechanism’.

10. Training and Capacity Development
   Programs
Arrangements will be made to provide training on risks & impacts on community health, safety and security due
to the K1 HPP Project. Training will be imparted to all working personnel including security personnel, and it will
be ensured that Contractors also provide similar training to their own personnel. Periodically and as required,
refresher trainings will also be provided. The training will cover relevant aspects such as information on
prevention of infectious diseases and sexually transmitted infections, prohibition of sexual exploitation, violence
and harassment, respect for the privacy of residents, especially women and familiarization with local cultural
practices, rituals and heritage.
Some of the trainings planned are as given below:
  • Training to workers: There will be two types of training for workers. The first will be an Induction Training
    which will cover all aspects of OHS and CHS including cultural sensitivity, gender-based violence, infectious
    disease and SEA/SH. In continuation to this, refresher trainings will also be conducted periodically on the

      same or any emergent issues. The training will be provided to on-roll workers as well as contractual
      workers.
  • Training to Security Personnel: Training for security personnel will cover the above aspect in addition to
    issues related to human rights and also sensitization on the needs of the community. Furthermore, aspects
    such as behaviour with community people, limited use of force in cases of conflict, methods of conflict
    resolution will be covered in these trainings.

1.                   INTRODUCTION
1.1                  Project Overview
Kyrgyz Republic has significant hydropower potential due to its mountainous terrain and abundant water
resources, particularly from the Naryn River and its tributaries. The country is estimated to have a hydropower
potential of 142 billion kWh annually (ITA, 20249), positioning it to become a major energy producer in the
Central Asia. However, currently, only about 10% of this potential has been developed, resulting in an installed
capacity of around 4,018 MW10.
The proposed Kambarata-1 Hydropower Plant (K-1 HPP) will be the largest hydropower project in the Kyrgyz
Republic and broader Central Asia region, with a capacity of ~1,880 MW and potential energy generation of
~5,961 GWh, which is ~50 % of the current installed capacity in the country. K-1 HPP, once operational, is
envisaged to improve the energy and food security of the Kyrgyz Republic while enhancing the country’s energy
export revenue through the distribution of surplus power to other Central Asian countries, including Uzbekistan,
Tajikistan and Pakistan. The large reservoir of K-1 HPP on the Naryn River is likely to improve the operation of
the Toktogul HPP reservoir, which can provide a much-needed balance to the water management regime in the
Naryn–Syr Darya Basin. The proposed project will also emerge as crucial step in fulfilling the government’s vision
for a green and affordable energy future.
The World Bank has agreed to finance project in accordance and in full compliance with the Bank’s
Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) comprising, inter alia, the Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs).
Ministry of Energy (MoE)/Project Management Office (PMO) has hired AFRY and SMEC to update the Feasibility
Study and undertake detailed Environment & Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) and prepare managements plans,
respectively.
This Labor Influx Management Plan (LIMP) for Kambarata-1 HPP is being prepared as part of Environmental and
Social Management Plan (ESMP), to be aligned with the requirements of Environmental and Social Standard
(ESS) 2: Labor and Working Conditions.


1.2                  Project Description
The proposed site for the K-1 HPP is located in the middle reaches of the Naryn River, about 14.5 km upstream
of the existing Kambarata-2 HPP dam, in the Toktogul District of Jalalabad Region. It is located in a gorge where
the river makes a large loop around the Karabash Mountain, first southward for more than 1 km, and then east-
westward for more than 1.5 km, prior to tracking in north-towards the mouth of the Termirkent Sai, a right-bank
tributary. The dam site is located at approximately at coordinates 41°46’14”Nand 73°26’30”E.




9
    International Trade Administration, 2024: Kyrgyz Republic - Energy
10 2024 World Hydropower Outlook, International Hydropower Association

                                                           Figure 15: Project Location

The proposed Project consists of the following main elements:
•        Curved Roller-compacted concrete gravity dam of 10 m wide at the crest having maximum height above
         the riverbed of 260.8 m.
•        A reservoir area of 61.7 km2 at a full supply level of 1,198 msl. The inundated area extends approximately
         68 km upstream from the Dam wall.
•        An overground powerhouse including Francis-type turbines with a total electricity generating capacity of
         1,880 MW.
•        The permanent resettlement of persons temporarily and permanently residing and pastures, structures,
         assets and sources of livelihood within the proposed inundation area. Also, restoration of access and
         social infrastructure affected.
•        The construction of temporary and permanent access roads and bridge structures within the Dam site.
•        The construction of water pipelines and tunnels.
•        Quarry sites to source raw materials for the Dam.
•        Temporary concrete batching plant, materials storage, waste disposal, construction sites and workforce
         accommodation facilities.
•        Permanent and temporary accommodation facilities for Dam and hydropower operations and
         maintenance personnel.
•        The peak construction labor force is estimated at 7,000 workers onsite. The estimated construction
         timeframe is estimated to be 8.5 years.
•        The early works and associated facilities -AF- (i.e. a 5.6 km access road to the K-1 site to the existing road
         network; a bridge over the Naryn River; temporary work camp; a 23.6 km long 110 kV overhead
         transmission line; and a 110/6 kV substation). According to the World Bank’s definition, the associated

    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                     11 August 2025                 Page | 283

         facilities must meet three criteria. That is, these facilities must be- (a) directly and significantly related to
         the Project; and (b) carried out, or planned to be carried out, contemporaneously with the Project; and
         (c) necessary for the Project to be viable and would not have been constructed, expanded or conducted if
         the Project did not exist.
•        It is important to consider that, in preparation to the project, a workers' camp has been built on land
         owned by the electricity company to accommodate workers for preparatory work such as access roads
         and a cement plant. The location of the workers' camp is still under consideration (a possible location is
         Kambarata village, 30–35 km from the dam).


1.3                  Purpose of the LIMP
The LIMP for the K-1 HPP and Associated Facilities has two key goals, as follows:
•        Effectively manage the influx of workers associated with the project while mitigating potential social,
         economic, environmental, and health-related impacts on the host communities.
•        Maximize project benefits for local communities by creating local jobs and providing training
         opportunities. This aligns with community expectations expressed during the public consultations. The
         majority of respondents (41%) stated that the construction would create new job opportunities in the
         region and help the local youths find jobs. Meanwhile, 24% expected improvements to local
         infrastructure, and around 23% believed it would contribute to rebuilding the local economy.
This plan aligns with the World Bank’s ESF, particularly ESS 2 (Labor and Working Conditions) and ESS 4
(Community Health and Safety), ensuring that labor-related risks are proactively addressed and local socio-
economic benefits among affected and local communities are enhanced. As the project involves major
construction activities involved, the magnitude of labor force required shall also be significant.
Key objectives of the LIMP are to:
•        Ensure responsible labor recruitment and management
        o Reduce labor influx by using local workforce wherever possible, promoting local hiring;
         Enhance local training/capacity/skills gradually, particularly targeting women and vulnerable groups of
         population as per Gender, Vulnerability and Inclusion Action Plan (GVIAP), considered to be critical labor
         management practice in project with long project cycle;
        o Prevent exploitative labor practices;
•        Assess and manage labor influx risks through appropriate instruments
        o     Assess the risks beforehand and review project sponsor’s capacity to mitigate it;
        o     Development of site-specific LIMP under the broader scheme presented in subject document;
        o     Development of Worker’s Camp Management Plan.
•        Minimise local price inflation;
•        Mitigate risks associated with labor influx, including sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA), sexual
         harassment (SH) risks;
•        Protect community health, safety, and resources;
•        Implement an effective Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM), consistent with the one included in the
         project’s Labour Management Plan (LMP) and Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); and
•        Monitor and enforce compliance with labor and social standards.

2.                CONTEXT AND RISK ASSESSMENT
2.1               Introduction
As noted above, this LIMP is an extension of the project’s LMP and applies to project workers as defined by ESS2 who
are employed directly by the project at project offices and on the construction sites and employees of contractors and
service providers, including part time, temporary, seasonal or migrant workers. Employees of direct suppliers to the
project are considered as primary supply workers and are covered by provisions relating to prevention of child labor,
forced labor and safety issues. It may be noted that project construction aspects and related workforce details will only
be available at the later stage, this LIMP and LMP (standalone document), in accordance with ESS2, provides estimates
only of the anticipated number, types and characteristics of the project worker force. 11
Similarly, the anticipated contracting structure for the project and the likely number of project workers to be employed
or engaged by each contractor/subcontractor is not known at this time. Once the relevant information is available, the
LIMP will be updated accordingly. Additionally, as already stated above, site specific LIMP as part of C-ESMP shall be
prepared by the contractors as the project progresses.

2.1.1             Socio-Economic Condition of Project Area
During the first round of consultations, local stakeholders emphasised job creation and training opportunities as key
priorities. Their feedback was provided through surveys, interviews, and focus group discussions, and it highlighted the
need for employment initiatives. However, this will be challenging due to the low population density and average low
qualification levels in the host area.
The project is located in a traditional Agro pastoralist region, where agriculture and livestock farming have long been
the primary economic activities. Considering that, it is crucial that the LIMP incorporates international best practices for
local hiring and workforce training.
Primary data collected in October–November 2024 indicates that the project area has a basic education infrastructure,
enabling children to access fundamental education. However, there is a significant gap in the availability of skilled and
vocational education. The region lacks vocational institutions, which forces youths interested in pursuing such courses
to travel to cities like Bishkek and Osh. This travel option is financially burdensome for many, creating a significant
barrier to accessing vocational training and skill development opportunities.
In majority of focus group discussions, local unemployment emerged as a major concern, particularly for rural women
and youth. Women in villages face limited employment opportunities, with many primarily engaged as housewives and
involved in domestic duties. While some women have begun exploring income-generating activities such as sewing,
they lack the necessary resources and support. As for the youth, there is a strong preference to leave the villages in
search of better employment opportunities in urban centres, such as Bishkek and Osh.
In addition, during initial consultations, some local communities expressed interest in hosting a workers’ camp, viewing
it as a potential development opportunity. This sentiment was particularly strong in Nickhe-Sai Village, where residents
mentioned designated land for establishing a workers' town/camp. Villagers anticipated that the camp would generate
economic opportunities for the local area.
It is also important to note that a former workers’ camp from the Kambarata-2 Hydropower Project (K2 HPP) was located
in Ketmen-tobo. The municipality reports that this village was established in 1991 by Soviet authorities to accommodate
40,000 people, but it peaked at 5,000 during the Kambarata-2 HPP construction. Many workers settled here after the
project, and no social conflicts, including sexual violence, occurred during that period. Today, the population has
declined due to limited job opportunities, though plans exist to relocate 500 families from a nearby town. Infrastructure
maintenance remains difficult due to limited public funds, and high costs have led to a shift from electric to coal heating.
The village has a new school, but key public facilities, such as the medical centre and the postal office, require

11
    The project will not engage community labor or security forces. However, while employing security personnel, the Borrower will
(i) make reasonable inquiries to verify that the security personnel employed by the Borrower to provide security are not implicated in past
abuses; (ii) train them adequately (or determine that they are properly trained) in the use of force (and where applicable, firearms), and
appropriate conduct toward workers
and affected communities; and
(iii) require them to act within the applicable law and any requirements set out in the ESCP.

 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1    Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                      SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                   11 August 2025                       Page | 285
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan



renovation. A clean water project worth 70 million Soms is underway, and an international investor plans to develop a
solar energy farm.
Local authorities have expressed strong interest in hosting the K-1 HPP workers’ camp, as the site is only 25 km from
the future K-1 HPP construction site. They report having sufficient municipal land for the camp's development, along
with public infrastructure, including a school, to accommodate workers and their families. However, some of this
infrastructure requires substantial renovation. However, at the time of preparing this LIMP, no decision had been made
on the workers camp location.

2.1.2                Demographic Details of Project area
Project Area
K-1 HPP is located in Toktogul district of Jalal-Abad Region in western Kyrgyzstan. Total population of Toktogul is 103,000
residents. It is located in the middle reaches of Naryn River, 14.5 km upstream from the existing dam of Kambarata-2
HPP, in a gorge where Naryn River loops around Temirkent Mountain. It is located at some 20 km from the top end of
Toktogul Reservoir.
The main infrastructure, including the dam and power generation facilities, is situated in Toktogul, a district of Jalal-
Abad Region in western Kyrgyzstan. However, given the scale and scope of project, some impacts (during and post
construction) and plausible mitigation measures might also include other districts of Jalal-Abad Region. However, same
is not clear at this stage and hence for the sake of assessing broader socio-economic conditions, document has
considered that impact from subject dam is concentrated in district of Toktogul only.
An area of 61.7 square kilometres expected to be impounded by the K1 HPP reservoir is located in a low population
area. However, the area to be impounded by K1 HPP plays a central role in managing pastures. For generations,
shepherd families have moved with their herds from the surrounding villages to the natural pastures located in the
project area, where they stay for several months: in some areas of the valley from April to May (spring pastures); from
October to November (autumn pastures); in others, from October to April (winter pastures, which are the most valuable
for their livelihoods), and the summer (Highland pastures). This is the main livelihood and source of income for most of
communities living in close vicinity of the project area. The major concern of the landowners / communities are that the
project shall lead them to lose most of the pastures they are using.
Broader socio-economic trends of Jalal- Abad region has also been assessed for better analysis and are presented herein:
Population size and density12 in the project area
•        As of January 1, 2023, the Jalal-Abad Region in Kyrgyzstan has a population of 1,311,007. The region spans an
         area of 32,418 square kilometres, resulting in a population density of approximately 40.4 inhabitants per square
         kilometre.
•        The Toktogul District, located within the Jalal-Abad Region, covers an area of 7,815 square kilometers. According
         to the National Statistical Committee of the Kyrgyz Republic (NSC) (2025), as of early 2024, the district's
         population was 107,707, leading to a population density of about 13.8 inhabitants per square kilometre.
Figure 16 shows the project location is very sparsely populated, more so the affected district. Hence labour influx will
be a critical issue to manage with low skill levels of the local population coupled with low population density.

2.1.2.1              Age distribution
As of early 2024, the Toktogul District in Kyrgyzstan has the following age distribution:
•        Total Population: 107,707
•        Youth Population (0-14 years): 40,853 (approximately 37.9%)
•        Working-Age Population (15-64 years): 62,793 (approximately 58.3%)
•        Senior Population (65 years and above): 4,061 (approximately 3.8%)
This district comprises a predominantly young population, with over one-third aged 14 or younger. The K1 HPP will
require a substantial amount of local population workforce, hence there is potential for school dropout to increase as
the contractors/ sub-contractors may also try to encourage / exploit this segment of population for cheaper workforce.
Dropping out from schools for temporary unskilled jobs also has potential implications in long term for the economy. In
order to prevent such adverse impacts, strict adherence to labor laws13, and Project Labor Management Plan (LMP) is
required. Additionally, the Labour Influx Management Plan (LIMP) includes activities in local communities to encourage
regular awareness drives (among schools and parents) towards skill upgrade programmes / internship with the project
as required.

does not interfere with education, individuals aged 14 and 15 may be employed with parental consent.

2.1.2.2              Gender distribution

              Jalal-Abad Region:

•        Total Population: Approximately 1,292,420 (as of 2022)14
•        Male Population: 649,897 (50.3%)
•        Female Population: 642,523 (49.7%)
•        Female employment: 34% (as of 2023)15

              Toktogul District:

•        Total Population: 107,707 (as of 2024)16
•        Male Population: 54,362 (50.5%)
•        Female Population: 53,345 (49.5%)
•        Female employment: not specified for Toktogul, however, in Osh, it was recorded at 54.2% (as of 2023) 17
In 2021, the female labor force participation rate in Kyrgyzstan was 45.4%, compared to 74.7% for men that jumped to
53.5% of women in Kyrgyzstan were in the labor force, compared to 79% of men in 2023.
Although there is no significant social boundary restricting female members from the society to actively enter the job
market in the region, structural boundaries still exists. Same is being reflected in the overall labor force participation
rate in Kyrgyzstan. This aspect can be specifically addressed in the GVIAP which targets increasing female workforce
participation on the K-1 HPP.

2.1.3                Education & Skill Levels
2.1.3.1              Literacy rates and school enrolment levels
During the primary data collection as part of ESIA, all 337 respondents provided feedback regarding the educational
institutions in their respective villages and Aiyl Aimak (AA). The distances to schools varied significantly across the
different areas surveyed:
•        In Atai AA, 90% of respondents reported schools within 0 to 1500 meters.
•        In Sary-Kamysh AA, 48% said schools are within 0 to 1500 meters.
•        In Imanaliev AA, 47.3% stated the school is within 0 to 1500 meters.
•        In Toluk AA, 72.9% said the school is within 0 to 1500 meters.
This data demonstrates that while access to schools is generally convenient in some areas, other areas face challenges
related to longer distances to educational institutions.
However, of all respondents across the 4 AAs, an overwhelming 95.8% expressed satisfaction with the educational
services provided by the local schools. However, 4.2% of residents voiced dissatisfaction with these services, with the
highest rate of negative responses recorded from Imanaliev AA. Among those who were dissatisfied, the predominant
concern was the lack of adequate space and facilities for students, highlighting the need for new school buildings to
accommodate the growing demand and improve the quality of education in the area.
Regarding Pre School-Institutions, 74.9% were satisfied with the preschool institutions' services in the village. 23.6% of
local residents expressed dissatisfaction with such services (a higher percentage of negative responses came from
Imanaliev AA). Among those local residents dissatisfied with the quality of preschool services, most indicated that a new
building for children's garden is necessary in the village since there are no conditions or space available.

P12: Labor Influx Management Plan



Based on the data provided, it is evident that the project area has basic education infrastructure in place, and children
are accessing fundamental education. However, when it comes to skilled and vocational education, the opportunities
are limited. There are no vocational institutions in the region, requiring youths interested in such programs to travel to
Bishkek. This option is not cost-effective for many, posing a significant barrier to accessing vocational training.

2.1.3.2              Availability of Vocational Training Institutions
There are several notable institutions and initiatives in Kyrgyzstan that contribute to vocational education and training
in Toktogul district. These include:
•        Kyrgyz-German Technical Institute (KGTI) at KSTU,
•        University of Technology Kyrgyzstan Campus (UTKC),
•        DVV International in Kyrgyzstan
All of which provide avenues to upgrade the skills of local population.
Additionally, Asian Development Bank (ADB) has been supporting Kyrgyzstan's primary vocational education by
introducing competency-based training methodologies. This approach involves developing skill standards for 18
occupations identified as priorities by employers, training providers, and government policymakers and skilling local
population in it.
The approach practiced by ADB’s initiative can be adopted by the K-1 HPP to upgrade local skills and provide them with
the opportunity to rise up the ladder. This will considerably reduce the stress on local resources owing to influx of
outside labor and their followers.

2.1.3.3              Skill Gaps in the Local Labor Market
With limited information at this stage in the Project, it is difficult assess these market gaps precisely. However, based
on limited studies already undertaken it can be safely assumed that there is considerable lack of ‘technical proficiency’
and ‘vocational training alignment’ in the project area. Lack of basic technical proficiency result in low productivity and
compromised work quality and increased dependency on external skilled labor.
The Project will create a need for more diversified employment in the area, which has the potential to lead to semi-
skilled/unskilled workers posing as skilled workers. This could result in safety issues due to unskilled handling of tools
and machinery. To minimise such impacts, the following steps are proposed to be undertaken during preparation of
site-specific contractor’s LIMP:
       •      Conduct localized skill assessment in collaboration with local authorities and community leaders to map the
              existing skill base
       •      Develop targeted training programmes drawing from the above assessment exercise
       •      Foster industry partnership including representation from construction firms, equipment suppliers, and other
              relevant industry players
         Promote continuous learning
Addressing the lack of technical proficiency and the misalignment of vocational training is critical to ensuring
sustainable employment, efficient project execution, and harmonious community engagement. The actions/provisions
outlined above under the contractor’s LIMP not only mitigate risks associated with labour influx, resistance and social
unrest but also contribute to local capacity building, social cohesion and long-term socio-economic development and
overall prosperity of the population of the region.
