2.1.4                Economic Indicators
2.1.4.1              Major Economic Activities and Unemployment Rates
The Jalal-Abad Region and Toktogul District in Kyrgyzstan have diverse economic activities shaped by their unique
geographical features and resources.
Jalal-Abad Region:
•        Energy Production: The region is a significant energy hub, with approximately 43% of its industrial output
         attributed to energy production. This prominence is due to the presence of major hydroelectric power plants,
         including the Toktogul Hydroelectric Power Plant (HPP), which is the largest in the country.
•        Metallurgy and Petroleum: Metallurgical industries and petroleum processing contribute around 35% to the
         region's industrial production, highlighting their importance in the local economy. 18
•        Agriculture: Agriculture plays a vital role, with initiatives aimed at enhancing crop cultivation and harvesting
         methods. However, it may be noted that this doesn’t has the major share in Regional economy.
Toktogul District:
•        Hydroelectric Power Generation: The Toktogul HPP, located on the Naryn River, is a cornerstone of the district's
         economy, generating approximately 40% of Kyrgyzstan's electricity. Recent modernization efforts have increased
         its capacity, underscoring its critical role in the national energy sector. 19
Recently, government is attempting to diversify the economy and considering the potential for tourism industry and
other related service sector, policy focus has shifted towards it.
Regarding unemployment rates, according to the Socio-economic profile of Toktogul District (2024), Toktogul district
recorded 1,746 officially registered unemployed individuals, resulting in an unemployment rate of 3.09%.

2.1.4.2              Cost of living index (housing, food, transport)
Average monthly expenses of Jalal-Abad and Toktogul is approximately 604-607 dollars, which is 1.27 – 1.26 times less
expensive than the national average in Kyrgyzstan. Toktogul is among top 18% of least expensive cities. Thus, cost of
living in the area shall not be an issue. However, any significant influx of worker and follower, coupled with high
transportation cost in mountainous terrain, shall add to the inflation in the local market. In proceeding chapters, LIMP
shall also include mitigation measure to counter this effect.

2.1.4.3              Presence of informal labor markets
Absence of a formal labor market and pre-dominance of informal agreements in the labor market is a common trend in
the region. This social trend further puts the local population open to exploitative practices.

2.1.5                Social & Community Dynamics
2.1.5.1              Ethnic and Cultural Composition
The Jalal-Abad Region is characterised by its diverse ethnic makeup. While specific statistics for the region and Toktogul
District are limited, the broader national context indicates a mosaic of ethnic groups, including Kyrgyz, Uzbeks, Russians,
and others. This diversity contributes to a rich cultural tapestry, influencing local traditions, languages, and communal
interactions.

2.1.5.2              Social Cohesion and History of Tensions/Conflicts
Kyrgyzstan has experienced periods of ethnic tension, notably the clashes in the southern regions in 2010. While Jalal-
Abad was among the affected areas, efforts have since been directed toward reconciliation and strengthening social
cohesion. Community-based initiatives and international support have played roles in fostering dialogue and rebuilding
trust among diverse ethnic groups.

19 timesca.com+1adb.org+1

2.1.5.3              Local Governance and Leadership Structures
Local governance in Kyrgyzstan operates through a decentralized system, with regional and district administrations
overseeing local affairs. In the Jalal-Abad Region and Toktogul District, local councils (Kenesh) and appointed officials
manage administrative functions, development projects, and community welfare programs. Traditional community
leaders and elders often hold significant influence, especially in rural areas, guiding social norms and conflict resolution.

2.1.5.4              Community Attitudes Toward Outsiders/Migrant Workers
Community attitudes toward outsiders and migrant workers can vary based on economic conditions, cultural
perceptions, and individual experiences. In regions with high levels of out-migration, such as parts of Kyrgyzstan,
returning migrants and newcomers can face challenges integrating into local communities. Efforts to promote social
cohesion emphasize the importance of inclusive policies, community engagement, and addressing potential sources of
tension. Understanding and improving these dynamics are crucial for fostering harmonious communities.

2.1.6                Health & Safety Indicators
General insights from national statistics and global health resources are presented herein below:

2.1.6.1              Availability of Healthcare Facilities
National Overview: Kyrgyzstan's healthcare system comprises hospitals, clinics, and primary healthcare centres. The
Jalal-Abad Region and Toktogul District have a network of healthcare facilities to serve the population. However, owing
to dispersed settlements and migrant nature of significant population, this may not be sufficient.

2.1.6.2              Prevalence of Communicable Disease
•        HIV/AIDS: Kyrgyzstan has a relatively low HIV prevalence, but there has been an upward trend in recent years.
•        Tuberculosis (TB): The country faces challenges with TB, including multidrug-resistant strains. National programs
         are in place to address this issue.
•        Malaria: Kyrgyzstan has achieved significant progress in malaria control, with no indigenous cases reported in
         recent years.

2.1.6.3              Health Workforce Capacity
National Statistics: The density of healthcare professionals varies across regions. Rural areas often experience shortages
of medical staff.

2.1.7                Gender & Vulnerability Assessments
2.1.7.1              Women's Participation in the Labor Force
National Overview: Kyrgyzstan has made strides in promoting gender equality in the workforce. According to the
National Statistical Committee of the Kyrgyz Republic, the labor force participation rate for women aged 15 and above
was approximately 46.3% in recent years and has seen significant improvement in recent years.

2.1.7.2              Risks of Gender-Based Violence (GBV) and Exploitation
GBV remains a significant concern in Kyrgyzstan. Reports from organizations like Human Rights Watch indicate that
domestic violence and bride kidnapping are persistent issues.

2.1.7.3              Prevalence of Child Labor and Forced Labor
•        Child Labor: While exact figures are limited, sectors like agriculture have reported instances of child labor. The
         government collaborates with international organizations to address this issue.
•        Forced Labor: Kyrgyzstan has been identified as a source and, to a lesser extent, a destination country for forced
         labor. Measures are being implemented to combat human trafficking and labor exploitation.



    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007

2.1.8                Broader Macro-economic and Political Challenges
Kyrgyzstan has been a democratic society since beginning but in recent times it’s been severely impacted by political
instability. In last two decades (2005–2025), the country has seen 19 heads of governments, which portrays high level
of political instability. Country’s economy heavily depends on gold production & remittances, which leaves the economy
vulnerable to external shocks. Food inflation in the region is also very high, posing serious challenge to food security. As
per many reports High food prices and job insecurity will continue to be the most significant concern for the welfare of
the population in 2023. Additionally, any further deterioration of Russian economy may lead to a decline in remittances.


2.2                  Category of Workers
In accordance with ESS2, all labor resources are divided into the following categories:

•        Direct workers;
•        Contracted workers;
•        Primary supply workers.
•        Community workers.

2.2.1                To hire staff for this Project, MoE will follow the established practices.
                     Direct workers (PMO staff)
People employed or engaged directly by the Borrower (including the project proponent and the project implementing
agencies) to work specifically in relation to the project. PMO staff (consultants) are not civil servants and will be hired
on a contract basis. The PMO will use two types of employment contracts: long-term employment contract and short-
term service contract.

 The PMO will comprise the Director and consultants (coordinators) for the three Components, including finance
specialists, procurement specialists, social specialist, environment specialists, monitoring and evaluation specialist and
other technical specialists who will coordinate Project implementation and carry out the following functions:
management of financial operations and procurements, and social and environmental risks; control, monitoring and
evaluation of project work plans execution; facilitation of cooperation between stakeholders at the central and local
levels; provision of project progress reports and ensuring that external audits of the project's financial management
and procurement procedures are conducted in a timely manner; ensuring monitoring and implementation of the
World Bank’s environmental and social standards. The PMO will also be responsible for preparation and
communication of information to all Project stakeholders.
To ensure successful implementation/coordination of the project, the project may engage additional short-term
consultants to upskill PMO, MoE, Chakan HPP, Electric Stations OJSC, and other organizations, as well as local
authorities. The Project may also engage short- term national consultants to conduct training workshops, organize
meetings and round tables, arrange annual public consultations and quarterly focus groups with the beneficiaries on
key activities related to the Project.
The Project is expected to engage:
•        Technical supervision specialists from both local community and other regions of the country (for
         example, from the area where the design organization is originally based). The Project may send its
         representatives to some Project-affected areas to provide support and oversee work implementation, as well as
         coordinate with contractors, local authorities, beneficiaries, and communities.
•        Consultants (specialists who have expertise in technical supervision) to exercise technical supervision and
         control over contractors’ (subcontractors’ and suppliers’) compliance with technological process and quality
         requirements, and make sure they complete works and services and comply with the contract terms (designs,
         terms of reference).
•        National short-term consultants to carry out institutional capacity building in the energy sector, hold training
         seminars, organize meetings on regional cooperation, monitor and evaluate work plans and Project reports.

    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 292

2.2.2             Contracted workers
Contracted workers are workers hired or engaged by a third party to perform works related to the main functions
of the Project, irrespective of the location where such works are implemented. Engineering, Procurement, and
Construction (EPC) contractors and subcontractors shall be identified during implementation of the Project. To
implement Project activities, the Project will sign agreements with contractors (whose exact number will be identified
only at the tender stage) in accordance with the World Bank Procurement Guidelines of the Project. The number of
contractors’ workers will be identified after commencement of construction/rehabilitation works. Contractors will be
selected through a competitive process guided by the World Bank procurement principles and the legislation of the
Kyrgyz Republic, and will engage subcontractors, who must be agreed with MoE represented by the PMO.
Subcontractors: To implement its activities in rural areas, the Project Contractors can engage subcontractors,
    including local private firms and non-governmental organizations. Contractors may also engage
    subcontractors for project construction and associated facilities. Subcontractors are expected to come from
    among local companies. Subcontractors will be guided by the national legislation, regulations, and the Labor
    Code of the Kyrgyz Republic.
      The legislation of the Kyrgyz Republic was updated in 2025. The new and updated Code of the Kyrgyz Republic
      No. 23 Labor Code was enacted by the Law of the Kyrgyz Republic on 24 dated January 23, 2025. The Goals and
      Objectives of Labor Legislation of the Kyrgyz Republic (as stated in Article 2) confirms the establishment of state
      guarantees of the labor rights and freedoms of citizens, the creation of favourable working conditions, and the
      protection of the rights and interests of employees and employers. The labor legislation is aimed at creating the
      necessary legal conditions for achieving optimal coordination of the interests of the parties to labor relations, as
      well as the interests of the state.
1.2.3 Primary supply workers:
A primary supply worker is a worker employed or engaged by a primary supplier, providing goods and materials directly
     to the project on an ongoing basis, over whom a primary supplier exercises control for the work, working
     conditions, and treatment of the person. Primary supply workers will be expected consisting of employees of
     third-party companies who will provide machinery and/or equipment for construction, infrastructure
     investment component of the project and employees of companies who will provide hardware, security, and
     cleaning services.
1.2.4 Community workers: Community workers will not be employed on this Project as members of the local
     community are hired by Contractors and thus are Contracted Workers.

Child labor is prohibited, and all workers will be over 18. It is assumed that the average age of workers will be 22-30
years. There will be no child or forced labor associated with this project.


2.3               Tentative Estimate of Labor Influx during Various
                  Project Cycle
According to the Kambarata-1 Feasibility Study Update (AFRY, June 2025), an estimated average of 4,622 personnel
per year will be needed over the 8.5-year construction period, with up to 7,000 personnel are expected to be
employed at the construction site during peak periods. Peak workforce—ranging from 5,000 to 7,000—anticipated
between years 4 and 8 of the construction phase. Once operational, the project will require approximately 200
personnel for the permanent operations and maintenance (O&M) team, covering all staffing levels.

In summary, the total labor engagement estimate is a Peak Workforce: 7,000 workers and Total Workers over the Entire
Project approximately 41,000 workers (rotational over ~10 years). It may be noted that in general hydro projects needs
approx. 30-50% of unskilled labor. Based on this estimate, the peak workforce is likely to involve approx. 12,000 – 20,000
unskilled workers.


2.4               Risk Assessment
The common social risks associated with labor influx are outlined below:

2.4.1             Risk of Social Conflict
Conflicts may arise between the local community and the construction workers, which may relate to various factors
including religious, cultural, or ethnic differences, or based on competition for local resources. Tensions may also
emerge within the labour force, and pre-existing conflicts in the local community may be exacerbated. Furthermore,
ethnic and regional tensions may escalate if workers from one group migrate into the territory of another.
This fragmentation may further increase (both internally as well as with the community) once the linguistic differences
come in play. As is expected, there shall be significant inward labour movement from various regions and conflicts
arising out of linguistic differences (e.g. lack of communication to diffuse tension owing to linguistic barriers, groupings,
resentments claiming favour to particular group etc.) may arise.

2.4.2             Risk of illicit behaviour that violates social norms
The influx of workers and service providers into communities may lead to an increase in crime rates and/or a heightened
perception of insecurity among local residents. This may include incidents such as theft, physical assaults, substance
abuse, prostitution, and human trafficking. Local law enforcement agencies may face challenges in adequately
addressing the potential increase in crime associated with the influx of temporary residents.

2.4.3             Influx of additional population
Projects with large footprints or longer durations may attract a significant influx of people beyond the core workforce,
increasing the risks associated with labor influx. This can include individuals seeking employment with the project, family
members of workers, and a range of service providers, including sex workers, particularly in areas with limited local
capacity to meet the increased demand for goods and services.

2.4.4             Local inflation of prices
The influx of labor can significantly increase the demand for goods and services, potentially leading to price inflation
and reduced access to essential goods and services for local community members.

2.4.5             Increased burden on and competition for public service provision
The presence of construction workers and service providers, often accompanied by their families, can significantly
increase the demand for public services, including water, electricity, medical care, transportation, education, and social
services. This strain on existing resources can be particularly pronounced when the influx of workers is not adequately
accommodated by additional or separate supply systems.
Pre-dominant issue of drinking water issue in the project area will likely further strain the available sources once labor
influx begins, likely leading to local conflicts if not comprehensively managed. Further, electrification is already an issue

 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1        Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                          SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                       11 August 2025                      Page | 295
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan



in the project area. Both issues will be addressed through a Community Benefits Sharing Pilot during Phase 1 and 2 of
the project, to ensure project benefits can be realised earlier and address equitable sustainable development of local
communities.

2.4.6                Increased risk of communicable diseases and burden on local health
                     services
The influx of people can introduce communicable diseases, including sexually transmitted infections, into the project
area, and may expose incoming workers to diseases for which they have limited immunity. This can significantly strain
local health resources. Workers with health concerns related to substance abuse, mental health issues, or sexually
transmitted infections may seek anonymous treatment at local clinics rather than utilizing the project's medical facility,
further burdening local healthcare providers. Additionally, local health and emergency services may be overwhelmed
and under-resourced to effectively respond to the potential increase in industrial accidents that can occur on a large
construction site.

2.4.7                Gender Based Violence (GBV) and Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (SEA)
                     /Sexual Harassment Risk
Construction industries are dominated by the males. Thus, significant influx of male construction workers, especially
those who are separated from their families and operating outside their usual social norms, presents a heightened risk
of social and ethical challenges. This can manifest in inappropriate and potentially criminal behaviors, such as sexual
harassment, exploitation, and illicit sexual relationships with minors within the host community. Furthermore, a large
influx of male workers can increase the vulnerability of women and girls to exploitation, including forced sex work,
driven by the increased demand for sexual services.
GBV is a known risk in Kyrgyz Republic. GBV risks related to management of project workers might include:
•        potential risks to women project workers travelling in remote areas (management of this risk should not result in
         any discouragement of employment of women in relevant capacities); and
•        potential risks of GBV committed by contractors’ workers, including those temporarily resident in site camps for
         construction works.
Although there are no official statistics available on the level and extent of sexual harassment, evidence suggests that
women are often subjected to sexual harassment at the workplace. Violence against women and girls is also a concern:
A 2019 study by the Kyrgyz Association of Women Judges revealed that approximately 25% of women have experienced
sexual harassment in their workplaces. Notably, 80% of these incidents occurred in public institutions, and 70% of the
victims were unmarried women aged between 20 and 38, typically in employee positions 20.
Given the scale of project and potentially very high migrant workers during construction phase occurrence of this risk
in the Kambara-1 HPP is quite high. The ESMP lays the first building block for addressing SEA/SH risks and provides the
appropriate umbrella framework for any proposed SEA/SH prevention and mitigation measures. This will ensure
contractors have safeguards that comply with World Bank standards, including those in the Good Practice Note on
Sexual Exploitation and Abuse and Sexual Harassment (SEA/SH).

2.4.8                Risks of unfair or discriminatory hiring practices
There is a high potential for unfair or discriminatory practices in hiring of direct project workers and in hiring of workers
by contractors and primary suppliers, especially the vulnerable workers including women workers Engagement of
community workers could also be unfair or discriminatory, particularly where community work is rewarded with
payments in cash or in kind or with other benefits. In case any community labor is employed, this aspect must be
taken into account that there is no discrimination against the community labor.




20 https://kawj.kg/report-on-the-study-on-the-prevalence-of-sexual-harassment-against-women-and-girls-in-the-workplace-and-workplace-

2019/?lang=en

    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007

    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                       Page | 296
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan




2.4.9             Increased pressure on accommodation and rents
Depending on worker income levels and the type of accommodation provided, increased demand for housing may lead
to higher rental prices and limited housing availability for local residents.

2.4.10            Increased in traffic and related accidents
The delivery of construction supplies, and the transportation of workers can contribute to increased traffic congestion
and a higher risk of accidents, placing a greater strain on existing transportation infrastructure.

2.4.11            Child Labor and School Dropout
A risk of child labor/ forced labor (in various forms) is significant in the project of this magnitude. Child labor, including
forced labor in domestic work and begging, is still prevalent in parts of the country. Children also perform dangerous
tasks in scavenging for garbage and in street work, sometimes as a result of human trafficking. This issue seems urgent
in light of following inputs – National Statical Committee of the Kyrgyz Republic in its most recent data has indicated
that approximately 24% of children aged 5-17 years were engaged in child labor. These high numbers have prompted
the government to undertake several initiatives in collaboration with ILO and UNICEF.
In the context of above figures, it is critical for project proponent to ensure that the project and its supply chain is not
involved in engaging child labor / forced labor.
Also, with increased opportunities for the host community to sell goods and services to incoming workers may
inadvertently lead to child labor, potentially contributing to higher school dropout rates.

2.4.12            Risk regarding project activities can impact / restrict community resources
The Project activities will lead to several restriction for the locals, most significantly the loss of pastures and access to
pastures (not inundated by the Project). It will be critical that Livelihood Restoration and Resettlement Plans are
efficiently implemented in local communities and monitored accordingly. Otherwise there is potential for current
project goodwill to evaporate quickly if negative livelihood impacts are realised.

2.4.13            Women and Vulnerable Groups not being provided full opportunity at
                  workplace
There is the potential for discrimination against women or members of other groups (e.g. workers with disability). In
spite of progress in legislation to reduce gender inequality and the expansion in female education, Kyrgyz Republic still
has low participation of women in the labor force, with women’s share of the labor force just above 50%
(theglobaleconomy.com).
As per data available at World Bank Gender Data Portal, the gender gap in labor force participation in the Kyrgyz
Republic is significant. In 2019, approximately 47.3 percent of Kyrgyz women ages 15 to 64 were economically active
compared to 74.5 percent of men in the same age range. Women’s employment in the Kyrgyz Republic varies greatly
by economic activity. While women represent a majority of workers in sectors such as health and social services (83.6
percent) and education (80.6 percent), they represent a minority of workers in highly paid technical sectors, such as the
production of gas, electricity, and water (only 9.5 percent in 2016). This data shows significant improvement in 2023,
53.5% of women in Kyrgyzstan were in the labor force, compared to 79% of men (Source: Kyrgyz-Republic-Gender Data
Portal-World Bank). This disparity is further enhanced by the fact that the project area lacks the required infrastructure
such child care facilities to assist women to become part of workforce.
These aspects are specifically addressed in the GVIAP which targets women and vulnerable groups gaining benefits,
including higher rates of workforce participation from the K1 HPP.

2.4.14            Preferencing local employment
Unfair labor hiring practices might fuel resentment in local communities. In one of local interaction with by the project,
it was highlighted by the local representative that the hiring practices should be transparent.
Pre project interactions by the project with community had also highlighted that the local job creation is one of the
major expectations of the community members. Hence, any partiality or favours in the process needs to be minimised.

 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 297
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan




2.4.15               Managing the expectation of local administration and community
Initial interaction with the administration and community has revealed that they want the labor camp to be located in
their area (sensing the economic opportunity associated with it). If expectations are not managed properly, it can lead
to potential non-cooperation / conflict.

2.4.16               Risk of Outward Migration and decrease in economic activities once
                     Project becomes operational
One more risk which can lead to public resentment and community discomfort by the time project gets operational is
the sudden decrease in requirement of workforce in the project. As seen in other such projects, long completion period
of such projects means that a large portion of migrant workforce settles in the nearby settlements (refer the
consultation documents). Additionally, as a major proportion of local population starts earning their livelihood from
economic activities related to the project, developing skills specific to the project requirements. Once the project is
operational, that requirement suddenly vanishes.
Lack of robust economic activities in the vicinity means that people in the area are left with neither the skillsets to adjust
to broader economic requirement nor an opportunity to switch their work profile. This threat is further substantiated
by the fact that as the economic activities related to K-2 HPP project ended, villages had to relocate to new places to
find new economic activities. Project safeguard documents should ensure that the project has a pragmatic and workable
‘Exit Plan’ to tackle the socio-economic hindrance caused by completion of project construction phase.


2.5                  Adverse Environmental Impacts
Given the anticipated workforce of approximately 3000 during the construction period, the environmental impacts
listed below are likely to be of significant concern.

2.5.1                Inadequate waste disposal and illegal waste disposal sites
The significant influx of a large workforce can generate substantial amounts of waste, potentially exceeding the capacity
of existing local waste management systems. This can lead to improper waste disposal practices, such as littering, illegal
dumping, and open burning, with potentially severe environmental and health consequences.

2.5.2                Wastewater discharges
Project-related activities sites, and activities undertaken at worker camps, in conjunction with inadequate wastewater
treatment and disposal systems, can result in the pollution of nearby water resources. This may pose a significant health
risk to the host community, particularly if latrine pits overflow into local streams used for drinking water.

2.5.3                Increase demand on potable water resources
The provision of clean drinking water and water for hygiene purposes can result in increased pressure on freshwater
resources in the project or camp site area.

2.5.4                Increased use of/ demand for natural resources
This can include logging for construction, fuel-wood collection, use of water resources, farming and grazing, hunting and
fishing, trade in endangered species, potential introduction of invasive or non-native species, and land degradation.


2.6                  Positive Labor influx
Labour influx can also have several beneficial impacts on the local community and economy including:
•        Job Creation
Construction and operation of a hydroelectric plant create numerous direct and indirect employment opportunities.
These jobs can range from engineers, project managers, and technicians to labourers, truck drivers, and security


    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
    Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 298
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan



personnel. Local businesses may also see an increase in demand for services such as food, transportation, and
accommodation during the construction phase.
•        Skills Development and Training
Workers involved in the hydro project may acquire new technical skills, particularly in construction, engineering, and
plant operations. These skills can help improve the local workforce's capabilities and may lead to long-term employment
opportunities in other sectors. Training programs and workshops for local workers can contribute to their professional
growth and enhance their employability in future projects or industries.
•        Economic Growth:
The influx of labor can boost local spending, especially for small businesses and service providers.
This can have a ripple effect on the local economy, promoting growth in order sectors like retail, hospitality, and
transportation.
Increased wages and salaries paid to local workers can elevate their standard of living and contribute to improved quality
of life in the community.
•        Increased Tax Revenues:
The employment generated by the project leads to higher income tax revenues for local governments, which can be
reinvested into community infrastructure, healthcare, education, and social services.
•        Improvement in Local Infrastructure:
The construction of a hydroelectric plant often requires enhancements to local infrastructure, such as roads, bridges,
and utilities, to accommodate workers and support the project. This infrastructure can have lasting benefits for the
community once the project is complete.




                                                           Figure 18 Risk Profile of Labor Influx

As detailed in Figure 18, risk profile of a project can be identified considering two factors i.e. absorption capacity of the
community and ratio of worker & community. For K1 HPP there is a history of similar project in near vicinity and no case
of community – project conflict, so it can be concluded that absorption capacity is high. However, this current positive
attitude can quickly turn negative if the community concerns are not addressed (e.g. loss of pasture land, winter pasture
access, horse roads, and unfair labor selection etc). These are major issues and directly impacting their livelihood.
Accordingly, absorption capacity of community is considered subjective. This is further reinforced by the fact that in
primary interaction by project, political executives have shown strong support for the project. On contrary, ratio of work
/ community is also very high as the area is scarcely populated and number of workers are very high. Hence, the project

can be categorized as ‘Medium’ to ‘High’ impact. Thus, the labor risk profile of the Project is rated as Significant. This is
further supported by the K1 HPP ESIA which has recommended a LIMP as key mitigation strategy.

2.6.1             Role of HR Development Contractor in Promoting Local Employment
In order to address the risk rating profile of the K1 HPP a number of key measure are set out in Table 78 to ensure local
employment is given the highest priority and there are s pecific actions to promote local hiring, with a focus on women
and vulnerable groups. The engagement of a National Human Resource Development Contractor (NHRDC) will be critical
in Phase 1 and first two years of Phase 2 of the project. The activities of the NHRDC will support all affected communities
over four (4) years (two (2) years during pre-construction phase and two (2) years during Phase 2 - Construction).

The primary role the NHRDC will be to take responsibility for developing and delivering training programs to local
communities. The training programs will aim to improve the capacity of local communities by preparing them for
employment and contracting opportunities created by the K1 HPP. In addition to pre-employment training, the NHRDC
will also develop other customized programs, especially for women and vulnerable groups to address specific skills gaps.
The NHRDC will play a key role in facilitating the survey and registration of eligible locals from the project affected
communities. The focus of the NHRDC activities will be in:

     •     Training and Capacity Building: Develop and deliver training programs to enhance the skills and knowledge of
           community members, making them more competitive for project-related jobs.
     •     Pre-Employment Training: Facilitate pre-employment training, preparing individuals for the specific skills
     •     and requirements of the project.
     •     Community Engagement: Work closely with project partners and local training providers to ensure the
           effectiveness and relevance of the training programs.
     •     Data Collection: Responsible for surveying and registering eligible locals from the project catchment area,
           creating a database of potential candidates.

The NHRDC, in collaboration with the PMO and Contractor, will ensure that skills training and capacity building inputs
provided to the manpower supply match the demand for workers, contractors and service providers in the area, using
the following steps:
     •     Create a roster of all eligible individuals in the project area.
     •     Create a database of in-demand local hire jobs, skills and service contracts.
     •     Develop an overall plan for matching supply with demand. The NHRDC will continue supporting individuals
           even after they have completed skills training programs.
As individuals graduate from the courses, the NHRDC shall establish a database of the pool of training graduates and
use this as basis for providing post-training assistance, including:
     •     referrals, job matching and placement;
     •     technical assistance in preparing resumes and proposals; and
     •     provision of follow-up training and technical support.
To support the development and improvement of the skills and capacities of local manpower resources to qualify for
and be effectively engaged in employment contracts associated with the K1 HPP, there will be two types of training
facilitated:
    (i)      Pre-employment training (PET)
The pre-employment training will work with the resources developed under existing programs in Kyrgyzstan to provide
out-of-school youth and unemployed individuals with basic knowledge, attitudes and skills before their entry/re- entry
into the labour force. The PET objective, “to introduce concepts and to orient the trainees to the world of work and self-
reliance through life skills development and life-long learning”, will be adapted to prepare employable individuals for
gainful employment with the construction and related activities of K1 HPP.
    (ii)      Technical and Vocational Training (TVT)




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 300
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan



This will provide skills training based on skill sets in-demand. Training will be provided to eligible individuals in the
technical and vocational courses identified in the database of in-demand local hire jobs, skills and service contracts and
targeted in the overall plan for the delivery of this component

The NHRDC shall closely work with the PMO and other stakeholders including other local potential employers to improve
the chances of the graduates to secure employment and/or contracts.



3.                Labor force Influx Impacts and Mitigation and
                  Enhancement Measures
3.1               Measures to Address Labor Force Influx Impacts
The following Action Plan, as summarised in Table 78 has been developed to target key aspects of the Project
implementation, that are aimed at ensuring that negative impacts of labour influx are minimised as much as possible
from the outset, and positive impacts are enhanced. It will be reviewed and monitored regularly, and a critical aspect
of this plan is its linkages with other key plans, namely:
     •     Stakeholder Engagement Plan
     •     Labour Management Plan
     •     Community Health, Safety & Security Plan
     •     Traffic Management Plan

3.2               Specific Measures to Address SEA & GBV
  Both SEA and GBV are deeply interconnected and share similar root causes, including gender inequality, power
  imbalances, and socio-cultural norms that normalize violence. Efforts to address these issues should be coordinated,
  holistic, and context-specific, ensuring that they are sensitive to the needs of the most marginalized and vulnerable
  individuals, particularly women and girls.
  In planning measures to address them priority must be on “Prevention” as it is critical in both SEA and GBV, requiring
  changes in societal attitudes, education, and gender norms. Other measures like continuous “Community
  Engagement” is key, with initiatives aimed at changing norms and empowering individuals to prevent violence. All
  possible “Support to Survivor” including access to legal, psychological, and medical services also plays an important
  role and last but not the least “Accountability” mechanisms must be robust to deter SEA and GBV and ensure
  perpetrators are held accountable.
  Therefore, SEA/SH risks need to be assessed throughout the project’s life by monitoring the situation, assessing the
  effectiveness of risk mitigation measures, and adapting them accordingly. When continuous monitoring efforts
  detect changes to the identified SEA/SH risks and/or actual incidences of SEA/SH, projects need to adapt the SEA/SH
  risk level and mitigation strategy. The following diagram describes the process.




                                  Figure 19 SEA/SH Risk Management Cycle Throughout Project Life




 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 314
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan




3.3               Major Stakeholders
More detailed stakeholder identification, mapping, engagement plan and frequency are included in the SEP.

Key stakeholders stakeholder is listed herein:
    • Government stakeholders;
    • Directly affected communities;
    • Pasture committee and their families;
    • Local institutes including vocational training institutes and focal points initiatives by multilateral agencies
        (Kyrgyz-German Technical Institute (KGTI) at KSTU, University of Technology Kyrgyzstan Campus (UTKC), DVV
        International in Kyrgyzstan)
    • Youth groups and educational institutes;
    • Traditional leadership and cooperative institutes, if any;
    • Local NGOs, CBOs etc.
    • Agencies involved in building roads and other infrastructures.

The PMO will manage:
•         ongoing monitoring of the implementation of LIMP by all categories of project workers during the construction
          phase. This will include inspections and periodic audits to ensure compliance with and effective implementation
          of the LIMP.
•         performance of contractors in relation to contracted workers, focusing on compliance by contractors with their
          contractual agreements and contractor LIMPs. This will include periodic audits, inspections, and/or spot checks
          of project locations as well as of labor management records and reports compiled by contractors.

4.2                  Reporting
PMO will carry out regular reporting on the performance onsite, in addition to reporting of any accidents, incidents
and/or emergencies and the measures undertaken in such cases to control the situation and prevent it from occurring
again.
Reporting of labor and working conditions/occupational health and safety (LWC/OHS) issues and outcomes will be
provided inter alia in a dedicated section of the periodic and annual project reports. Reporting will include the following
parameters:
•        Awareness raising materials produced and trainings undertaken;
•        Outcomes of recruitment of direct project workers by age group, origin, gender and ethnicity;
•        Records of the project direct worker grievance mechanism;
•        Recording of LWC/OHS incidents. These are to be systematically recorded and reported through the responsible
         IA or in a standard format including (1) type of incident; (2) severity class of incident (major, moderate, minor);
         incident classification (e.g. fatality, illness, medical treatment, etc.), and notification timeframe (e.g. Immediately,
         within 72 hours, etc.).
•        Cases of serious breaches of contractors’ obligations, and remedial actions taken.
Apart from above, the same mechanism shall be utilized to address any issues related to community – worker conflict.
CLO, as proposed in above chapter shall ensure that differences are not escalated to serious conflicts.
The effectiveness of implementation of the LIMP will be evaluated by an independent evaluator as part of the annual
Project audit cycle described in the ESMP and ESCP. This evaluation will include confidential interviews with project
workers in all categories to determine their awareness of their workplace rights and their experience of working
conditions.
During the Project implementation period semi-annual reports on ESS2 implementation will be prepared and submitted
to the World Bank. These reports will include LMP& LIMP sections that will provide updates on direct workers, contract
workers and GM.


4.3                  Record Keeping
Record keeping will be an integral part of the LWC/OHS monitoring process, in keeping with the provisions of Kyrgyz
labor legislation, ESS2 and the requirements of the World Bank's General EHS Guidelines.


4.4                  Capacity Building
The Project will develop and deliver training sessions to project staff and prepare simple awareness raising materials to
implement the LMP. Capacity building and awareness activities are covered under the proposed budget in the umbrella
instrument, ESMF/ESMP.
PMO will develop and deliver a short training courses for contractors and/ or contractors site managers, explaining the
obligations of the contractor as set out in the ESMP and LMP. Attendance at this course is to be a pre-condition for
bidding for EPC works contracts. Supervising engineers are also to attend these courses.
As of now, the PMO staff selected for Environmental and Social (E&S) safeguards have limited experience with the
implementation of provisions under the World Bank’s ESF. However, some officials from JSC EPP and NEGK possess
limited experience in implementing the Bank's policies during the execution of projects such as CASA-1000, Kambarata-
2, and Toktogul HPP (ADB-supported).
Given the scale of the proposed Project and the associated environmental and social risks, it is essential that the staff
responsible for implementation are well-equipped to meet the required environmental and social standards set by the
World Bank, regulators, and other funding bodies. Therefore, it is crucial to conduct a Capacity Building Assessment for
the E&S staff to evaluate their skills, knowledge, and abilities to effectively manage E&S risks and implement relevant
management systems and policies in line with the World Bank’s ESS.



    Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
    Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
    Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 317
P12: Labor Influx Management Plan



A comprehensive training and skill enhancement program is necessary for the Implementing Agency (IA) staff,
particularly the E&S team, to ensure effective safeguard implementation and compliance with the World Bank’s ESS
requirements. Given the high E&S risks associated with the project, the Needs Assessment is proposed as the first step
in the capacity-building process, the detailed capacity building steps are also included in the ESIA ToR and will be
followed during E&S assessment studies and outcome shall be implemented prior to start of construction activities.



5.                GRIEVANCE REDRESSAL MECHANISM
The project shall use GRM proposed in LMP for the purpose of this LIMP including that of Sensitive Grievances.

6.3               Mitigation Measures Part of Binding Contract
                  Condition
Past experiences clearly establish that Labor and safety issues must be made contractually binding in the bidding
documents and provision of contracting company’s CEO undertaking to abide all such provisions also plays an important
tool in reducing negative incidences. Moreover, the Bank has already made mandatory that Standard Procurements
Documents (SPDs) must include ESF requirements. To achieve these objective relevant provisions / requirements must
be included in procurement documents and may be linked to remedies that include removal of contractor’s personnel,

withholding payments, cashing out performance securities, and termination so that implementation of these
measures/plans become a contractually binding requirement.


6.4               Budget Allocation
The budget for implementing and monitoring of the LIMP will be financed under the Project Component 3 -
Implementation of ESMP.
A substantial portion of this budget will be allocated to personnel costs, including the hiring of a qualified Social
Development Specialist and Social Development Officers. These personnel will be responsible for coordinating activities,
liaising with contractors and service providers, and overseeing the monitoring and reporting of the LIMP measures. It
should be noted that the cost of employing Social Specialists within contractor teams will be borne by the contractors
themselves, as part of their contractual obligations under project implementation. Similarly, LIMP-realted mitigation
measures at construction sites will be embedded within the construction costs and covered by the contractors.
Measures related to labour influx, including the development and maintenance of worker camps, provision of amenities
and physical infrastructure, and camp operations, will be integrated into the project construction costs. These measures
also overlap with other sub-plans of the ESMP, such as the SEP, GRM, and others. Therefore, no additional budget under
the LIMP is anticipated for these overlapping activities.
One of the major cost items under the LIMP will be related to the design and delivery of training programs, capacity
building, and the management of employment systems, compliance processes, and recruitment efforts. An initial
budget for the implementation of the LIMP during 8.5-year construction is provided in the table below. These figures
are indicative only and exclude personnel costs to provide an estimate of the additional budget needed for LIMP
implementation.

Appendix A                         Project Employment Hierarchy
Policy
This policy establishes a clear, fair, and inclusive framework for recruiting and deploying labor at the Kambarata-1
Hydropower Project (K-1 HPP). It aligns with the World Bank’s ESS2 and the Labor Code of the Kyrgyz Republic, ensuring
that employment opportunities support local development while meeting international labor standards.
The policy applies to all categories of workers engaged throughout the project lifecycle, including:
    •      Direct workers
    •      Contracted workers
    •      Primary supply workers
    •      Community workers (if applicable)
The policy is founded on six core principles:
    •      Local First: Maximize employment opportunities for affected and local communities.
    •      Fairness & Non-discrimination: Equal access regardless of gender, ethnicity, language, disability, or age.
    •      Skills & Capacity: Match roles with capabilities; support training where needed.
    •      Gender Inclusion: Proactively recruit and retain women in all occupational levels.
    •      Transparency: Publicly disclose employment procedures and job opportunities.
    •      Compliance: Uphold international labor standards; no child or forced labor.
To ensure local benefit and effective skills deployment, hiring follows this hierarchy:
    •      Tier 1 – Project Affected People (PAPs): Individuals displaced or economically impacted by the project are
           given first priority for unskilled and semi-skilled roles, with access to targeted job-readiness training. At least
           10% of total unskilled and semi-skilled positions should be filled by candidates from this tier.
    •      Tier 2 – Local Communities and Districts: Residents within the Toktogul District and the broader Jalal-Abad
           Region—though not directly affected—are prioritized for semi-skilled and skilled roles. This tier should
           account for at least 60% of unskilled/semi-skilled hires and a minimum of 10% of skilled hires.
    •      Tier 3 – National and International Labor: Qualified national professionals are prioritized for technical, skilled,
           and managerial positions. International workers may be recruited only when specific expertise is unavailable
           locally and must contribute to knowledge transfer and mentoring. The target is for at least 70% of skilled
           professionals to be hired nationally. For managerial and technical leadership roles, a minimum of 80% should
           be national hires, with no more than 20% international.
    •      In addition, the Project will ensure employment for women and other possible vulnerable groups by
           establishing a quota, not less than 15% of total workforce at the stage of construction and 25% at the
           operation phases of hydroelectric power plants. A separate threshold of at least 3% participation by persons
           with disabilities will be established to ensure inclusive access and representation, and will be monitored
           independently from other vulnerability or gender-related targets.
These targets are indicative and will be periodically reviewed and adjusted based on labor market conditions, workforce
performance, and project phase.
The project will invest in training programs to enhance the employability of PAPs and local residents. Pre-employment
orientation will be offered to introduce basic health and safety practices, worksite behavior expectations, and vocational
skills.



 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                      Page | 325
P12.1: LABOUR MANAGEMENT PLAN



The project developer holds overall accountability, with contractors and subcontractors required to implement the
policy and report labor data regularly. Community Liaison Officers (CLOs) will facilitate communication with local
stakeholders, while local authorities and independent monitors support labor mobilization and compliance checks.
The Policy shall be communicated to all contractors, with compliance monitored throughout implementation.
The policy will be reviewed annually or as needed in response to legislative or project changes. Input from workers,
communities, and partners will guide updates to maintain relevance and effectiveness.

 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                          11 August 2025                 Page | 329
LABOR MANAGEMENT PLAN




1.                INTRODUCTION
1.1               Project Background
Kyrgyz Republic has significant hydropower potential due to its mountainous terrain and abundant water resources,
particularly from the Naryn River and its tributaries. The country is estimated to have a hydropower potential of 142
billion kWh annually (ITA, 202422), positioning it to become a major energy producer in the Central Asia. However,
currently, only about 10% of this potential has been developed, resulting in an installed capacity of around 4,018 MW23.
The proposed Kambarata-1 HPP (K-1 HPP) will be the largest hydropower project in the Kyrgyz Republic and broader
Central Asia region, with a capacity of ~1,880 MW and potential energy generation of ~5,961 GWh, which is ~50 % of
the current installed capacity in the country. K-1 HPP, once operational, is envisaged to improve the energy and food
security of the Kyrgyz Republic while enhancing the country’s energy export revenue through the distribution of surplus
power to other Central Asian countries, including Uzbekistan, Tajikistan and Pakistan. The large reservoir of K-1 HPP on
the Naryn River is likely to improve the operation of the Toktogul HPP reservoir, which can provide a much-needed
balance to the water management regime in the Naryn–Syr Darya Basin. The proposed project will also emerge as crucial
step in fulfilling the government’s vision for a green and affordable energy future.
The World Bank has agreed to finance project in accordance and in full compliance with the Bank’s Environmental and
Social Framework (ESF) comprising, inter alia, the Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs). MoE/PMO, has hired AFRY
and SMEC to update the Feasibility Study and undertake detailed Environment & Social Impact assessment and
managements plans respectively. This Labour Management Plan (LMP) for Kambarata-1 HPP is being prepared as part
of Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP), 2024 and is aligned with the requirements of WB ESF-ESS 2:
Labour and Working Conditions.


1.2               Project Description
The K-1 HPP area is in the middle reaches of the Naryn River, 14.5 km upstream of the existing Kambarata-2 HPP dam
in the Toktogul District of Jalalabad Region. It is located in a gorge where the River makes a large loop around the
Karabash Mountain first southward for more than 1 km, and then east-westward for more than 1.5 km, prior to tracking
in a north–- towards the mouth of the Termirkent Sai, a right-bank tributary. In this section of the Naryn River, the
average water level is at an elevation of 950-955 m asl. The Karabash Gorge is a V-shaped valley intersecting granite
rocks with steep and in some areas with vertical slopes, rising at a level of more than 300 m above the riverbed. The
dam site is located at approximately at coordinates 41°46’14”Nand 73°26’30”E.
The reservoir area has been estimated to be about 61.7 km 2 with the length of the reservoir of about 80 km. Figure 20
shows the K-1 HPP dam site location and the reservoir. The site layout of the K-1 HPP is presented in Figure 21.




22 International Trade Administration, 2024: Kyrgyz Republic - Energy
23 2024 World Hydropower Outlook, International Hydropower Association

 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                 Page | 332
LABOR MANAGEMENT PLAN




The proposed Project consists of the following main elements:
    •      Curved Roller-compacted concrete (RCC) gravity dam of 10 m wide at the crest having maximum height above
           the riverbed of 260.8 m.
    •      A reservoir area of 61.7 km2 at a full supply level (FSL) of 1,198 msl. The inundated area extends approximately
           68 km upstream from the Dam wall.
    •      An overground powerhouse including Francis-type turbines with a total electricity generating capacity of 1,880
           MW.
    •      The permanent resettlement of persons who are either temporarily and permanently residing there and
           pastures, structures, assets and sources of livelihood within the proposed inundation area. Also, restoration of
           access and social infrastructure affected.
    •      The construction of temporary and permanent access roads and bridge structures within the Dam site.
    •      The construction of water pipelines and tunnels.
    •      Quarry sites to source raw materials for the Dam.
    •      Temporary concrete batching plant, materials storage, waste disposal, construction sites and workforce
           accommodation facilities.
    •      Permanent and temporary accommodation facilities for Dam and hydropower operations and maintenance
           personnel.
    •      The peak construction labour force is estimated at about 7,000 with an average of about 4,622 workers per
           year will be needed over the 8.5-year construction period.
    •      The associated facilities (i.e. a 5.6 km access road for the K-1 site to the existing road network; a bridge over
           the Naryn River; temporary work camp; a 23.6 km long 110 kV overhead transmission line; and a 110/6 kV
           substation). According to the World Bank’s definition, the associated facilities must meet three criteria. That
           is, these facilities must be- (a) directly and significantly related to the Project; and (b) carried out, or planned
           to be carried out, contemporaneously with the Project; and (c) necessary for the Project to be viable and would
           not have been constructed, expanded or conducted if the Project did not exist.


1.3               Previous Studies
Initial Feasibility Study of Kambarata 1 and 2 HPPs, was undertaken in 1988 by Sredazgidroproekt Institute. In 2014, a
detailed Feasibility Study for Kambarata-1 Hydro Power Plant on Naryn River was carried out by SNC Lavalin of Canada
and ENEX of Russia. The study also examined various dam heights, construction types, and potential installed capacities
of up to 1,900 MW. The Kyrgyz energy institutions preferred the higher dam option, as it, despite the greater energy
costs, would enable water storage during the summer months for electricity generation in the winter when demand is
highest. Based on the feasibility study an ESIA, Socio-Economic studies were also conducted in 2014.
However, as part of Master Plan for Complex Development of the Energy Sector of the Kyrgyz Republic, CESI & CAIC,
2022 (including Rapid Assessment of Kambarata 1 HPP), AFRY has been engaged in 2024 to update the 2014 Feasibility
Study by SNC and ENEX, taking into account, the data from these and other existing sources, along with the necessary
engineering surveys and studies to update the Feasibility Study.


1.4               World Bank’s Technical Assistance
On 31 October 2023, The World Bank’s Board of Executive Directors approved $2 million in grant financing from the
International Development Association (IDA) for the Technical Assistance for Financing Framework for K-1 HPP. This TA
aims to support the Kyrgyz Republic/Ministry of Energy in improving the financial and commercial frameworks of the
Kambarata-1 Hydropower Plant (HPP) Project and enhancing its technical, environmental and social sustainability.
Activities covered under this financing include updating the feasibility study, conducting environmental and social
studies, and drafting procurement documents and implementation agreements. This support will include: (i) updating
the earlier studies to verify engineering and economic feasibility, and preparing baseline design and tender documents,
(ii) developing and updating environmental impact assessment study and land acquisition and resettlement plans, and

 Draft of Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report. Volume IV. Book 4.1   Client Reference No. 5018007
 Technical Assistance for Kambarata 1 Hydropower Plant Project                     SMEC Internal Ref. 5018007
 Prepared for Ministry of Energy, Kyrgyz Republic                                  11 August 2025                     Page | 333
LABOR MANAGEMENT PLAN



(iii) improving the quality of procurement documents and implementation agreements, including shareholder
agreement, implementation agreement, electricity purchase agreement, etc.
This TA component will also improve the readiness of the K-1 HPP to increase the financing required for its completion.
It would help to update the financing plan to ensure that annual spending on the project does not create macro-fiscal
risks for the country and that the HPP has a reliable commercial framework for sale of electricity to domestic consumers
and for exports.


1.5               Objectives of Labour Management Plan
As part of the financing agreement, the Project is required to comply with the World Bank’s Environmental and Social
Framework (ESF) comprising, the Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs).
This Labour Management Plan (LMP) document has been developed to:
     •     Meet the objectives of World Bank ESS 2: Labour and Working Conditions (ESS2).
     •     Set out the terms and conditions for employment or engagement of workers on the Project,
     •     Specifies the requirements and standards to be met and the policies and procedures to be followed,
     •     Assesses risks and proposes the implementation of compliance measures and promotes fair treatment, non-
           discrimination and equal opportunity of Project workers.
The LMP is developed to help avoid, mitigate and manage risks and impacts in relation to Project workers and sets out
the way in which Project workers will be managed, in accordance with the requirements of national law and the ESS2.
The plan address the way in which both standards will apply to different categories of Project workers, including direct
workers, and the way in which third parties will manage their workers to comply with applicable requirements.
The LMP applies to Project workers as defined by ESS224. One of the areas of focus of this document is on direct workers
recruited by Project Management Office (PMO) under the Ministry of Energy (MoE) as well as contracted workers who
are employed by a contractor or other third party. There will essentially not be any community workers (see Section
3.2) because members of the local community are hired to be part of the contractors’ labour force. In addition, the LMP
will also apply to workers employed by primary suppliers of the construction program, with requirements focused on
to identify, assess and manage potential risks of child labour, forced labour and serious safety issues which may arise in
relation to primary suppliers, and outlines the roles and responsibilities of the PMO/MoE to monitor primary suppliers
and cascade requirements to those suppliers.
Kyrgyz Republic is signatory to most of the Conventions of International Labour Organization (ILO) and other United
Nations (UN) organizations related to labour and working conditions informing the ESS2. 25 Partly as a result, the legal
framework of Kyrgyz Republic regarding Labour and Working Conditions is, with a few exceptions, relatively compliant
with ESS2. The main gaps of Kyrgyz Republic labour legislation with respect to the World Bank’s ESS2 includes
requirements for a workplace grievance mechanism, explicit prohibition of sexual harassment in the workplace and
employment, provision of a grievance mechanism for sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA) and sexual harassment (SH),
prohibition of employing women in hazardous jobs and specific requirements for separate facilities for men and women.
All these gaps will be addressed by measures stipulated in this LMP and other management plans that have been
developed for the Project (e.g., Gender, Vulnerability, and Inclusion Action Plan, Occupational Health and Safety Plan
and Community Health, Safety, and Security Plan).
The LMP is a live document that will be reviewed periodically, no less than annually, throughout construction of K-1
HPP. It will be updated as necessary to reflect new or changed activities and conditions. All revisions will be subject to
approval by the World Bank.


24The term “Project worker” refers to: (a) people employed or engaged directly by the Borrower (including the Project proponent and the Project

implementing agencies) to work specifically in relation to the Project (direct workers); people employed or engaged through third parties to perform
work related to core functions of the Project, regardless of location (contracted workers); (c) people employed or engaged by the Borrower’s primary
suppliers (primary supply workers); and (d) people employed or engaged in providing community labor (community workers). ESS2 applies to Project
workers including fulltime, part-time, temporary, seasonal and migrant workers.

2.                PROJECT LABOUR USE
2.1               Introduction
As noted above, this LMP applies to project workers as defined by ESS2 who are employed directly by the project at
project offices and on the construction sites and employees of contractors and service providers, including part time,
temporary, seasonal or migrant workers. Employees of direct suppliers to the project are considered as primary supply
workers and are covered by provisions of the LMP relating to prevention of child labour, forced labour and safety issues.
It may be noted that project construction aspects and related workforce details will only be available at the later stage,
this LMP, in accordance with ESS2, provides estimates only of the anticipated number, types and characteristics of the
project worker force27.
Similarly, the anticipated contracting structure for the project and the likely number of project workers to be employed
or engaged by each contractor/subcontractor is not known at this time. Once the relevant information is available, the
LMP will be updated accordingly. In accordance with ESS2 (para 8), the provisions of ESS2 do not apply to civil servants
except with regard to the provisions on child labour, forced labour and OHS. The chapter, based on previous experience
and informal data collected from multiple sources, has presented a rough estimate of labour influx during different
phases of project development.


2.2               Types and Numbers of Workers
The Kambarata-1 HPP is a large-scale infrastructure project in Kyrgyzstan, expected to generate 1,880 MW of power.
This shall result in a peak work force of over 7,000. The project proponent will engage different categories of workers
during the project cycle

2.2.1             Category of Workers
     •     In accordance with ESS2, all labour resources are divided into the following categories: Direct workers
           (PMO staff)
Direct Workers (PMO staff): Project direct workers are workers employed or engaged directly by the Project to perform
Project-related works, including civil servants and Project consultants who will operate in accordance with the national
legislation and under agreed contracts with MoE. To hire staff for this Project, MoE will follow the established practices.
PMO staff (consultants) are not civil servants and will be hired on a contract basis. The PMO will use two types of
employment contracts: long-term employment contract and short-term service contract.
The PMO will comprise the Director and consultants (coordinators) for the three Components, including finance
specialists, procurement specialists, social specialist, environment specialists, monitoring and evaluation specialist and
other technical specialists who will coordinate project implementation and carry out the following functions:
management of financial operations and procurements, and social and environmental risks; control, monitoring and
evaluation of project work plans execution; facilitation of cooperation between stakeholders at the central and local
levels; provision of project progress reports and ensuring that external audits of the project's financial management and
procurement procedures are conducted in a timely manner; ensuring monitoring and implementation of the World
Bank’s environmental and social standards. The PMO will also be responsible for preparation and communication of
information to all Project stakeholders.
To ensure successful implementation/coordination of the project, the project may engage additional short-term
consultants to upskill PMO, MoE, Electric Stations OJSC, and other organizations, as well as local authorities. The Project
may also engage short- term national consultants to conduct training workshops, organize meetings and round tables,
arrange annual public consultations and quarterly focus groups with the beneficiaries on key activities related to the
Project.



27
  The project will not engage community labor or security forces. However, while employing security personnel, the Borrower will (i) make reasonable
inquiries to verify that the security personnel employed by the Borrower to provide security are not implicated in past abuses; (ii) train them
adequately (or determine that they are properly trained) in the use of force (and where applicable, firearms), and appropriate conduct toward workers
and affected communities; and (iii) require them to act within the applicable law and any requirements set out in the ESCP.

The Project is expected to engage technical supervision specialists from both local community and other regions of the
country (for example, from the area where the design organization is originally based). The Project may send its
representatives to some Project-affected areas to provide support and oversee work implementation, as well as
coordinate with contractors, local authorities, beneficiaries, and communities.
The Project will also engage consultants (specialists who have expertise in technical supervision) to exercise technical
supervision and control over contractors’ (subcontractors’ and suppliers’) compliance with technological process and
quality requirements, and make sure they complete works and services and comply with the contract terms (designs,
terms of reference).
The Project may also engage national short-term consultants to carry out institutional capacity building in the energy
sector, hold training seminars, organize meetings on regional cooperation, monitor and evaluate work plans and Project
reports.
    •      Contracted workers
Contracted workers are workers hired or engaged by a third party to perform works related to the main functions of
the Project, irrespective of the location where such works are implemented. EPC contractors and subcontractors shall
be identified during implementation of the Project. To implement Project activities, the Project will sign agreements
with contractors (whose exact number will be identified only at the tender stage) in accordance with the World Bank
Procurement Guidelines of the Project. The number of contractors’ workers will be identified after commencement of
construction/rehabilitation works. Contractors will be selected through a competitive process guided by the World Bank
procurement principles and the legislation of the Kyrgyz Republic, and will engage subcontractors, who must be agreed
with MoE represented by the PMO.
Subcontractors: To implement its activities in rural areas, the Project Contractors can engage subcontractors, including
local private firms and non-governmental organizations. Contractors may also engage subcontractors for project
construction and associated facilities. Subcontractors are expected to come from among local companies.
Subcontractors will be guided by the national legislation, regulations, and the Labour Code of the Kyrgyz Republic and
the provisions of this LMP.
The Labour Code of the Kyrgyz Republic dated as January 23, 2025) has a section on “Labour Protection”. The Labour
Code of the Kyrgyz Republic obliges employers to ensure labour protection and establishes national regulatory
requirements for labour protection and employees’ obligations in the field of labour protection. It guarantees
employees’ right to labour protection, training and instruction, sanitary and medical care. It also covers creation and
operation of labour protection services, investigation and registration of work-related accidents and occupational
diseases, establishment of benefits and compensations for certain working conditions.
Article 281 of the Labour Code of the Kyrgyz Republic guarantees a healthy and safe working environment. Employers
are obliged to create healthy and safe working conditions for employees, introduce tools and technologies that ensure
compliance with sanitary and hygienic norms and labour protection standards.
Article 282. The Labour Code of the Kyrgyz Republic establishes labour protection requirements for construction and
operation of industrial buildings, structures, and equipment. Industrial buildings, structures, equipment, and
technological processes must be organized, as to meet the requirements associated with work health and safety. These
requirements include rational use of the territory and industrial premises, proper use of equipment and organization of
technological processes, protection of workers from exposure to harmful working conditions, maintenance of industrial
premises and workplaces in accordance with sanitary and hygienic standards and rules, and arrangement of sanitary
and amenity premises. Industrial buildings and structures must be so designed, constructed, and operated, as to ensure
compliance with labour protection rules and regulations. Machines, tools, and other production equipment must be so
designed, as to comply with safety requirements and industrial hygiene.
    •      Primary supply workers
A primary supply worker is a worker employed or engaged by a primary supplier, providing goods and materials to the
project, over whom a primary supplier exercises control for the work, working conditions, and treatment of the person.
Primary supply workers will be expected consisting of employees of third-party companies who will provide machinery
and/or equipment for construction, infrastructure investment component of the project and employees of companies
who will provide hardware, security, and cleaning services.
    •      Community workers
